### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-08T01:35:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-157806>

@[ssj3](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9728/) and @[NeilBB](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6879/)

Time to post of the half-baked thoughts regarding protein intake I guess  . Too lazy to source all my references right now, so you'll have to look for them yourselves if you want to verify my claims

\----
**Personal Bias**

I will state up front that I am not a fan of building extra muscle. I'm fine with how much muscle I'm carrying right now (me at 6'2'' and 175lbs in red underwear  -- [http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/2015-01-25/front.jpg](http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/2015-01-25/front.jpg)), after having done basically no resistance training for the last 7 months, and just biking around the place in tropical Cairns while focusing on strength and stability rather than moving heavy things around.

I tend to agree with Neil that building extra muscle is energetically very costly, and that strength in natural movements can be attained without extra muscular hypertrophy.

I tend to endorse periods of cyclic protein consumption -- high protein days and low protein days. High meaning "ad libitum", and low meaning 100g or less. (this will not be elaborated in this post, because it's already too long as is )

\----

**Protein "Requirements"
**
A lot of the studies in this respect are context specific, and tend to rely on the concept of Nitrogen Balance. Many of the studies that Bill Lagakos cites in his Protein Requirements blog rely on this concept -- [http://caloriesproper.com/protein-requirements-carbs-and-nutrient-partitioning/](http://caloriesproper.com/protein-requirements-carbs-and-nutrient-partitioning/)

There are flaws with using Nitrogen Balance to measure protein needs, but mainly because measurement of Nitrogen Balance has wide error margins, and not that it is an inaccurate way of measuring protein needs.

I think the general direction that Bill is hinting at is correct, that any exercise helps to maintain nitrogen balance. This refers to anything from resistance training to endurance activity.

Caveat: IMO the issue with net protein degradation with endurance activity is when energy requirements cannot be met. This is a separate issue. ie: if you can supply adequate energy to the muscle tissue through fatty acid oxidation, I suspect that protein retention will continue to be improved even with long bouts of endurance activity​

Kevin Tipton and Robert Wolfe did a review considering most practical aspects of this -- [http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/0264041031000140554?journalCode=rjsp20](http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/0264041031000140554?journalCode=rjsp20) . I also like Lyle McDonald's take on this subject, from his 'The Protein Book'.

Personally, I think:

You will find net nitrogen balance cap off maybe around 1.7g/kg a day of protein intake.

Net positive protein synthesis will likely increase until about an intake of 2.5g/kg

Any extra protein in the context of heavy resistance training athletes will likely be oxidized, or shunted to glucose. Athletes can probably deal with this excess protein, and extra glucose just continues to drive work capacity anyway. An intake of up to 3.0g/kg for athletes is not likely harmful from the perspective of maximizing muscular hypertrophy
​

There are many, many, many, many confounding factors . Just some examples:

**\- What if the athlete was fasted during training?**

**\- How much carbohydrate were they consuming**? This is especially a big factor with chronic high carbohydrate consumption

**\- How accustomed to the training stimulus are they?** Old-school bodybuilders would periodise different training mesocycles with differing levels of volume and intensity. Daily-undulating periodisation is used by everyone from bodybuilders (Layne Norton's power-hyper split), to powerlifters (Mike Tuscherer's Heavy-Med-Low days). "Confusing the Muscle" with different stimuli is definitely effective at forcing net protein accumulation within the muscles.

On the flip side, "getting used to an exercise stimulus" doesn't warrant extra tissue from being built, and we can see this reflected both in clinical studies of net nitrogen retention when a training protocol is sustained, and in anecdotal reports of bodybuilders "getting stale" on a particular protocol.

\----

**Muscle Type
**
Slow twitch fibers have lots of mitochondria in them. Fast twitch fibers don't.

Muscle-building approaches generally target hypertrophy of both fiber types, on top of getting. You will hear the quote that, "the strongest bodybuilders are the biggest". This is generally true, and "getting strong" is usually synonymous with simultaneous sarcoplasmic hypertrophy along with myofibrillar hypertrophy of all muscle fiber types.

Recently I came across Pavel Tsatouline's recommendations on Slow Twitch fiber hypertrophy -- [http://www.strongfirst.com/how-to-build-your-slow-fibers-part-i/](http://www.strongfirst.com/how-to-build-your-slow-fibers-part-i/) and [http://www.strongfirst.com/how-to-build-your-slow-fibers-part-ii/](http://www.strongfirst.com/how-to-build-your-slow-fibers-part-ii/)

I like a lot of his principles, but specific to the topic, it made me think if growing mitochondria-bearing slow twitch fibers isn't that bad of thing in terms of "robbing energy from the brain".

Yes, you will need to put extra energy into the muscles, but those fibers can deal with incoming substrate very well, increase full-body fatty acid utilisation, while enabling a lot of force generation capacity if need be. They can also help to both deal with and generate a free radical stimulus.

I do not search for hypertrophy per se, but if it comes, then I want it to be muscle tissue that actually contain fat-burning mitochondria.

\----

**Strength
**
Most people would use less than 50% of muscles fibers when performing the first rep of a set of bicep curls using a weight that is a 10 rep maximum (RM).

Fiber recruiting increases if you try to move the 10RM weight faster, but not everyone can activate their nervous system to actually move the weight fast. Teaching the nervous system to activate more strongly even with that 10RM weight is "getting stronger" during the first rep of that bicep curl.

Generalised strength in a coordinated system of movement is 1,000,000 times more complex. What about sticking points? How do muscle groups synergise with each other in a particular movement? What about intra-abdominal pressure (Pavel Kolar's DNS comes to mind)? What about fascia restrictions? etc ......

"Strength" needs to be qualified as the ability to move through a specific range of motion, using certain joints, under a specific set of metabolic conditions, perhaps with a load, etc .....

My opinion is that nervous system coordination of muscles with simultaneous maintenance of core stability is what is limiting people in terms of generalised strength -- the type that helps you climb trees, protect your back while carrying your child, running around on the beach, etc ....

\----

**mTOR and Protein Synthesis
**
No, mTOR doesn't drive all forms of protein synthesis. I like to view it as a systemic mechanism that regulates full-body growth.

I do not know how the feedback cycles work. Here's a doom-and-gloom scenario against trying to build big muscles: You build muscles in an energy deficient, get great protein synthesis in those muscles, and then they feedback to suppress systemic protein synthesis in the brain (due to lack of energy). Brain doesn't see adequate mTOR stimulus and doesn't get the appropriate autophagy. Again, completely hypothetical, but possible, and borne of all of unknown factors.

\----

**Eating Protein and/or Carbs after a Workout**

Bridging off the previous section, skeletal tissue probably requires both a high insulin and high circulating Amino Acid (AA) level for good protein synthesis.

I do not think that a lot of protein is required for "high levels of AAs", and even something like 5g of Essential Amino Acids (EAA) seem to mimic this condition in serum.

Carbohydrate requirements can be viewed from both the perspective of glycogen replenishment, and from the perspective of increasing the anabolic insulin stimulus.

Clearly, we need better muscular hypertrophy with a combination of carbs + protein after exercise. The dose depends on so many factors, though most athletes would probably benefit from 0.3 to 0.5g/kg of protein post workout. (again, emphasis on the word "athlete")

With reference to the Ubiquination 6 article, do you need to eat protein to gain the "benefits of exercise"? If the benefits you are looking for are muscle hypertrophy, then absolutely yes. If the benefits are improved collagen quality due to improved autophagy inside the cell, then I actually think that the answer is probably "No" (unless your cell is seriously energy depleted due to the exercise, which again, begs the question of why are you doing exercise beyond your hormetic threshold in the first place)

....

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-08T01:36:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-157807>

**Practical Measurement of Exercise Tolerance**

TBH, at the moment I see no way of measuring acute effects to determine if a particular exercise regime is working for you.

Short of actually performing an on-the-spot biopsy and examine levels of AMPk and other growth factors, fatty acid vs carb utilisation, etc ... I don't see a way at present to do this. In the future, NMR or MRI techniques may allow to scan a muscle non-invasively to measure stuff like substrate utilisation, but that's not practically today either. (Of course, do you even need to have this level of data?  I suspect not)

I am still wondering if Delayed Onset Muscle Soreness (DOMS) is an indicator of anything. It could potentially mean excessive H+ ion buildup in muscle tissue, and if so, then persistent DOMS is a good sign that muscle clearance of free radicals sucks, and that you should be cautious about your exercise protocol.

Manifestation of trigger points may be a good sign that things are going wrong. I will need to read more of Kolar's stuff before I make any conclusions.

Anything that affects your sleep negatively is obviously bad.

Still, I do not know what the best way to measure the effects of exercise is.

My intent right now is to:

\- maintain a consistent low-carb dietary approach (in this state, I will know when I am craving carbs, which indicates energetic inefficiency)
\- as low stress an environment as possible
\- and then perform personal experiments to see how exercise affects me personally

.....

> Night time exercise **WILL NOT WORK** if exercise cannot activate AMPk well. The way you tell this for sure is with a high evening cortisol. If you are feeling shitty and can't sleep after exercise, it's probably a good sign that you should back off the night time workout protocol.

### Paleodocteur — 2015-03-08T20:43:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-157861>

warning to new Paleos or ketone lovers

While walking the streets of the big city, with my frIPhone in the right pocket, I was happy to be able to listen to Science, BMJ, Nature’s podcasts. And it was fun to listen to Kresser’s and Jimmy-QuestBar’s. They were all reinforcing my enthusiastic belief that disease and midlife decay was reversible, if there was appropriate management of food. For the first time in my life I was able to run fast think fast and lift my body up and my mood up . I was throwing ketones in the air and it felt great!

Evenings were spent on the same machine reading Peter’s blog or Emily’s or Jack’s or PlosOne or whatever excitotoxic knowledge I could gorge on. I was pushing my central clock further. Then you first pointed how important it was to pay attention to circadian signaling . I tried to follow the sun and felt better for it. I did CT and felt better again. I wasen’t mitigating nnEMF as it was not yet talked about.

Meanwhile after may be 6 months of LCKD and a year lowish GFCarbs It was apparent something was a little off. Secondary infertility was diagnosed.

Gulp! Jimmy’s disease! I was a little bit metabolically deranged but I had never been overweight! So why me too?

I had something in common with Jimmy: Xgeneration, ketones , plastics and electronics, blue jeans and bluelights.

I don’t think he bikes though….

Docs didn’t know why. I still don’t know why. Just a bunch of hypothesis to sort out. And thank you so much, this blog helps a lot to connect some dots.

### Paleodocteur — 2015-03-08T21:33:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-157877>

Prostatis are common among young people. They are also more common in winter.… CT mismatch?

So the helicoidally shaped mitos require enough protons to make it to paradise. The pH 8 of my measly sample caught the eyes of the urologist….pointing to a an hampered prostatic function.

We are after DHA that is on sn-2 location.
Excess EPA suppresses AA.
We need good amount of AA.
At LabCorp or Mayo Clinic one can get fatty acids checked:

Fatty Acid Profile, Comprehensive (C8-C26), Serum

Above test checks 35 fatty acids, each comes with range of values.
Aim at mid range of those values for each of the 35 fatty acids.
Manipulate your environment, life style, Circadian Rhythm, diet to achieve that.

In view of dr K discussions I guess that it is ok to have more of DHA on that test, good amount but not too much EPA.

### CTforlife — 2015-03-09T23:16:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158021>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=157806)
>
> @[ssj3](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9728/) and @[NeilBB](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6879/)
>
> Time to post of the half-baked thoughts regarding protein intake I guess  . Too lazy to source all my references right now, so you'll have to look for them yourselves if you want to verify my claims
>
> \----
> **Personal Bias**
>
> I will state up front that I am not a fan of building extra muscle. I'm fine with how much muscle I'm carrying right now (me at 6'2'' and 175lbs in red underwear  -- [http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/2015-01-25/front.jpg](http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/2015-01-25/front.jpg)), after having done basically no resistance training for the last 7 months, and just biking around the place in tropical Cairns while focusing on strength and stability rather than moving heavy things around.
>
> I tend to agree with Neil that building extra muscle is energetically very costly, and that strength in natural movements can be attained without extra muscular hypertrophy.
>
> I tend to endorse periods of cyclic protein consumption -- high protein days and low protein days. High meaning "ad libitum", and low meaning 100g or less. (this will not be elaborated in this post, because it's already too long as is )
>
> \----
>
> **Protein "Requirements"
> **
> A lot of the studies in this respect are context specific, and tend to rely on the concept of Nitrogen Balance. Many of the studies that Bill Lagakos cites in his Protein Requirements blog rely on this concept -- [http://caloriesproper.com/protein-requirements-carbs-and-nutrient-partitioning/](http://caloriesproper.com/protein-requirements-carbs-and-nutrient-partitioning/)
>
> There are flaws with using Nitrogen Balance to measure protein needs, but mainly because measurement of Nitrogen Balance has wide error margins, and not that it is an inaccurate way of measuring protein needs.
>
> I think the general direction that Bill is hinting at is correct, that any exercise helps to maintain nitrogen balance. This refers to anything from resistance training to endurance activity.
>
>
> Caveat: IMO the issue with net protein degradation with endurance activity is when energy requirements cannot be met. This is a separate issue. ie: if you can supply adequate energy to the muscle tissue through fatty acid oxidation, I suspect that protein retention will continue to be improved even with long bouts of endurance activity​
>
>
> Kevin Tipton and Robert Wolfe did a review considering most practical aspects of this -- [http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/0264041031000140554?journalCode=rjsp20](http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/0264041031000140554?journalCode=rjsp20) . I also like Lyle McDonald's take on this subject, from his 'The Protein Book'.
>
> Personally, I think:
>
>
> You will find net nitrogen balance cap off maybe around 1.7g/kg a day of protein intake.
>
> Net positive protein synthesis will likely increase until about an intake of 2.5g/kg
>
> Any extra protein in the context of heavy resistance training athletes will likely be oxidized, or shunted to glucose. Athletes can probably deal with this excess protein, and extra glucose just continues to drive work capacity anyway. An intake of up to 3.0g/kg for athletes is not likely harmful from the perspective of maximizing muscular hypertrophy
> ​
>
> There are many, many, many, many confounding factors . Just some examples:
>
> **\- What if the athlete was fasted during training?**
>
> **\- How much carbohydrate were they consuming**? This is especially a big factor with chronic high carbohydrate consumption
>
> **\- How accustomed to the training stimulus are they?** Old-school bodybuilders would periodise different training mesocycles with differing levels of volume and intensity. Daily-undulating periodisation is used by everyone from bodybuilders (Layne Norton's power-hyper split), to powerlifters (Mike Tuscherer's Heavy-Med-Low days). "Confusing the Muscle" with different stimuli is definitely effective at forcing net protein accumulation within the muscles.
>
> On the flip side, "getting used to an exercise stimulus" doesn't warrant extra tissue from being built, and we can see this reflected both in clinical studies of net nitrogen retention when a training protocol is sustained, and in anecdotal reports of bodybuilders "getting stale" on a particular protocol.
>
> \----
>
> **Muscle Type
> **
> Slow twitch fibers have lots of mitochondria in them. Fast twitch fibers don't.
>
> Muscle-building approaches generally target hypertrophy of both fiber types, on top of getting. You will hear the quote that, "the strongest bodybuilders are the biggest". This is generally true, and "getting strong" is usually synonymous with simultaneous sarcoplasmic hypertrophy along with myofibrillar hypertrophy of all muscle fiber types.
>
> Recently I came across Pavel Tsatouline's recommendations on Slow Twitch fiber hypertrophy -- [http://www.strongfirst.com/how-to-build-your-slow-fibers-part-i/](http://www.strongfirst.com/how-to-build-your-slow-fibers-part-i/) and [http://www.strongfirst.com/how-to-build-your-slow-fibers-part-ii/](http://www.strongfirst.com/how-to-build-your-slow-fibers-part-ii/)
>
> I like a lot of his principles, but specific to the topic, it made me think if growing mitochondria-bearing slow twitch fibers isn't that bad of thing in terms of "robbing energy from the brain".
>
> Yes, you will need to put extra energy into the muscles, but those fibers can deal with incoming substrate very well, increase full-body fatty acid utilisation, while enabling a lot of force generation capacity if need be. They can also help to both deal with and generate a free radical stimulus.
>
> I do not search for hypertrophy per se, but if it comes, then I want it to be muscle tissue that actually contain fat-burning mitochondria.
>
> \----
>
> **Strength
> **
> Most people would use less than 50% of muscles fibers when performing the first rep of a set of bicep curls using a weight that is a 10 rep maximum (RM).
>
> Fiber recruiting increases if you try to move the 10RM weight faster, but not everyone can activate their nervous system to actually move the weight fast. Teaching the nervous system to activate more strongly even with that 10RM weight is "getting stronger" during the first rep of that bicep curl.
>
> Generalised strength in a coordinated system of movement is 1,000,000 times more complex. What about sticking points? How do muscle groups synergise with each other in a particular movement? What about intra-abdominal pressure (Pavel Kolar's DNS comes to mind)? What about fascia restrictions? etc ......
>
> "Strength" needs to be qualified as the ability to move through a specific range of motion, using certain joints, under a specific set of metabolic conditions, perhaps with a load, etc .....
>
> My opinion is that nervous system coordination of muscles with simultaneous maintenance of core stability is what is limiting people in terms of generalised strength -- the type that helps you climb trees, protect your back while carrying your child, running around on the beach, etc ....
>
> \----
>
> **mTOR and Protein Synthesis
> **
> No, mTOR doesn't drive all forms of protein synthesis. I like to view it as a systemic mechanism that regulates full-body growth.
>
> I do not know how the feedback cycles work. Here's a doom-and-gloom scenario against trying to build big muscles: You build muscles in an energy deficient, get great protein synthesis in those muscles, and then they feedback to suppress systemic protein synthesis in the brain (due to lack of energy). Brain doesn't see adequate mTOR stimulus and doesn't get the appropriate autophagy. Again, completely hypothetical, but possible, and borne of all of unknown factors.
>
> \----
>
> **Eating Protein and/or Carbs after a Workout**
>
> Bridging off the previous section, skeletal tissue probably requires both a high insulin and high circulating Amino Acid (AA) level for good protein synthesis.
>
> I do not think that a lot of protein is required for "high levels of AAs", and even something like 5g of Essential Amino Acids (EAA) seem to mimic this condition in serum.
>
> Carbohydrate requirements can be viewed from both the perspective of glycogen replenishment, and from the perspective of increasing the anabolic insulin stimulus.
>
> Clearly, we need better muscular hypertrophy with a combination of carbs + protein after exercise. The dose depends on so many factors, though most athletes would probably benefit from 0.3 to 0.5g/kg of protein post workout. (again, emphasis on the word "athlete")
>
> With reference to the Ubiquination 6 article, do you need to eat protein to gain the "benefits of exercise"? If the benefits you are looking for are muscle hypertrophy, then absolutely yes. If the benefits are improved collagen quality due to improved autophagy inside the cell, then I actually think that the answer is probably "No" (unless your cell is seriously energy depleted due to the exercise, which again, begs the question of why are you doing exercise beyond your hormetic threshold in the first place)
>
> ....

From my own N=1, I can confidently say building lean muscle mass with dha increases total body energy delivery to all organs, not reduces. If you use ct plus dha and lift heavy (which feels incredibly natural and not stressful in the least bit). Jack has a post saying dha is the best way to thermodynamical alter a human. If you build your muscles with dha protein sources and don't aim for hypotrophy style training while lifting heavy your muscles grow without having to train anything close to a body builder... Just because you get bigger doesn't mean it's invoking energy mass equivalence. Muscles still need dha at the inner mitochondrial membrane ? And cell membrane? I would think more dha = better. That's what my own n=1 showed me, not be scared of muscle, just to be scared of how you got it.

Someone can take steroids and eat like poop and gain muscle or they could do the above. What's the difference , how the dha is being used!

> [CTforlife said:](/goto/post?id=158021)

If you use ct plus dha and lift heavy (which feels incredibly natural and not stressful in the least bit). Jack has a post saying dha is the best way to thermodynamical alter a human. Just because you get bigger doesn't mean it's invoking energy mass equivalence. Muscles still need dha at the inner mitochondrial membrane ? And cell membrane? I would think more dha = better. More mass means more energy required to both build and sustain that mass.

Agreed that most people try to add energy only to the muscles while not having a large enough systemic increase in energy, which results in energy steal from the brain.

Once you've added muscle mass, how easy is it for the body to maintain the added muscle in the long term? How does added muscle mass affect how well the brain can handle energy-sapping stressors? My personal bias is always to err on the side of caution, and trying to deal with a higher-energy system is something that requires more balancing, and which I still is fragile as a result.

I tend to agree with the philosophies of people like Pavel Tsatouline and Edo Portal, who seek strength in 3-dimensions while maintaining core stability. This is training the nervous system first, which is basically adding energy to both the CNS and PNS. In other words, this is filling up the brain first, and then allowing it to build whatever muscles it wants to build.

If that is the definition of lifting heavy -- low reps, focusing on neuro-muscular efficiency, stable core, no significant H+ ion muscular accumulation, etc .... then I think any added muscular mass is "permitted by the brain due to favourable energetics".

### PaulG — 2015-03-10T01:26:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158030>

Biochemistry Geek warning

This article "[**Hydrogen Tunneling Links Protein Dynamics to Enzyme Catalysis**](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4066974/)**"** connects Ubiquination series to Tensegrity series (and OSF) and methylation

> Jack Kruse said:
>
> Ubiquitination appears to represent a regulated mechanism of direct reversible control over the PTEN enzyme. PTEN is a key signaling event mediating these metabolic and proliferative responses is the activation of the phosphatidylinositol-3 kinases (PI3K) pathway in cancer. PI3K keeps the balance between metabolism and tumor growth, so my bet is, that mitochondrial carrier proteins are key enzymes coupling these pathways. Since they are small enzymes, they must use proton tunneling, to link growth and metabolism to circadian cycles. If tunneling become impossible because of redox shifted mitochondria decoupling dominates the cell.

> **SUMMARY POINTS**
>
> 1.  The persistent deviation of empirical observations for enzymatic C–H activation from semiclassical predictions leads to the incorporation of QM nuclear tunneling into theoretical models.
> 2.  The pervasiveness of tunneling properties in enzymatic C–H activation that cannot be explained by a 1D tunneling correction leads to a multidimensional physical picture that resembles Marcus models for electron transfer.
> 3.  The availability of an analytical rate expression for nonadiabatic H-transfer, which can be used to represent physical parameters that control tunneling and to quantitatively reproduce or even be fitted to experimental data, is discussed.
> 4.  The importance of proximal, active-site side-chain packing in achieving an optimal approach of the H-donor to the H-acceptor is apparent in numerous systems.
> 5.  The importance of a stochastic protein conformational sampling process is presented in the context of achieving the optimal TRS.
> 6.  The link of protein conformational substates to the properties of active-site tunneling is demonstrated in two paradigmatic enzyme systems, ht-ADH and DHFR.
> 7.  The role for active-site compaction in enzymatic methyl transfer reactions, which is modulated by active-site side chains, may represent an extension of the concepts introduced for H-transfer reactions to larger group transfer reactions.
> 8.  The above features are likely to be relevant to future designs of highly active/selective catalysts and, possibly, to new strategies for drug design.
>
> **FUTURE ISSUES**
>
> 1.  More efforts are needed for the development of an accessible analytical rate expression for H-tunneling that can accommodate, within a cohesive physical picture, the processes underlying both nonadiabatic (e.g., hydrogen atom transfer) and adiabatic (e.g., hydride and proton transfer) reactions.
> 2.  A possible role for nonstatistical dynamics in promoting dynamics of atoms remote from the H-donor and -acceptor is yet to be deciphered theoretically and experimentally.
> 3.  The structural networks within proteins that act, via dynamical sampling, to establish the TRS in enzyme active sites will need to be identified in a system-dependent manner.
> 4.  More work is required before the above information can be used to develop a set of rules for predicting the regions of proteins that will be most influential in linking protein flexibility/motions to active-site compactness.
> 5.  Researchers need to continue the exploration of spectroscopic methods that allow specific time constants to be linked to H-transfer, in particular in the picosecond to femtosecond range. Time-dependent fluorescence is a promising tool, as is nonlinear vibrational spectroscopy, which probes the dynamics of the reacting ligands.
> 6.  A focus on the principles that arise from these studies may enable the sought-after goal of de novo design of enzyme catalysts that reproduce the activity of native enzymes.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-10T07:15:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158052>

> [CTforlife said:](/goto/post?id=158044)
>
> Dha has a mass but jack still recommends eating "massive amounts". I think this is solely a context issue. In one post "the colder the water, the bigger the mammal". Well if you are a human who only eats dha protein sources and uses ct post exercise, I'm going to assume the muscle becomes an energy producer and not an energy hog. Do you see where I'm coming from? The tissues with the most dha has the highest metabolic rate... If your muscles are filled with dha?

> [CTforlife said:](/goto/post?id=158046)
>
> Elaborate. Where can I find the Q & A

Gotta be a member for the Q&A , and the recording is not out yet since it was just held about 6 hours ago. Recording probably out tomorrow.

I know where your logic is coming from, and I think it is correct that Cold **permits** larger mass due to favourable energetics.

I'm not saying that you can't add extra muscle if you manage to add extra energy into the system. I'm cautioning against the fragility of being dependent on needing more energy to maintain tissues which cannot be net energy producers

We just need to look at what happens to those large animals when they find themselves in an environment that cannot provide them with the appropriate energetic efficiency . You will see system failure because the larger mass becomes an impediment to the function of critical organs. You can't keep a Moose in a tropical climate, and Polar Bears in tropical zoos suffer like crazy.

Of course, humans are more adaptable than large cold-adapted animals, and even a large human is not actually that large. Still, what happens to a large muscle-bound human when he steps on board a 14-hour flight across the Pacific?

DHA in muscles isn't going to help at all. What will help is to try as much as possible to uncouple the metabolism (Rubin's cold glove ), to produce EZ-water-generating IR light to counter the dehydrating effect of all that nn-EMF while you're in the air.

I personally have a hunch that having larger muscles macroscopically actually scale to increased microscopic distances between cells/fibers, which will in turn reduce the relative amount of EZ in skeletal muscles, and decrease their energetic efficiency.

Study on muscle fibers in the vastus medialis of trained males -- [http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1113/jphysiol.2005.101642/full](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1113/jphysiol.2005.101642/full)

That study basically echoes the same sort of findings that we see in all Heavy Weight Training studies. Heavy resistance exercise basically causes hypertrophy of primary Type 2 "fast-twitch" fibers. Type 2A fibers have lower mitochondrial and capillary density than Type 1 "slow-twitch" fibers, and Type 2X fibers have basically no mitochondria, and are dependent on glycolytic fuelling.

On top of that, most studies show an adaptation to convert Type 1 fibers to Type 2 fibers in response to heavy training.

On top of that, almost all studies will show that the increase in muscle volume is due to sarcoplasmic hypertrophy -- more fluid in the muscle, which increases the mean distance of any water molecule to a hydrophilic protein (not a good thing for energy production).

Under almost any circumstance, **you're not going to be able to add more muscle fibers**. Bigger muscles are a result of either increased muscle fiber size, or more likely, increased fluid retention in the muscle. To me, this does not sound like improved energetic efficiency.

\----

Appropriate use of endurance activity actually seems to bode well -- [http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/232/6/H705.short](http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/232/6/H705.short)

**quote**: "The capillaries per fiber ratios were 1.77+/-0.10 and 2.49+/-0.08 (mean+/-SE) in the UT and ET groups, respectively. The numbers of capillaries around each fiber were 4.43+/-0.19 (UT) and 5.87+/-0.18 (ET). The numbers of capillaries per mm2 were 585+/-40 (UT) and 821+/-28 (ET)"

"ET' stands for "Endurance Trained", and "UT" stands for "Untrained".

More capillaries mean more water trapped in small spaces => more EZ water and more energy delivery potential.

HIIT, which should not be confused with heavy resistance training, produces at least the adaptation for increased mitochondrial density -- [http://ajpregu.physiology.org/content/295/1/R264.short](http://ajpregu.physiology.org/content/295/1/R264.short)
​

Now if this is true (that smaller muscles are more tightly packed muscles), how would a person with less muscle mass and a tighter collagen network be affected by that same 14-hour plane flight? My guess is that the dehydrating effects are less so due to the average decreased distance between water molecules and hydrophilic proteins.

\----

DHA is not a producer of energy. It's role is to turn light signals into electricity and vice versa. DHA in the skin is useful because it can take sunlight and turn it into electric charge to drive chemical processes (especially sulfation of Vit D).

As far as we're concerned the only type of energy that can be emitted by mitochondria within the muscles is IR light (assuming that those are slow twitch fibers that actually contain DHA). There is free radical production, but those are signalling agents, not an energy source.

So for muscles to become a net producer of energy, they need to have the heat produced by the muscle structure water and leads to better collagen tensegrity, this is dependent on how tight your collagen networks are, not how much DHA there is in individual muscle cells.

I don't think any tissue can ever become a net producer of energy. Animals are fundamentally disconnected from the earth, and need both proton and electron currents to build EZ water to act as the battery of life.

....

Hopefully, my snipping out bits doesn't damage the message...

Don't know about those papers. Here is my personal observation. I am a post-menopausal woman who lifts heavy weights (squats, deadlifts, clean&jerk, snatch, etc.) 2 days a week for an hour each time. I've been doing this for a decade (I love the mental stress relief the training affords me). When I add a 3rd day, my muscles get rock hard even when I'm not flexing. It feels like more tensegrity, not less. When the lifting dose is high enough for long enough it seems to lead to an increase in water structuring. I know I'm not instantly adding myofibrils or titin strands in a one week interval. It seems to me it's gotta be water packing. Pollack's book "Muscles and Molecules: Uncovering the Principles of Biological Motion" (1990) discusses the mechanics of things like potassium ion migration, charge screening, myofibril distances that make me think that the muscle training creates adaptions in existing musculature that enhance water structuring and create more dense muscles. People with lots of testosterone do this, too, but also more easily add more myofibrils, etc. over time (e.g. a 12-week lifting cycle).

The appendix of Pollack's muscle book talks about testable predictions arising from the theory of this book. There are all sorts of structural things that can change in the course of acting on muscles without appreciably increasing total amount of protein mass. Helix-coil transitions, A-bridges, phosphorylation of light chains, etc. etc. These adaptions could very well increase the water structuring activation sites without adding more mass... just re-arranging the bits that are in situ allowing for more structuring to increase tensegrity. Strength results from tensegrity.

### Paul S — 2015-03-10T19:01:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158111>

....maybe I can convince my family, who always makes fun of me that I cannot float on my back in water (even the ocean) to save my life, is a good thing...I sink like a stone....

Question: Similar to what @[CindyB](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5731/) mentions above, I lift heavy only 1-2x per week for 30-40 minutes. However, with respect to this discussion of hypertrophy, it made me wonder the impact of T-replacement therapy? I've been relatively lean, but toned, for several years. I had an immediate positive response to paleo/epi-paleo when I first transitioned to that "diet" 4 years ago. Stayed same weight +/- 5lbs without effort. However, in past 10 months, I started exogenous T replacement based on labs and limited upward movement in levels without exogenous help. As a result, while purposefully reducing my weight training routine since starting T cypionate, I am still lean, but 10 pounds heavier, and stronger (more muscle mass, not surprisingly).

However, while having good levels of Free / Total T is the goal for health reasons, is the added muscle mass and weight a downside to this? Though, Dr Kruse, directly above, indicates he is talking about abnormal hypertrohy, which would not apply in my case. I am considering stopping T therapy (unrelated to this thread) in the coming months to see how well I maintain my levels given yet another year under the belt of following strict epi-paleo, EMF mitigation, circadian cycle matching, CT, etc.... Thoughts?

### Brother John — 2015-03-10T20:20:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158127>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=157739)
>
> I think this reflects back to my earlier observation that it's really important to specify the type and goals of the "exercise." To lump all forms of exercise together seems inadequate to me as well. I have questions too, but these are my current opinions...
>
> If your goal is bodybuilding (bigger muscles, more mass), it seems to me that you may need more protein intake as well. To use my analogy above, this would be like adding whole new wings to the existing building. If signaling is good, and you are leptin sensitive, then it seems to me that providing post workout protein (raw materials) makes some sense for this...
>
> mTOR is pro-growth but has also been linked with aging and multiple diseases. Some mTOR activity (like insulin activity, etc) is clearly necessary, but too much of it is almost surely a huge problem. It seems that the modern environment makes finding that sweet spot pretty difficult. So it may be pretty difficult to be a serious bodybuilder today without significant drawbacks. The question then has to rise, if intense bodybuilding is making it harder rather than easier to remain healthy, than why am I doing it? Is it worth it? Can I really _afford_ to maintain bigger muscles today? May it be better to have a smaller, more efficient house? A bigger house will after all, always require more maintenance.
>
> I would say if its really worth it to you to induce maximal muscular hypertrophy, then try to optimize circadian signaling and sleep and do intermittent fasting whenever you can. Maybe try to work out outside if possible.
>
> Personally, after I saw the ripped Mike Mentzer (who I admired very much for his work in philosophy and strategy of HIT in the 1990's) drop dead at age 49 back in 2001, I had to seriously question the modern assumption that maximum muscular hypertrophy equals maximum health. I don't think it does, especially today. So perhaps, one has to decide ultimately how far one is willing to stray from optimal health to support maximum muscular development. That has to be an individual value judgment.
>
> Remember that unique among primates, humans traded large muscles for large brains. To support large brains in today's environment is pretty tough. So to hypertrophy muscles too much seems counter-evolutionary, especially since it's a zero-sum game of limited resources.
>
> I would also say this. For me, aggressive CT along with optimized diet and circadian timing improved my own body comp without really much of any intense exercise at all for quite a while. Maximum hypertrophy? No, but far from Ethiopian runner too!
>
> There are also some who think that it possible to increase muscular strength without much actual muscular hypertrophy. Mentzer didn't believe this, but I think JoshL was talking about something like this recently. So there may actually be further choices between not only "health vs size," but also even "strength vs size."

Neil,
I remember when Mentzer died too... I think tremendous increases in strength can result without size increase when the nervous systems understand the movement and adapt/grow to accommodate.
Thanks,
Brother John

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-10T22:15:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158134>

> [CindyB said:](/goto/post?id=158096)
>
> Hopefully, my snipping out bits doesn't damage the message...
>
> Don't know about those papers. Here is my personal observation. I am a post-menopausal woman who lifts heavy weights (squats, deadlifts, clean&jerk, snatch, etc.) 2 days a week for an hour each time. I've been doing this for a decade (I love the mental stress relief the training affords me). When I add a 3rd day, my muscles get rock hard even when I'm not flexing. It feels like more tensegrity, not less. When the lifting dose is high enough for long enough it seems to lead to an increase in water structuring. I know I'm not instantly adding myofibrils or titin strands in a one week interval. It seems to me it's gotta be water packing. Pollack's book "Muscles and Molecules: Uncovering the Principles of Biological Motion" (1990) discusses the mechanics of things like potassium ion migration, charge screening, myofibril distances that make me think that the muscle training creates adaptions in existing musculature that enhance water structuring and create more dense muscles. People with lots of testosterone do this, too, but also more easily add more myofibrils, etc. over time (e.g. a 12-week lifting cycle).
>
> The appendix of Pollack's muscle book talks about testable predictions arising from the theory of this book. There are all sorts of structural things that can change in the course of acting on muscles without appreciably increasing total amount of protein mass. Helix-coil transitions, A-bridges, phosphorylation of light chains, etc. etc. These adaptions could very well increase the water structuring activation sites without adding more mass... just re-arranging the bits that are in situ allowing for more structuring to increase tensegrity. Strength results from tensegrity.

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=158098)
>
> True..........we are talking abnormal hypertrophy that steals from other tissues to build the muscle.......this is why heavy muscled people are not long lived. mTOR is why.
>
> Closest guys to having it right LeCorre and McGuff........but neither know why. Ubi 7 you get that answer.

@[CindyB](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5731/): Yup, this is correct.

The technical term for more muscle fibers is "myofibrillar hyperplasia", meaning that you actually gain more muscle fibers

There is very little evidence at present that adding more myofibrils above the baseline that you were born with is possible at all (though we do suspect that):

\- [https://lup.lub.lu.se/search/publication/1105995](https://lup.lub.lu.se/search/publication/1105995)
\- [http://jap.physiology.org/content/81/5/2004](http://jap.physiology.org/content/81/5/2004)

Now, myofibrillar **hypo**plasia is possible in certain illnesses, which means that you lose contractile fibers. If that is the case, then restoration of number of fibers (using something like exogenous testosterone supplementation) is more "returning to baseline" rather than trying to add more tissues above baseline

The current research shows that under anything within normal endogenous physiologic conditions, you don't add any more fibrils above that you were born with.

Strength is always good, and yes I think you're right that contractile force can increase through the improvements in the collagen network like you're talking about. That sort of adaptation increases tensegrity of the system, without a lot of added muscle volume. IMO, i**t is increased muscle volume, which is almost always largely a result of increased sarcoplasmic hypertrophy, which increases distances between water and proteins, and leads to decreased efficiency**

\----

I like your plan of lifting heavy twice a week

As a related point, "heavy" here needs to be defined.

Across a single set, "heavy" will be defined by:

(a) intensity -- how much of your 1RM are you lifting (1RM means "one rep maximum", the weight which you can only do one repetition with)
(b) how far you take the set to failure -- as measured by RPE (rating of perceived exertion)

Lifting at 80% intensity (ie: 160lbs squat when you can squat a 1RM of 200lbs), when you could actually do 6 reps, but stop at 4 reps (RPE 8), is still "lifting heavy". This will recruit all fiber types starting from the first rep, while producing full nervous system activation on all reps, but not actually "burning the nervous system out" (CNS over-activation)

Across multiple sets, "heavy" refers to anything that remains purely a neural stimulus, without metabolic strain.

I personally think that once RPEs move by 1 point, (eg: an RPE 8 weight becomes an RPE 9), then it is time to terminate sets. This assumes straight sets with the same weight at the same intended number of reps. Complications will arise if we're working with different muscle groups (due fiber type differences), different fatigue schemes (eg: occlusion training), or different rep schemes (eg: ladders)

I definitely endorse "heavy" lifting. This is not Mike Metzner or Martin Berkhan style reverse pyramid training, whereby you basically go to failure on your top set, and then proceed to add more drop sets. That is a different sort of stressor that serves as both a tension and metabolic stress to the muscle. I personally think that the tension component is what builds tensegrity, and is beneficial.

\----

Sidenote: If you listen to some of Gerald Pollack's interviews, he actually mentions that his research career started with researching muscle tissue. Then he realised that people were studying muscles in vitro, with the water taken out of them. That led to his whole career arc into the biology and physics of water.

....

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-03-11T18:13:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158224>

> [CindyB said:](/goto/post?id=158096)
>
> Hopefully, my snipping out bits doesn't damage the message...
> The appendix of Pollack's muscle book talks about testable predictions arising from the theory of this book. There are all sorts of structural things that can change in the course of acting on muscles without appreciably increasing total amount of protein mass. Helix-coil transitions, A-bridges, phosphorylation of light chains, etc. etc. These adaptions could very well increase the water structuring activation sites without adding more mass... just re-arranging the bits that are in situ allowing for more structuring to increase tensegrity. Strength results from tensegrity.

I agree with everything you say. There are a lot of "pro-brain" factors released by muscle, both as it grows and also when it contracts, anyhow. There are also a lot of issues, unfortunately, with some of the things said in this thread. Apparently, we're applying basic 'normal' physics to a whole 'alternative' field of biology, biochemistry, and biophysics; it would be nice if we applied the work of physicists who shaped the ideas of (and in many respects were light years ahead of) Ling, Pollack, Becker, etc. such as Walter and Lao Russell, AI Oparin, and many others. When applying their work, the whole mass equivalence thing may not even hold up, nor would many of the other arguments made here.

I wonder if people are familiar with this law (no, not a hypothesis like the work of Cunnane et al or anything like that): [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frank–Starling\_law](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frank%E2%80%93Starling_law)

Guess what plays a large role in the above? Skeletal muscle - the blood is massaged and milked back toward the heart, which in turn automatically provides an increased cardiac output to fuel the very muscles that are providing the needed venous return. More muscle, more muscle strength, the better the above works, the better your heart. This completely goes against 'heavily muscled individuals dying of heart attacks'. In fact, myokines secreted from muscle tissue (the more muscle the more myokines) impacts the heart and brain in beneficial ways. And going by anecdotes, I personally know no heavily muscular individuals who've died of heart attacks, yet I have many family members from the military who were literally jacked into their elderly years and lived a long time, family who played college football and are now older yet for most of their life were 200+lbs primarily of muscle, etc. I know that doesn't mean anything, but neither does the 'examples' of heavily muscled people dying for any reason.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-03-11T18:55:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158226>

> [bio-fractal-soul-self said:](/goto/post?id=158224)
>
> I agree with everything you say. There are a lot of "pro-brain" factors released by muscle, both as it grows and also when it contracts, anyhow. There are also a lot of issues, unfortunately, with some of the things said in this thread. When applying their work, the whole mass equivalence thing may not even hold up, nor would many of the other arguments made here.
>

>
> Guess what plays a large role in the above? Skeletal muscle - the blood is massaged and milked back toward the heart, which in turn automatically provides an increased cardiac output to fuel the very muscles that are providing the needed venous return. More muscle, more muscle strength, the better the above works, the better your heart. This completely goes against 'heavily muscled individuals dying of heart attacks'. In fact, myokines secreted from muscle tissue (the more muscle the more myokines) impacts the heart and brain in beneficial ways. And going by anecdotes, I personally know no heavily muscular individuals who've died of heart attacks, yet I have many family members from the military who were literally jacked into their elderly years and lived a long time, family who played college football and are now older yet for most of their life were 200+lbs primarily of muscle, etc. I know that doesn't mean anything, but neither does the 'examples' of heavily muscled people dying for any reason.

Maybe not to get too 'sciency' about this and more think what is 'natural' (or at least as much as possible) I grew up on a farm, we had no tractors or machines of any kind. All work was done by horses my dad and his children (us). I am not saying it was totally 'natural' but well closer than the way we live now. I am 62 y.o. now and thinking back to my dad when he was that age the idea of him 'working out' or 'lifting heavy weights' or 'jogging' or even "HIIT" well it seems a bit incredible..............as in a quite silly and un-likely thing for him to do.

I can remember building stone fences with him something so there was 'heavy lifting' involved in that but that would be RARE, as for as him sprinting and jogging etc it makes no sense. So I kind of take my lead from that why would I be acting so much different. I agree we have a problem now in our sedentary lifestyles it is quite different to the way he lived or people did for eons. There was a lot of walking around, lifting things but not so heavy, really heavy lifting would be rare indeed.

This might seem like a 'weak' argument but it reminds me when I came to the US in 1978 and there was thing thing going on about 'low fat' or 'no fat' milk and it seemed so weird to me. I mean we had 'no fat' milk which was what was left after the cream had been removed to make butter etc but we (humans) would not TOUCH that stuff, we had no regard at all for it. We fed it back to the animals. Anyway just to say because of my 'background' I could never quite get with the program in regards to things like 'no fat'. It was really something as simple as that that just did not make sense to me and maybe saved me from a worse (health) fate.

Anyway aside from being lazy I kind of apply that 'test' to things like lifting to the point of muscle failure................I am skeptical and also I think it varies according to age etc. I don't know enough 'science' at all to judge in that way but I don't need to say 'scientists' can get to prove anything so long as that is their orientation. So "pro brain factors" etc..........I dunno............anything can be 'proven' and what strikes me about a lot of this 'science' is it takes one 'snapshot' of a situation and then generalizes out from there. I think this is highly un-trustworthy as a 'method' and it is worth keeping Dr K's insistence how ALL biological reaction take place in 'time' any snapshot has a snapshot preceding it and one after it extending you might say to infinity in both directions. To me this is the great danger of mis-direction in a lot of this 'sciency' stuff.

### NeilBB — 2015-03-11T19:02:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158227>

> [bio-fractal-soul-self said:](/goto/post?id=158224)
>
> I agree with everything you say. There are a lot of "pro-brain" factors released by muscle, both as it grows and also when it contracts, anyhow. There are also a lot of issues, unfortunately, with some of the things said in this thread. Apparently, we're applying basic 'normal' physics to a whole 'alternative' field of biology, biochemistry, and biophysics; it would be nice if we applied the work of physicists who shaped the ideas of (and in many respects were light years ahead of) Ling, Pollack, Becker, etc. such as Walter and Lao Russell, AI Oparin, and many others. When applying their work, the whole mass equivalence thing may not even hold up, nor would many of the other arguments made here.
>
> I wonder if people are familiar with this law (no, not a hypothesis like the work of Cunnane et al or anything like that): [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frank–Starling\_law](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frank%E2%80%93Starling_law)
>
> Guess what plays a large role in the above? Skeletal muscle - the blood is massaged and milked back toward the heart, which in turn automatically provides an increased cardiac output to fuel the very muscles that are providing the needed venous return. More muscle, more muscle strength, the better the above works, the better your heart. This completely goes against 'heavily muscled individuals dying of heart attacks'. In fact, myokines secreted from muscle tissue (the more muscle the more myokines) impacts the heart and brain in beneficial ways. And going by anecdotes, I personally know no heavily muscular individuals who've died of heart attacks, yet I have many family members from the military who were literally jacked into their elderly years and lived a long time, family who played college football and are now older yet for most of their life were 200+lbs primarily of muscle, etc. I know that doesn't mean anything, but neither does the 'examples' of heavily muscled people dying for any reason.

Oh, Christ! Are you serious man? Clearly, skeletal muscle health, strength, and maintenance are important. Nobody here said that all exercise was bad or that we shouldn't lift heavy things when healthy.

Personally, when I do go to gyms, I still lift very heavy weight to near-failure (or failure) with a very low number of reps/sets and it makes me feel great and I have remained quite strong, even with long intervals between lifting, and never hurt myself. I am muscular for my age, but not really "big." But that's my N=1.

Those of us that have gone to medical school know that the actual Frank-Starling mechanism pertains to cardiac muscle, which is a whole different animal than skeletal muscle, because it has to constantly contract. Contraction of skeletal muscle does increase venous return which increases preload which invokes the Starling Law to increase cardiac output. True. But you don't need to be as big as Mentzer or Schwarzenaggar to make this happen. Too much muscle may even quite possibly impede venous return. Did you know that many bodybuilders also use occlusive devices around muscles _specifically_ to _impair_ venous return to the heart? Ostensibly to hypertrophy muscles by engorging them with blood at the _expense_ of venous return, thereby explicitly thwarting the Starling mechanism? Yep. It's called BFR (blood flow restriction) training. If that's not the definition of robbing Peter to pay Paul, and a clear acknowledgement of the zero-sum nature of this game, then I don't know what is, man!

What I was talking about above was going to extraordinary measures to cause unnatural degrees of hypertrophy, as many bodybuilders and powerlifters do. Not reasonable high intensity training in healthy individuals. Details matter.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-03-11T19:40:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158233>

I am not in any way trying to promote 'anti-science' but I am amazed by what I am reading in that book about vaccines and polio and HIV. It really makes the point which I suppose was Neil's point in his blog about 'belief systems' etc. It is amazing to me that 'scientists' can be so blind!

To summarize what she is saying: Retro-viruses were considered quite dangerous and very likely implicated in cancer. I am talking '60's - 70's here, the whole theory of 'onco-genes' was around that time and very much related to that idea. This theory was foundering badly but Robert Gallo (the 'discoverer/inventor' of HIV) was wedded to that notion. He has 'isolated' two retro-viruses HTLV-1 and HTLV-2. But his 'research' was going nowhere, 'reverse-transciptase' which he though the villain was turning out to be in ALL our cells and in ALL cells even plants. All of these processes it seems are completely 'normal' in the cell (also as Dr Kruse has shown about how we 'absorb' viruses or viral particles into our genes)

Anyway along comes AIDS.............and Gallo literally 're-resurrected' his career behind that 'epidemic'............and guess what turns out (according to him) it's HTLV-3..............how amazing JUST the 'family' of retro-viruses he was working on. What a co-incidence! Lightning just struck! (again) And I am afraid it is that silly and that 'wrong'. And it just gets worse from there. His HTLV-3 was later re-named HIV in a 'political compromise' with the French (Montagnier)

To be clear also I am not trying to promote the idea that HIV is an 'innocent bystander' and quite the wrong culprit (though now I DO believe that to be the case) but more to kind of show how totally 'blind' science can be. Especially of course 'bad' science. I am someone not really from a 'scientific' background or at least not 'heavily' and I am amazed how specialized and 'wrong' science can be. Again it kind of shows to me now the point Neil was making in his blog..................I really didn't think it could get that 'bad'

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-12T00:36:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158268>

Regarding muscular hypertrophy, what @[NeilBB](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6879/) talks about with occlusion style training is accurate.

For those interested in protocols, lookup Borge Fagerli's Myo Reps scheme ([http://borgefagerli.com/myo-reps-in-english/](http://borgefagerli.com/myo-reps-in-english/)), which is based on the research done on KAATSU Occlusion Training, as well as that done by Wernborn et al.

This has clinical relevance because it allows the utilisation of very light lights to stimulate muscular hypertrophy. Very useful for people with muscle wasting diseases, major nervous system dysfunctions (which prevent muscular coordination), and people subject to muscle wasting conditions (like Astronauts )

There is probably a way to use this in short bursts in a healthy fashion, but doing them as a means of excess muscular hypertrophy is an exercise to generate inefficiency.

When it comes to mechanics behind muscle growth, let's take the case of a diseased muscle, whereby myofibrillar hypo-plasia has occurred, and then subject it to both tension and metabolic stimulus.

(1) First, the lost fibrils are regrown
(2) Then, crosslinks between fibrils are re-established

If stimulus continues over a longer period:

(3a) increase in size of the fibrils occurs
(3b) increase in sarcoplasmic volume occurs

Muscle Cross Sectional Area will continue to increase across all stages, but (3a) and (3b) lead to energetic inefficiency, because water molecules are further away from hydrophilic proteins. Inter-fibril Cross linking may continue, but the actual unit of the muscle, the sarcomere volume will increase in size in greater proportion to the ability of proteins to close the distance between water molecules.

I am always for function first and foremost. It is more correct for the body to be able to do the things that @[prAna303](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1363/) and Jeremy Thomley, and then whatever muscular development that happens as a result of that is simply a side effect.

Force output is one way to measure function, but as Jeremy would tell you, climbing a rock face is a lot more than just about force production in 2-planes (which most exercises are, including stand like deadlifts and squats, which while have some sagittal loading, is still mostly just an exercise in axial resistance).

\----

Damnit @[patgrif@hotmail.com](mailto:patgrif@hotmail.com) , now you've thrown me down the rabbit hole of viral mechanics

HIV was supposedly exported to the world via a plane flight . Not saying that it's the flight that did it of course, but that the increased nn-EMF activity in the last 4-5 decades is probably the trigger for the human body "adopting the HIV virus" to attempt to solve particular immune system related problems caused by nn-EMF. It's no co-incidence that a HIV vector as superoxide stimulator was mentioned in Ubi 6.

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=158268)
>
> Regarding muscular hypertrophy, what @[NeilBB](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6879/) talks about with occlusion style training is accurate.
>
> For those interested in protocols, lookup Borge Fagerli's Myo Reps scheme ([http://borgefagerli.com/myo-reps-in-english/](http://borgefagerli.com/myo-reps-in-english/)), which is based on the research done on KAATSU Occlusion Training, as well as that done by Wernborn et al.
>
> This has clinical relevance because it allows the utilisation of very light lights to stimulate muscular hypertrophy. Very useful for people with muscle wasting diseases, major nervous system dysfunctions (which prevent muscular coordination), and people subject to muscle wasting conditions (like Astronauts )
>
> There is probably a way to use this in short bursts in a healthy fashion, but doing them as a means of excess muscular hypertrophy is an exercise to generate inefficiency.
>
> When it comes to mechanics behind muscle growth, let's take the case of a diseased muscle, whereby myofibrillar hypo-plasia has occurred, and then subject it to both tension and metabolic stimulus.
>
> (1) First, the lost fibrils are regrown
> (2) Then, crosslinks between fibrils are re-established
>
> If stimulus continues over a longer period:
>
> (3a) increase in size of the fibrils occurs
> (3b) increase in sarcoplasmic volume occurs
>
> Muscle Cross Sectional Area will continue to increase across all stages, but (3a) and (3b) lead to energetic inefficiency, because water molecules are further away from hydrophilic proteins. Inter-fibril Cross linking may continue, but the actual unit of the muscle, the sarcomere volume will increase in size in greater proportion to the ability of proteins to close the distance between water molecules.
>
> I am always for function first and foremost. It is more correct for the body to be able to do the things that @[prAna303](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1363/) and Jeremy Thomley, and then whatever muscular development that happens as a result of that is simply a side effect.
>
> Force output is one way to measure function, but as Jeremy would tell you, climbing a rock face is a lot more than just about force production in 2-planes (which most exercises are, including stand like deadlifts and squats, which while have some sagittal loading, is still mostly just an exercise in axial resistance).
>
> \----
>
> Damnit @[patgrif@hotmail.com](mailto:patgrif@hotmail.com) , now you've thrown me down the rabbit hole of viral mechanics
>
> HIV was supposedly exported to the world via a plane flight . It's no co-incidence that a HIV vector as superoxide stimulator was mentioned in Ubi 6.

It's a fascinating book from a 'theoretical' point of view but also has huge practical implications. It really starts to look like $200 billion down the drain on AIDS research...........

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-03-12T02:20:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158286>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=158227)
>
> Oh, Christ! Are you serious man? Clearly, skeletal muscle health, strength, and maintenance are important. Nobody here said that all exercise was bad or that we shouldn't lift heavy things when healthy.
>
> Personally, when I do go to gyms, I still lift very heavy weight to near-failure (or failure) with a very low number of reps/sets and it makes me feel great and I have remained quite strong, even with long intervals between lifting, and never hurt myself. I am muscular for my age, but not really "big." But that's my N=1.
>
> Those of us that have gone to medical school know that the actual Frank-Starling mechanism pertains to cardiac muscle, which is a whole different animal than skeletal muscle, because it has to constantly contract. Contraction of skeletal muscle does increase venous return which increases preload which invokes the Starling Law to increase cardiac output. True. But you don't need to be as big as Mentzer or Schwarzenaggar to make this happen. Too much muscle may even quite possibly impede venous return. Did you know that many bodybuilders also use occlusive devices around muscles _specifically_ to _impair_ venous return to the heart? Ostensibly to hypertrophy muscles by engorging them with blood at the _expense_ of venous return, thereby explicitly thwarting the Starling mechanism? Yep. It's called BFR (blood flow restriction) training. If that's not the definition of robbing Peter to pay Paul, and a clear acknowledgement of the zero-sum nature of this game, then I don't know what is, man!
>
> What I was talking about above was going to extraordinary measures to cause unnatural degrees of hypertrophy, as many bodybuilders and powerlifters do. Not reasonable high intensity training in healthy individuals. Details matter.

Did I say that everyone here said that every form of exercise is always bad? No. Please re-read my post and don't jump to conclusions. I'm specifically talking about muscle mass. Secondly, you should re-investigate the Frank-Starling Law:

Starling’s Law states that cardiac output is directly proportional to the volume of blood that is returned to the heart, more or less. If a larger volume of blood is returned to the right side of the heart, the ventricle is stretched and the cardiac myocytes are in turn stretched, which causes them to contract more forcefully..…skeletal muscle mass and how hard it is working at any given time ties directly into this. Through this mechanism, cardiac output can auto-regulate on an almost purely mechanical basis. So when a large mass of skeletal muscle is working really hard, a large volume of venous blood is massaged and milked back toward the heart, which in turn automatically provides an increased cardiac output to fuel the very muscles that are providing the needed venous return.

Here is another cool thing about skeletal muscle’s contribution to Frank Starling’s Law: coronary artery blood flow is directly proportionate to the volume of venous return on the right side of the heart. When a given volume of blood arrives in the right heart, Frank Starling’s Law dictates that this is the volume of blood that will be delivered to the left ventricle, which in turn determines the volume of blood that will be ejected out of the aortic valve into the aorta during systole. During diastole (the relaxation phase) a portion of the blood ejected into the aorta will backwash into the now closed aortic valve. At the base of the aorta are three valve leaflets that are now closed and look like a Mercedes emblem. Two of these leaflets have “ostia” or openings that lead into the left and right main coronary arteries ([http://www.vhlab.umn.edu/atlas/aorta/coronary-artery-ostia/index.shtml](http://www.vhlab.umn.edu/atlas/aorta/coronary-artery-ostia/index.shtml)). Thus we can now see that volume returned to the right heart determines blood ejected from the left heart and thus the amount of blood that will flow through the coronary arteries. This is the basis for a type of treatment for coronary artery disease and congestive heart failure called “extracorporeal counterpulsation” ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enhanced\_external\_counterpulsation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enhanced_external_counterpulsation)). This system uses air bladders on the legs that contract in synchrony with diastole to enhance venous return and thus improve coronary artery blood flow, collateralizing of coronary arteries and improvement of cardiac function in those with CHF. Guess what does the same thing by the same mechanism? Resistance training, especially high intensity resistance training.

More muscle, more strength, more muscle activation, more cardiac auto-regulation, better signals to the muscles, etc.; a positive feedback loop of sorts.

Oh, by the way, I'm one of "us" when it comes to medical school. Well, kinda. Attaining a PsyD, but taking some time to complete course work in medical physics. Also going to be attaining certifications in Bau Biology. Shouldn't DHA help with jumping to conclusions somehow, perhaps in a quantum mechanism  ?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-12T02:36:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158291>

Acta Ophthalmol Suppl. 1993;(208):1-50.
**[Inhibition of enzymes by short-wave optical radiation and its effect on the retina.](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8384503)**
Chen E.
**Exposure to short-wave optical radiation is a potential hazard for vision. In the present study, blue-light damage is studied in rat retina.** It was hypothesized that the absorption of blue light by cytochrome oxidase in rat retina inhibits this enzyme, and may reduce the retinal oxidative metabolism. Irreversible inhibition of the oxidative metabolism may decrease the activity of the Na/K-ATPase, hence redistribute ions, increase intracellular osmotic pressure and cause cellular edema. Severe retinal edema may be the cause of retinal degeneration…**Blue light inhibited cytochrome oxidase at a retinal dose of about 110 kJ/m2. This inhibition was reversible, and is probably related to the light regulation of retinal metabolism. At a retinal dose of about 380 kJ/m2, the inhibition of cytochrome oxidase was followed consecutively by a probable redistribution of chlorine and potassium in the inner and outer segments, damage to the mitochondria in the inner segments, edema in the inner and outer segments, and progressive degeneration of photoreceptor cells. Dark adaptation did not increase the blue-light retinal injury. These findings support the hypothesis that inhibition of cytochrome oxidase is one of the causes of blue-light retinal damage.** The alteration of enzyme kinetics after in vitro exposure to short-wave optical radiation was estimated using lactate dehydrogenase as a model. The ultraviolet-radiation exposure inhibited lactate dehydrogenase with a significant decrease in maximal velocity, while Michaelis constant remained unchanged. LIGHT IS THE DOMAIN OF PHYSICS AND LIGHT ENTRAINS THE SCN. BIOLOGY PAYS ATTENTION TO PHYSICS

Their work maybe more important to your perspective........not mine.

^Cites Pollack, Electric Universe folks, and others. Check all the citations. This isn't some new age quackery. This is science that has been discussed for over 100 years. He doesn't mention Stan Meyer if I'm not mistaken. Stan Myer discussed H2 and had a hunch water and H2 could power vehicles. He then created a buggie running off of these sources. He then mysteriously died. 17 years later, guess what is coming out? Cars running on the same things, lol. All this stuff is available for us to create at home, all it requires is know how. Anyone can teach themselves electronics, physics, etc. I started from being 100% disabled to now, as I said, attempting to attain a PsyD while studying medical physics on the side; but.... like 50% of everything I was "taught" I already learned from DIY stuff, pubmed, purchasing textbooks and reading them while disabled, etc. What I'm getting at is, this stuff is out there. If people want to remain blind, that's up to them, but I've been extremely enlightened by Dr. Kruse, but him not even knowing about these other researchers is, well, beyond disheartening. I don't think one should discuss Ling and Pollack without reading their work first. Ling got his ideas from Budenborg de Jong, and Budenborg de Jong's work is related to AI Oparin's on the origin of life which directly connects to Russellian science, although the two men never met or have referenced each other or anything. This then all really elucidates some of the EU stuff some people post here. It's all connected.

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-03-12T03:24:08+0200

Tesla's alternating currents really had nothing to do with his supposed "madness". He also ate a really weird diet, never slept, did lots of work in complete darkness, abstained from sunlight at certain times due to working for days straight (again, without sleep), and "bio-hacked" with weird tech to access alternate states of consciousness. Interestingly, Becker has also referenced some of the work of Tesla's colleagues, so it seems as though he was up to date on REAL physics and biophysics, too.

I'll discuss this more in my journal, or another thread, as this is derailing the topic of ubiquination. But I'd suggest people look into:
\- Budenborg de Jong (predates all of Ling's research)
\- Viktor Schauberger
\- AI Oparin
\- Walter and Lao Russel
\- Jason Verbelli
\- Dr. George Washington Crille
\- And idk, about 15 others I can't think of right now
\- (none of these people were quacks, especially the MDs listed - never lost a medical license, were suspended, etc.; perhaps some ridicule, but that's it; no quackery here)

.... all their work predates and trumps Becker's and Ling's, and not reading theirs will give a major misinterpretation, or, can at least help to connect more dots.
