### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-12T03:37:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158314>

Remember to keep things civil guys , and to stay focused on distilling information into useful statements.

Arguments over a particular person's flaws or "A was ahead of B" doesn't help to keep the discussion focused on the ideas that those people had. We're here to turn ideas into testable and actionable hypotheses.

@[bio-fractal-soul-self](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/12496/) : You can help a great deal by writing down the implications of the work that you've read in concise and precise statements

\-----

The reason I worry about quantum mechanics in this specific discussion about muscle, autophagy, etc ...., is because all those processes are driven by enzymatic pathways, and all enzymes require water to work as a quasicrystal, and require proton tunnelling for their effects to manifest.

\----
**Mass Equivalence**

I tend to agree that the term "Mass equivalence" can be thrown around a fair bit too loosely, and I think it gets confounded with statements along the lines of "Energetic inefficiency causes a mitochondria to swell".

Let's get the arrow of causality straight: When your cells swell, the RESULT is that you become energy inefficient in that cell.

"Inefficiency" means that you produce 100 units of energy and can now only use 20 units to do useful work, instead of the 50 units you normally would.

In a coupled system, this inefficiency tends to propagate to the rest of the system. (much to the chagrin of this software developer )

Now we get to the "mass equivalence" part .... because there is **less efficiency**, you need **more total energy** for things to work correctly.

More energy => more mass. This is a definition in Physics (mass is just a measure of electromagnetic momentum of a particle -- [http://qhwiki.com/video:eu-nov-2014-wal-thornhill](http://qhwiki.com/video:eu-nov-2014-wal-thornhill)), and our empirical observations seem to support this at everything from the pico-meter to light-year scale.

\-----

> [bio-fractal-soul-self said:](/goto/post?id=158224)
>
> I wonder if people are familiar with this law (no, not a hypothesis like the work of Cunnane et al or anything like that): [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frank–Starling\_law](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frank%E2%80%93Starling_law)

The Frank-Starling Law is an **observation** that "stroke volume of the heart increases in response to an increase in the volume of blood filling the heart (the end diastolic volume) when all other factors remain constant"

Hypothetical Question: Is increased stroke volume a symptom of greater energy efficiency and energy delivery to tissues?

Gerald Pollack has found that blood delivery to tissues of the body is mostly powered by ionic gradients over EZ water. Those ionic potentials become electric potentials once you hit the small nanometer tight spaces of the capillaries, and that is what mainly causes blood flow and oxygen (electron) delivery tissues like the skeletal muscles.

Personally, I'm ambivalent to the Frank-Starling Law, and think that it doesn't offer enough consideration to the rest of the factors affecting energetic delivery to tissues to be useful to this discussion. Doesn't mean that it isn't valid in a lot of cases, it just doesn't answer the question about how to improve energy efficiency through growth of skeletal tissues.

\----

Whether or not more muscle means better health outcomes will depend on the person's context. Could a person going from 180lbs at 15% to 170lbs at 12% negatively affect their health?

Yes, and just to use an extreme example, imagine the example of a person who moves to Colorado (high altitude), jumps on a paleo diet, lifts heavy 3 times a week, and cycles on the weekends, while using carbs to fuel his training. That person has increased energy in his system using methods that aren't sustainable. Any muscle he has gained requires that higher energy state to be sustained. If that high energy state is not sustained, bad things happen as energy is lost from the brain.

The way that we figure out if the extra muscle is good or bad is:

(1) take clinical measurements (hormones, etc ...) and self-assessments of redox potential
(2) marry actual empirical observations with empirical mechanistic explanations (which usually boil down to biology + physics), and then use that to generate plausible and testable hypotheses
(3) apply those hypotheses, and go back to step (1)

\----

And of course, using nature as a straight edge, I want to crawl, dive, sprint, climb, walk, roll ..... and do that in a way in which I can maintain stability of my core, and use as little energy with each movement as possible.

....

Or this thing about Archimedes Principle.................I find myself endlessly 'playing' with the idea of being in the womb, or organelles in cells or floating in the ocean or the brain in CSF...............to me it is stimulating to my 'imagination' and I think that is one of the wonderful things about it. I even think as people get more 'connected' they can do that just a little bit more...............I see that kind of thinking as symptomatic of a 'connected' brain...................

I'm going to have to bio back this and get back. I think their are some wrong assumptions about muscle size and tensgrit

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=158268)
>
> Regarding muscular hypertrophy, what @[NeilBB](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6879/) talks about with occlusion style training is accurate.
>
> For those interested in protocols, lookup Borge Fagerli's Myo Reps scheme ([http://borgefagerli.com/myo-reps-in-english/](http://borgefagerli.com/myo-reps-in-english/)), which is based on the research done on KAATSU Occlusion Training, as well as that done by Wernborn et al.
>
> This has clinical relevance because it allows the utilisation of very light lights to stimulate muscular hypertrophy. Very useful for people with muscle wasting diseases, major nervous system dysfunctions (which prevent muscular coordination), and people subject to muscle wasting conditions (like Astronauts )
>
> There is probably a way to use this in short bursts in a healthy fashion, but doing them as a means of excess muscular hypertrophy is an exercise to generate inefficiency.
>
> When it comes to mechanics behind muscle growth, let's take the case of a diseased muscle, whereby myofibrillar hypo-plasia has occurred, and then subject it to both tension and metabolic stimulus.
>
> (1) First, the lost fibrils are regrown
> (2) Then, crosslinks between fibrils are re-established
>
> If stimulus continues over a longer period:
>
> (3a) increase in size of the fibrils occurs
> (3b) increase in sarcoplasmic volume occurs
>
> Muscle Cross Sectional Area will continue to increase across all stages, but (3a) and (3b) lead to energetic inefficiency, because water molecules are further away from hydrophilic proteins. Inter-fibril Cross linking may continue, but the actual unit of the muscle, the sarcomere volume will increase in size in greater proportion to the ability of proteins to close the distance between water molecules.
>
> I am always for function first and foremost. It is more correct for the body to be able to do the things that @[prAna303](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1363/) and Jeremy Thomley, and then whatever muscular development that happens as a result of that is simply a side effect.
>
> Force output is one way to measure function, but as Jeremy would tell you, climbing a rock face is a lot more than just about force production in 2-planes (which most exercises are, including stand like deadlifts and squats, which while have some sagittal loading, is still mostly just an exercise in axial resistance).
>
> \----
>
> Damnit @[patgrif@hotmail.com](mailto:patgrif@hotmail.com) , now you've thrown me down the rabbit hole of viral mechanics
>
> HIV was supposedly exported to the world via a plane flight . Not saying that it's the flight that did it of course, but that the increased nn-EMF activity in the last 4-5 decades is probably the trigger for the human body "adopting the HIV virus" to attempt to solve particular immune system related problems caused by nn-EMF. It's no co-incidence that a HIV vector as superoxide stimulator was mentioned in Ubi 6.
>
> ...

> \-----
>
> The reason I worry about quantum mechanics in this specific discussion about muscle, autophagy, etc ...., is because all those processes are driven by enzymatic pathways, and all enzymes require water to work as a quasicrystal, and require proton tunnelling for their effects to manifest.
>
> \----
> **Mass Equivalence**
>
> I tend to agree that the term "Mass equivalence" can be thrown around a fair bit too loosely, and I think it gets confounded with statements along the lines of "Energetic inefficiency causes a mitochondria to swell".
>
> Let's get the arrow of causality straight: When your cells swell, the RESULT is that you become energy inefficient in that cell.
>
> "Inefficiency" means that you produce 100 units of energy and can now only use 20 units to do useful work, instead of the 50 units you normally would.
>
> In a coupled system, this inefficiency tends to propagate to the rest of the system. (much to the chagrin of this software developer )
>
> Now we get to the "mass equivalence" part .... because there is **less efficiency**, you need **more total energy** for things to work correctly.
>
> More energy => more mass. This is a definition in Physics (mass is just a measure of electromagnetic momentum of a particle -- [http://qhwiki.com/video:eu-nov-2014-wal-thornhill](http://qhwiki.com/video:eu-nov-2014-wal-thornhill)), and our empirical observations seem to support this at everything from the pico-meter to light-year scale.
>
> \-----
>
>
>
> The Frank-Starling Law is an **observation** that "stroke volume of the heart increases in response to an increase in the volume of blood filling the heart (the end diastolic volume) when all other factors remain constant"
>
> Hypothetical Question: Is increased stroke volume a symptom of greater energy efficiency and energy delivery to tissues?
>
> Gerald Pollack has found that blood delivery to tissues of the body is mostly powered by ionic gradients over EZ water. Those ionic potentials become electric potentials once you hit the small nanometer tight spaces of the capillaries, and that is what mainly causes blood flow and oxygen (electron) delivery tissues like the skeletal muscles.
>
> Personally, I'm ambivalent to the Frank-Starling Law, and think that it doesn't offer enough consideration to the rest of the factors affecting energetic delivery to tissues to be useful to this discussion. Doesn't mean that it isn't valid in a lot of cases, it just doesn't answer the question about how to improve energy efficiency through growth of skeletal tissues.
>
> \----
>
> Whether or not more muscle means better health outcomes will depend on the person's context. Could a person going from 180lbs at 15% to 170lbs at 12% negatively affect their health?
>
> Yes, and just to use an extreme example, imagine the example of a person who moves to Colorado (high altitude), jumps on a paleo diet, lifts heavy 3 times a week, and cycles on the weekends, while using carbs to fuel his training. That person has increased energy in his system using methods that aren't sustainable. Any muscle he has gained requires that higher energy state to be sustained. If that high energy state is not sustained, bad things happen as energy is lost from the brain.
>
> The way that we figure out if the extra muscle is good or bad is:
>
> (1) take clinical measurements (hormones, etc ...) and self-assessments of redox potential
> (2) marry actual empirical observations with empirical mechanistic explanations (which usually boil down to biology + physics), and then use that to generate plausible and testable hypotheses
> (3) apply those hypotheses, and go back to step (1)
>
> \----
>
> And of course, using nature as a straight edge, I want to crawl, dive, sprint, climb, walk, roll ..... and do that in a way in which I can maintain stability of my core, and use as little energy with each movement as possible.
>
> ....

Okay, read through this. Don't have much to add. I have to still get some studying in, then sleep  I tend to agree with what you state above, especially with YOUR interpretation of the way we can/should apply "mass equivalence". My point in a more simplistic manner was more so that adding a realistic, "natural" amount of muscle will not, in terms of any evidence - direct, indirect, Ling, Pollack, physics, etc - detract from brain power, especially when looking into the epigenetics of muscle signalling, myokines, and more. Brain size isn't 100% tied to intelligence anyhow.

I will, as I said, make a separate thread or post in my journal about some of the things I linked above. For anyone who doesn't believe me, just search up John Searl, Albert Roy Davis, Walter C. Rawls, DR. Hongsheng Zhao, or any of the others I will post about soon. I will be utilizing a lot of their work in physics, geobiology, and more, in my own home and land soon (building biodomes, completely self sustaining gardens, etc) and will updated about it a lot. There are also sites where you can link up with open soure projects utilizing all this information on water and electromagnetism. For instance, this page (which also has a youtube page): [http://rwgresearch.com/](http://rwgresearch.com/)

There's some really nifty stuff we can do with the things contained in the rwg research page

>
> If you'd like, you can search of the open source projects yourself. Free energy is a misnomer; we're not talking about zero-point stuff, really.
>
>
>
> ^Cites Pollack, Electric Universe folks, and others. Check all the citations. This isn't some new age quackery. This is science that has been discussed for over 100 years. He doesn't mention Stan Meyer if I'm not mistaken. Stan Myer discussed H2 and had a hunch water and H2 could power vehicles. He then created a buggie running off of these sources. He then mysteriously died. 17 years later, guess what is coming out? Cars running on the same things, lol. All this stuff is available for us to create at home, all it requires is know how. Anyone can teach themselves electronics, physics, etc. I started from being 100% disabled to now, as I said, attempting to attain a PsyD while studying medical physics on the side; but.... like 50% of everything I was "taught" I already learned from DIY stuff, pubmed, purchasing textbooks and reading them while disabled, etc. What I'm getting at is, this stuff is out there. If people want to remain blind, that's up to them, but I've been extremely enlightened by Dr. Kruse, but him not even knowing about these other researchers is, well, beyond disheartening. I don't think one should discuss Ling and Pollack without reading their work first. Ling got his ideas from Budenborg de Jong, and Budenborg de Jong's work is related to AI Oparin's on the origin of life which directly connects to Russellian science, although the two men never met or have referenced each other or anything. This then all really elucidates some of the EU stuff some people post here. It's all connected.

Nobody here disputes the Frank-Starling Law's validity, but many bodybuilders actually try to circumvent it. If you understood what I wrote above you'd get that. And I really don't need to be tutored in medical physiology by you, thank you.

Everything is connected. And all of the thinkers you mentioned had errors contained in their work and assumptions. Ling and Becker, et al, included. None of them got it totally right. Nobody!

The trick in making progress today is to interconnect the true and relevant parts from previous thinkers and to reject the old errors, through a prioritized and rational process. Your publicly throwing slings at Jack for not "knowing everything in the history of the world and caring who influenced who from the beginning of time" are less than productive. No one knows everything. Fortunately, you don't need-to to make progress. Knowledge is always a work in progress and one has to prioritize. If you actually understood rational epistemology, you might realize that.

In any event, your barking out criticisms and holding Jack up to some standard of omniscience is just being an ungrateful jerk, in my book. No one is stopping you from making your own observations and contributions here, or anywhere else, for that matter. But it sure is a lot easier to just berate others for not being what you want them to be, isn't it?

### CindyB — 2015-03-12T18:48:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158375>

Argh. Typed up a response but lost it attempting to post it. More transpired so will try again...

Thanks, Yew, for the deeper info about what "hypertrophies" when we speak of "muscle hypertrophy". So, more calcium stores, more glucagon stores, more spare parts storage and more waste product lymphatic transport shuttles in the "hypertrophy" spaces.

I lift as heavy as I can to achieve my goals for the C&J and snatch. Each lifting session is 3-5 reps working my way up in weights until I don't recover my breathing rate (BR) within about a minute. If BR doesn't recover, drop reps. If it seems too easy, increase weights. If my total stressors are too great, my BR is affected so I back off reps or weights. I lift for the stress relief. It requires my entire focus and leaves my mind mellow. I also lift to fight sarcopenia. Since sarcopenia starts at about age 30, accelerates at about age 75 and is the "norm" for those over 85, it seems that exploring the mechanisms and issues associated with sarcopenia would be more productive than discussing body building levels of muscle hypertrophy.

One of many articles on sarcopenia: [http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/91/4/1123S.full](http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/91/4/1123S.full)

> Cachexia is a metabolic condition that is always associated with an underlying illness and inflammation. It is characterized by loss of skeletal muscle and body weight. Nutritional interventions have shown limited success in preserving fat but not muscle mass. Sarcopenia is the age-associated loss of skeletal muscle and function. Sarcopenia is a life-long process with a complex and multifactorial etiology. The effects of inactivity, and in particular bedrest, may mimic those of cachexia. However, there are important metabolic distinctions with significant nutritional implications.

Since it happens to most everyone and since body building hypertrophy is \_relatively\_ rare, the ubiquitin marking of proteins/cells (both? independently?) is probably a more relevant mechanism to consider. Thanks for discussing the muscle fiber recovery mechanism here: [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...uitination-6-is-live.13196/page-6#post-158268](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/page-6#post-158268) , Yew. Does that mechanism only apply to cachexia or also to sarcopenia and/or lack of physical activity? The above paper suggests there are different mechanisms involved.

Muscle wasting is common. It's called sarcopenia. If you're over 30, you've got it. Deliberately doing physically challenging activities to stimulate helpful muscle fiber changes to maintain muscle mass is necessary to optimal health. Sarcopenia existed before the advent of nnEMF. Protein (w/ probably a bit more luecine and maybe some extra glycine) plus resistance training improves sarcopenia. These seem to put spare parts in the right areas to be used if autophagy occurs properly. The physical motions of resistance training also pumps the lymphatic system which helps move waste products (and autophagied bits) out so spare parts can move in. The entropy of the system resists change unless some bits are moved out of the way. If autophagy doesn't proceed properly, the spare parts can accumulate taking room that would prevent the ubiquitin marked bits from having a place to go.

There is also some phenomenon that happens when you train muscles that carries beyond the specific muscles trained. A well known phenomenon in training is the carryover of training an un-injured limb to the injured limb. Something about moving muscles in one part of the body affects other muscles in a positive way.

As with most things, muscle training and protein consumption probably operates on a U-shaped benefits curve: too little is bad, too much is bad, find where you like to operate in the plateau region.

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-03-12T22:01:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158387>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=158052)
>
> I personally have a hunch that having larger muscles macroscopically actually scale to increased microscopic distances between cells/fibers, which will in turn reduce the relative amount of EZ in skeletal muscles, and decrease their energetic efficiency.
>
>
> Study on muscle fibers in the vastus medialis of trained males -- [http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1113/jphysiol.2005.101642/full](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1113/jphysiol.2005.101642/full)
>
> That study basically echoes the same sort of findings that we see in all Heavy Weight Training studies. Heavy resistance exercise basically causes hypertrophy of primary Type 2 "fast-twitch" fibers. Type 2A fibers have lower mitochondrial and capillary density than Type 1 "slow-twitch" fibers, and Type 2X fibers have basically no mitochondria, and are dependent on glycolytic fuelling.
>
> On top of that, most studies show an adaptation to convert Type 1 fibers to Type 2 fibers in response to heavy training.
>
> On top of that, almost all studies will show that the increase in muscle volume is due to sarcoplasmic hypertrophy -- more fluid in the muscle, which increases the mean distance of any water molecule to a hydrophilic protein (not a good thing for energy production).
>
> Under almost any circumstance, **you're not going to be able to add more muscle fibers**. Bigger muscles are a result of either increased muscle fiber size, or more likely, increased fluid retention in the muscle. To me, this does not sound like improved energetic efficiency.
>
> \----
>
> Appropriate use of endurance activity actually seems to bode well -- [http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/232/6/H705.short](http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/232/6/H705.short)
>
> **quote**: "The capillaries per fiber ratios were 1.77+/-0.10 and 2.49+/-0.08 (mean+/-SE) in the UT and ET groups, respectively. The numbers of capillaries around each fiber were 4.43+/-0.19 (UT) and 5.87+/-0.18 (ET). The numbers of capillaries per mm2 were 585+/-40 (UT) and 821+/-28 (ET)"
>
> "ET' stands for "Endurance Trained", and "UT" stands for "Untrained".
>
> More capillaries mean more water trapped in small spaces => more EZ water and more energy delivery potential.
>
> HIIT, which should not be confused with heavy resistance training, produces at least the adaptation for increased mitochondrial density -- [http://ajpregu.physiology.org/content/295/1/R264.short](http://ajpregu.physiology.org/content/295/1/R264.short)
> ​
>
>
> Now if this is true (that smaller muscles are more tightly packed muscles), how would a person with less muscle mass and a tighter collagen network be affected by that same 14-hour plane flight? My guess is that the dehydrating effects are less so due to the average decreased distance between water molecules and hydrophilic proteins.
>
> \----
>
> DHA is not a producer of energy. It's role is to turn light signals into electricity and vice versa. DHA in the skin is useful because it can take sunlight and turn it into electric charge to drive chemical processes (especially sulfation of Vit D).
>
> As far as we're concerned the only type of energy that can be emitted by mitochondria within the muscles is IR light (assuming that those are slow twitch fibers that actually contain DHA). There is free radical production, but those are signalling agents, not an energy source.
>
> So for muscles to become a net producer of energy, they need to have the heat produced by the muscle structure water and leads to better collagen tensegrity, this is dependent on how tight your collagen networks are, not how much DHA there is in individual muscle cells.
>
> I don't think any tissue can ever become a net producer of energy. Animals are fundamentally disconnected from the earth, and need both proton and electron currents to build EZ water to act as the battery of life.
>
> ....

In light of your amazing summary/thoughts above, I wonder what we can say about this (I'm not certain I agree with everything, but some good points are arisen):
[https://pranarupa.wordpress.com/201...mitochondria-and-metamorphic-mutation-magick/](https://pranarupa.wordpress.com/2015/03/12/muscle-metabolism-mitochondria-and-metamorphic-mutation-magick/) **Muscle, metabolism, mitochondria, and metamorphic mutation Magick**

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-13T02:28:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158400>

> [CindyB said:](/goto/post?id=158375)
>
> Argh. Typed up a response but lost it attempting to post it. More transpired so will try again...
>
> Thanks, Yew, for the deeper info about what "hypertrophies" when we speak of "muscle hypertrophy". So, more calcium stores, more glucagon stores, more spare parts storage and more waste product lymphatic transport shuttles in the "hypertrophy" spaces.
> .

OK, I'll try to break things down. Bear in mind that my reading on sarcopenia and muscle wasting is limited, and it was just a casual exploration during my bodybuilding obsessed days  (had 6 years of that, no more )

\----
**Olympic Lifts
**
I tend to think that if these are performed with good technique, they are great exercises that can be done very regularly, with the following caveats:

\- No eccentric stress -- full snatches and jerks, not too much work from the hang, not too much strenous accessory work.
\- If you keep to sub-maximal weights, then it's always going to be more nervous system activation rather than metabolic fatigue
\- I like your method of using Breathing Rate to measure metabolic fatigue. I should encourage more people to experiment with that
\- I would rather see work in the 1-3 rep range as opposed to 3-5 reps with movements as technical as the oly lifts

Anecdotally, the old soviet block lifters could basically lift every day to what they call a daily maximum, which is always characterised by a lack of any "psyche up". ie: Nervous system activation is always kept to voluntary baseline levels, and never forced to increased. You can get more nervous system activation, and thus more force production, by using loud music, aggressive self talk, jumping around, etc .... but the daily maximum attempt must be attempted in a calm and focused fashion, which is what you're doing anyway

\----
**Sarcopenia
**
This definitely starts to occur as we age, and it seems inevitable. However, I personally feel that the regular definitions are too sketchy.

For me personally, I define this as only one thing: **a shift in muscle fiber composition to contain more Type 1 rather Type 2 fibers.**

By my definition, this muddies the waters for so-called observed sarcopenia -- a loss in volume or cross-sectional area of the muscle is to be expected by the shift in fiber composition, but **this may not be a bad thing if the number of fibers remain relatively constant.** (ie: you just have conversion of Type 2 to Type 1 fibers)

First, I still think that the method of diagnosis for sarcopenia is clear: You measure your relative fiber compositions over time and watch for shifts in fiber type. Of course, this is not practical for most people, but it should give a reliable diagnosis.

Now, Type 2 fibers generally "have more growth potential" when stimulated. This seems to pan out in the observations, but TBH, I'm not sure enough to make any major conclusions. There could be a contribution from glycogen storage, and increased fiber thickness may or may not correlate to good outcomes. In any case, I still think that fiber packing density is the key to energy efficiency.

Type 2 fibers contain more glycogen than Type 1 fibers. Every gram of muscle glycogen stores with it 4grams of water, which means that lost of muscle glycogen storage capacity will lead to a huge decrease in muscle volume and cross-sectional area. However, if the number of fibers remain constant, then the fact that Type 1 fibers have as much contractile force as Type 2 fibers on a per-fiber basis, means that there could be no loss of strength at all.

From the Quantum Health perspective, there is even a plausible argument to be made that predominance of Type 1 fibers at the expense of Type 2 fibers may even be better for strength and efficiency. Type 1 fibers contain lots of mitochondria, and and still remain very closely packed together for all the crosslinking that you speak of.

Anecdotally, older weightlifters don't carb-load as well, probably a sign of decreased Type 2 fiber count.

Also, older endurance athletes tend to do better than younger ones, and it is plausible that having more "fat burning" Type 1 fibers are beneficial for endurance activity (they contain lots of capillary supply and are rich with mitochondria).

And you know what, maybe "old man strength" is real after all  -- the muscles may look stringy and flat, but it could just be a whole bunch of tightly packed Type 1 fibers, which may not have the voluminous "pump" from the glycogen stores, but can still pack a lot of contractile power (deceptively so for their apparent size).

\----
**So what to do then?
**
I'll say first up that I don't have specific recommendations . If you believe my interpretation of sarcopenia above, then it's not even harmful so long as you maintain the tension stimulus on the muscles.

A consistent tension stimulus is doable even just using your bodyweight (and a set of gymnastics rings .... or a tree for climbing). I think exercise regimes like yours will help to preserve fiber count, which to me, is the main parameter when it comes to energetic efficiency of the muscles to achieve high performance together with longevity.

What you lose with conversion of Type 2 to Type 1 fibers is the ability to sustain longer bouts of glycolytic activity like 400m sprinting, cage fighting, sprint canoe races, track bike races, etc .....

Sidenote: No, I'm not saying that HIIT is bad as you age. But perhaps now that you're older, you only need 5 interval repeats instead of 10 repeats to get the same mitochondrial biogenesis stimulus ​

Again, if you believe my premise, then you have to ask the question: Is it wise to continue training like a Type 2 dominant athlete as muscle fiber typing changes with age?

My bias, especially after reading Ubiquination 6, is that this more Type 1 dominant muscle becomes even more energy efficient, and needs lower ubiquination rates to maintain at a high level. This means less dietary protein intake as you age, and more tension stimulus as opposed to metabolic stimulus to the muscles during exercise.

Sidenote: I've been using the terms "metabolic" and "tension" stimulus.

Tension literally means mechanical tension on the muscle fibers, and generally you want this very high during "heavy lifting".

If the heavy lifting goes on for multiple reps, the muscles will start to demand extra fuel to maintain the same amount of tension. This "getting better at getting and using extra fuel" is what I mean by a metabolic stimulus.​

Systemic ubiquination rates are preferable to be lower as you age, and IMO the exercise stimulus should reflect that.

\----

Finally, I want to re-emphasise this point by Cindy:

Each lifting session is 3-5 reps working my way up in weights **until I don't recover my breathing rate (BR) within about a minute**. If BR doesn't recover, drop reps. If it seems too easy, increase weights. If my total stressors are too great, my BR is affected so I back off reps or weights. I lift for the stress relief. It requires my entire focus and leaves my mind mellow​

I think this is an **excellent**  way to regulate metabolic stimulus. The exact time to recover will have to be tweaked per the individual, but I think that this simple practice will help people biohack their exercise bouts a lot better

....

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-13T03:06:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158401>

> [bio-fractal-soul-self said:](/goto/post?id=158387)
>
> In light of your amazing summary/thoughts above, I wonder what we can say about this (I'm not certain I agree with everything, but some good points are arisen):
> [https://pranarupa.wordpress.com/201...mitochondria-and-metamorphic-mutation-magick/](https://pranarupa.wordpress.com/2015/03/12/muscle-metabolism-mitochondria-and-metamorphic-mutation-magick/) **Muscle, metabolism, mitochondria, and metamorphic mutation Magick**

I only gave it a brief skim, and I think I agree with all the mechanistic discussion, and only disagree with some of the way that they go about things.

An example is that they give a really great explanation of the role of the diaphragm, but I think some of the traditional yoga poses are horrible at training the diaphragm the right way

I got schooled a little in this regard during my recent catch-up with @[Josh (Paleo Osteo)](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9815/) . I did not know how to instinctively activate the diaphragm in conjunction with the pelvic floor to create good intra-abdominal pressure. I was thought in many martial arts to assume a hollow abdominal position, and some of the yoga poses encourage this too. Yes, this may target the diaphragm, but it's not doing so with a stable core position where good intra-abdominal pressure is maintained.

Anyway, my quibble is just in regards to the practical implementation, not the principles which the linked site provides. I don't even think that I have that many quibbles at all, and on the whole, a yoga practice is probably going to lead to more good than harm.

Sidenote: I do disagree with some of the yoga stretches though. Example: the Padmasana (lotus position) can put excessive knee torque on most people, and many of the back hyper-extension exercises don't seem that good. Anything that causes excessive tendon stretching is not a good thing in my opinion.​

As for actual practical recommendations, that's why you go see a doctor . I'm still personally going through Pavel Kolar's Dynamic Neuromuscular Stabilisation textbook, and I'm optimistic that some practical strategies will emerge once I've assimilated the concepts -- [http://www.rehabps.com/REHABILITATION/Home.html](http://www.rehabps.com/REHABILITATION/Home.html)

....

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=158374)

> Nobody here disputes the Frank-Starling Law's validity, but many bodybuilders actually try to circumvent it. If you understood what I wrote above you'd get that. And I really don't need to be tutored in medical physiology by you, thank you.

>
> The trick in making progress today is to interconnect the true and relevant parts from previous thinkers and to reject the old errors, through a prioritized and rational process. Your publicly throwing slings at Jack for not "knowing everything in the history of the world and caring who influenced who from the beginning of time" are less than productive. No one knows everything. Fortunately, you don't need-to to make progress. Knowledge is always a work in progress and one has to prioritize. ### CindyB — 2015-03-13T21:55:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158471>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=158400)
>
> OK, I'll try to break things down. Bear in mind that my reading on sarcopenia and muscle wasting is limited, and it was just a casual exploration during my bodybuilding obsessed days  (had 6 years of that, no more )
>
> \----
> **Olympic Lifts
> **
> I tend to think that if these are performed with good technique, they are great exercises that can be done very regularly, with the following caveats:
>
> \- No eccentric stress -- full snatches and jerks, not too much work from the hang, not too much strenous accessory work.
> \- If you keep to sub-maximal weights, then it's always going to be more nervous system activation rather than metabolic fatigue
> \- I like your method of using Breathing Rate to measure metabolic fatigue. I should encourage more people to experiment with that
> \- I would rather see work in the 1-3 rep range as opposed to 3-5 reps with movements as technical as the oly lifts
>
> Anecdotally, the old soviet block lifters could basically lift every day to what they call a daily maximum, which is always characterised by a lack of any "psyche up". ie: Nervous system activation is always kept to voluntary baseline levels, and never forced to increased. You can get more nervous system activation, and thus more force production, by using loud music, aggressive self talk, jumping around, etc .... but the daily maximum attempt must be attempted in a calm and focused fashion, which is what you're doing anyway
>
> \----
> **Sarcopenia
> **
> This definitely starts to occur as we age, and it seems inevitable. However, I personally feel that the regular definitions are too sketchy.
>
> For me personally, I define this as only one thing: **a shift in muscle fiber composition to contain more Type 1 rather Type 2 fibers.**
>
> By my definition, this muddies the waters for so-called observed sarcopenia -- a loss in volume or cross-sectional area of the muscle is to be expected by the shift in fiber composition, but **this may not be a bad thing if the number of fibers remain relatively constant.** (ie: you just have conversion of Type 2 to Type 1 fibers)
>
> First, I still think that the method of diagnosis for sarcopenia is clear: You measure your relative fiber compositions over time and watch for shifts in fiber type. Of course, this is not practical for most people, but it should give a reliable diagnosis.
>
> Now, Type 2 fibers generally "have more growth potential" when stimulated. This seems to pan out in the observations, but TBH, I'm not sure enough to make any major conclusions. There could be a contribution from glycogen storage, and increased fiber thickness may or may not correlate to good outcomes. In any case, I still think that fiber packing density is the key to energy efficiency.
>
> Type 2 fibers contain more glycogen than Type 1 fibers. Every gram of muscle glycogen stores with it 4grams of water, which means that lost of muscle glycogen storage capacity will lead to a huge decrease in muscle volume and cross-sectional area. However, if the number of fibers remain constant, then the fact that Type 1 fibers have as much contractile force as Type 2 fibers on a per-fiber basis, means that there could be no loss of strength at all.
>
> From the Quantum Health perspective, there is even a plausible argument to be made that predominance of Type 1 fibers at the expense of Type 2 fibers may even be better for strength and efficiency. Type 1 fibers contain lots of mitochondria, and and still remain very closely packed together for all the crosslinking that you speak of.
>
> Anecdotally, older weightlifters don't carb-load as well, probably a sign of decreased Type 2 fiber count.
>
> Also, older endurance athletes tend to do better than younger ones, and it is plausible that having more "fat burning" Type 1 fibers are beneficial for endurance activity (they contain lots of capillary supply and are rich with mitochondria).
>
> And you know what, maybe "old man strength" is real after all  -- the muscles may look stringy and flat, but it could just be a whole bunch of tightly packed Type 1 fibers, which may not have the voluminous "pump" from the glycogen stores, but can still pack a lot of contractile power (deceptively so for their apparent size).
>
> \----
> **So what to do then?
> **
> I'll say first up that I don't have specific recommendations . If you believe my interpretation of sarcopenia above, then it's not even harmful so long as you maintain the tension stimulus on the muscles.
>
> A consistent tension stimulus is doable even just using your bodyweight (and a set of gymnastics rings .... or a tree for climbing). I think exercise regimes like yours will help to preserve fiber count, which to me, is the main parameter when it comes to energetic efficiency of the muscles to achieve high performance together with longevity.
>
> What you lose with conversion of Type 2 to Type 1 fibers is the ability to sustain longer bouts of glycolytic activity like 400m sprinting, cage fighting, sprint canoe races, track bike races, etc .....
>
>
> Sidenote: No, I'm not saying that HIIT is bad as you age. But perhaps now that you're older, you only need 5 interval repeats instead of 10 repeats to get the same mitochondrial biogenesis stimulus ​
>
>
> Again, if you believe my premise, then you have to ask the question: Is it wise to continue training like a Type 2 dominant athlete as muscle fiber typing changes with age?
>
> My bias, especially after reading Ubiquination 6, is that this more Type 1 dominant muscle becomes even more energy efficient, and needs lower ubiquination rates to maintain at a high level. This means less dietary protein intake as you age, and more tension stimulus as opposed to metabolic stimulus to the muscles during exercise.
>
>
> Sidenote: I've been using the terms "metabolic" and "tension" stimulus.
>
> Tension literally means mechanical tension on the muscle fibers, and generally you want this very high during "heavy lifting".
>
> If the heavy lifting goes on for multiple reps, the muscles will start to demand extra fuel to maintain the same amount of tension. This "getting better at getting and using extra fuel" is what I mean by a metabolic stimulus.​
>
>
>
> Systemic ubiquination rates are preferable to be lower as you age, and IMO the exercise stimulus should reflect that.
>
> \----
>
> Finally, I want to re-emphasise this point by Cindy:
>
>
> Each lifting session is 3-5 reps working my way up in weights **until I don't recover my breathing rate (BR) within about a minute**. If BR doesn't recover, drop reps. If it seems too easy, increase weights. If my total stressors are too great, my BR is affected so I back off reps or weights. I lift for the stress relief. It requires my entire focus and leaves my mind mellow​
>
>
>
> I think this is an **excellent**  way to regulate metabolic stimulus. The exact time to recover will have to be tweaked per the individual, but I think that this simple practice will help people biohack their exercise bouts a lot better
>
> ....

"With aging, multiple processes occurring within muscle tissue, such as denervation, changes in the hormonal and inflammatory environment, mitochondrial dysfunction, and changes in the expression of regulatory factors affecting the fate of satellite cells, combine to produce losses in the bulk properties of muscle tissue such as muscle mass and strength."

From: [http://tinyurl.com/qj4lfu3](http://tinyurl.com/qj4lfu3)

I'd like to fork this discussion here:
"This definitely starts to occur as we age, and it seems inevitable. However, I personally feel that the regular definitions are too sketchy.

For me personally, I define this as only one thing: a shift in muscle fiber composition to contain more Type 1 rather Type 2 fibers."

I think denervation is a significant element of the definition for sarcopenia. A close 3rd would be loss of function. The 3 can be seen in a feedback loop: as denervation proceeds, the ability to move or activate muscle fibers decrease, as the ability to move muscle fibers decrease, the size of muscle fibers decreases (w/ apparent preference for one type of muscle fiber over the other-- see paper for details) which contributes to denervation... etc. The protein blend (bump up certain fractions especially leucine) along with training stimulus (pick what turns your turbine or otherwise is easier to do than not do) along with intention for enervation and one would be addressing the top 3 elements of sarcopenia. The interesting thing about intention is the involvement of the mind/nervous system with acting on muscle fibers. I visualize, for example, reaching toward my muscles or feeling the ground contacting the whole of the bottom of my foot while lifting. Intent. One JK description in particular is part of my visualization process these days: signal conduction occurring as a result of plasma shifting of electron clouds along the nerve fibers. Does popping (Gotthaus mechanism?) the end piece of the plasma shift into the muscle fiber provide a signaling mechanism in the ubiquitin marking system? Does the "ubiquitin-proteosome pathway" (cf above paper) get its coordinating to the next several levels of order-making from this QED plasma shift? Does my intention "observe" the probability function and physically perform signal transduction through QED?

There are definitely anecdotal reports of the mental aspects of training and their influence on performance and muscle growth. Is this where "with a thought you can change your self" comes in?

### Josh — 2015-03-13T23:09:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158475>

> [bio-fractal-soul-self said:](/goto/post?id=158327)
>
> Okay, read through this. Don't have much to add. I have to still get some studying in, then sleep  I tend to agree with what you state above, especially with YOUR interpretation of the way we can/should apply "mass equivalence". My point in a more simplistic manner was more so that adding a realistic, "natural" amount of muscle will not, in terms of any evidence - direct, indirect, Ling, Pollack, physics, etc - detract from brain power, especially when looking into the epigenetics of muscle signalling, myokines, and more. Brain size isn't 100% tied to intelligence anyhow.
>
> I will, as I said, make a separate thread or post in my journal about some of the things I linked above. For anyone who doesn't believe me, just search up John Searl, Albert Roy Davis, Walter C. Rawls, DR. Hongsheng Zhao, or any of the others I will post about soon. I will be utilizing a lot of their work in physics, geobiology, and more, in my own home and land soon (building biodomes, completely self sustaining gardens, etc) and will updated about it a lot. There are also sites where you can link up with open soure projects utilizing all this information on water and electromagnetism. For instance, this page (which also has a youtube page): [http://rwgresearch.com/](http://rwgresearch.com/)
>
>
>
> There's some really nifty stuff we can do with the things contained in the rwg research page

The challenge with the group you refer to is that the gradient from what is available is way too steep and has too many blanks to fill in. As I see it, the main goal here is to apply as much as possible to the current and soon to emerge practical application to human health. I have plenty of friends playing with all of the things you mention including the person who just showed up for tea who lives somewhere in the boonies with a huge shop and stays up late reading and scratching his head over Russell, Keely, Pound, Schauberger, etc.. I have other friends out in the woods too...None of them have much practical to say about how to address a given health issue or how to d/dx it. This is a relative exercise with acknowledgement of the ideal scene as part of it....gradient is everything in application....

To play with analogies a little more, my friends and I were discussing a piece of machinery that another friend had attempted to engineer. The customer, also a friend, has now asked the friend drinking tea with me to fix the other person's design. We laughed about the thankless task of repairing and modifying a flawed design and how typically "the last one who touched it owns it...". Well in modern healthcare and management, every body that a practitioner sees or a person has is more or less in the same category as that machine. It would be nice to just build bodies and healthcare from the ground up with a better design, but we are stuck in the middle of multiple overlapping cycles and interests that must be navigated to simultaneously clear old dogmas and beliefs while simultaneously building new paradigms and applying them in vivo. This is much more complicated than building free energy machines from the ground up. In theory, all bodies are "free energy machines" and they are all broken and miss-engineered at this point driving down broken pot-holed roads. To point to the design and R+D departments when one needs the engine rebuilt and body repaired is not helpful. It is the rare design engineer who can repair and re-engineer his own, let alone other people's designs ongoing...

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-03-14T01:58:27+0200

My ramblings on all that was more so due to the interconnectedness of all this stuff. I like complexity, so for me, this is a non-issue. For instance, I hypothesize I can (almost for free) power my home in the next year or two, while working on a self-sustaining biodome built into the ground (how's that for magnetism? haha); actually, this is an idea I wanted to post about here - there are people building homes in or under the ground for $50-5000, I'd imagine that'd be a great environment hack lol.

I think that we can apply all their research and make it more concise and readable for lay people, it just takes others to do the work and synthesize it in a fashion that Dr. Kruse has with Becker, Ling, Delecea (is that the name?), etc. Jason Verbelli is doing this rather well, even going to physics, biophysics, etc. conferences and lecturing people, despite having no classical training and education in this stuff.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-14T03:43:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158485>

> [CindyB said:](/goto/post?id=158471)
>
> I think denervation is a significant element of the definition for sarcopenia. A close 3rd would be loss of function. The 3 can be seen in a feedback loop: as denervation proceeds, the ability to move or activate muscle fibers decrease, as the ability to move muscle fibers decrease, the size of muscle fibers decreases (w/ apparent preference for one type of muscle fiber over the other-- see paper for details) which contributes to denervation... etc. The protein blend (bump up certain fractions especially leucine) along with training stimulus (pick what turns your turbine or otherwise is easier to do than not do) along with intention for enervation and one would be addressing the top 3 elements of sarcopenia. The interesting thing about intention is the involvement of the mind/nervous system with acting on muscle fibers. I visualize, for example, reaching toward my muscles or feeling the ground contacting the whole of the bottom of my foot while lifting. Intent. One JK description in particular is part of my visualization process these days: signal conduction occurring as a result of plasma shifting of electron clouds along the nerve fibers. Does popping (Gotthaus mechanism?) the end piece of the plasma shift into the muscle fiber provide a signaling mechanism in the ubiquitin marking system? Does the "ubiquitin-proteosome pathway" (cf above paper) get its coordinating to the next several levels of order-making from this QED plasma shift? Does my intention "observe" the probability function and physically perform signal transduction through QED?

The "multiple factors characterise" sarcopenia part is what I don't like , because that means researchers use the word "sarcopenia" to mean a lot of different things.

So let's:

**(1)** Pull apart specific mechanisms of the disease state, instead of labelling the whole thing as "sarcopenia", because this definition is too unspecific given the Quantum Health perspective

**(2)** always bear in mind that the ultimate goal is to preserve the ability to use the muscles for functional movement. eg: climb a tree with good core stability

Let's define "loss of function" as any of:

\- the inability for a muscle contractile units to fire strongly enough
\- the inability to send the correct nervous impulse to a muscle

Which leads to being able to being unable to move in the way that the person "wants to".

Overall muscle size is a derived side effect; You have to discuss specific mechanisms as to why muscle size is increased or decreased, and those mechanisms dictate whether or not the person is able to get more "real-life muscle function" as a result.

\----

Denervation is a big problem, and I think it is the first problem in a line of cascading issues that lead to ability to lose muscle function.

I think that this is independent of fiber type changes.

I also think that the mechanisms that lead to functionally relevant denervation are independent of fiber type changes and muscle size.

First, we should talk about the actual mechanism, which is dysfunction of the motor unit, specifically the axons and the axon terminals. These are the necessary components to signal to a muscle for contraction (and relaxation ) to occur.

....

We should now ask: **How would a motor neuron fail?**

In my mind, it's either:

**(a)** De-myelination of the axon => inability to transmit signal from the motor neuron to the muscle (all the blogs talking about de-myelination apply here . Especially the link between nn-EMF, iodine, and ketosis in EE7)

**(b)** Malfunction at the axon terminal (see image below)

These do not seem like inevitable malfunctioning to me . It just seems like another standard failure cascade when you put age-related loss of redox potential together with a lot environmental stress. Studies that do not take into account environmental stress will give an incomplete picture ....

\-----

Now, the ultimate test of nervous impulse is "signalling sensitivity" from the nervous system to the muscles.

This is hard to measure, because the impulses are so quick, and they are electro-chemical in nature. I think if you had a something like a continuous imaging MRI over a muscle, you could actually see how much impulse is attempted to be stimulated, and then how much resulted effect in the motor unit you see.

NOTE: Just like you can get Leptin Resistance, you can also get nervous signalling resistance, and IMO it all comes back down to de-myelination of the nervous system.​

Most of the studies regarding sarcopenia will take a snapshot of the muscle, and then see if it "looks like it is denervated". This usually correlates well with actual lose of nervous system signalling sensitivity, but we need to take a look at the specific measure.

Some diseases like ALS that show definite loss of motor neurons. This is definitely a sign of things going wrong with denervation.

There are studies which look at this sort of motor neuron loss -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0531556513000260](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0531556513000260)

**My opinion**: This sort of "sarcopenia" is a spectrum disorder between healthy and ALS states. I think everything in the MS blog applies here, and the same broad disease treatment protocol should be put into play -- [http://jackkruse.com/organizational-structural-failure-10-multiple-sclerosis/](http://jackkruse.com/organizational-structural-failure-10-multiple-sclerosis/)

Back to studies, this is a rat study, but also shows how you can have the same amount of signalling with fewer neurons, due to greater axon branching increasing the innervation ratio -- [http://biomedgerontology.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2013/09/27/gerona.glt135.short](http://biomedgerontology.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2013/09/27/gerona.glt135.short)

The innervation ratio is basically a measure of how many muscle fibers a single motor axons signals to.​

There is huge variation between muscle groups, ranging from 200 in the deltoids (shoulder), to up to 2,000 in the gastrocnemius (calves).​

Cites for in situ studies of actual human muscle:
\- [http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1748-1716.1959.tb01678.x/abstract](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1748-1716.1959.tb01678.x/abstract)
\- [http://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF00238380](http://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF00238380)
​

**Out on a limb alert (logical speculations)**: Could it be that Type 2 fibers need a greater innervation ratio than Type 1 fibers to function properly? (since Type 2 fibers don't have magnetism-generating mitochondria)

If this were true (and I'm saying that it is, but let's ask a plausible "what if" question here) => then decreased innervation ratio is completely expected with a shift in fiber type from Type 2 to Type 1 fibers. This would then mean that "apparent denervation" due to fiber type changes are not harmful.

\----
**Treatment**

At the end of the day, it's still coming back to lack of nervous system functionality as the primary issue with actual loss of muscle function. This just sounds like the same old age-related loss of myelination that Jack has been harping on for years now.

The usual protocols that Jack recommends to restore nervous system health is a pre-requisite here IMO, waaaay ahead of trying to use exercise or diet to fix these issues. Again it comes back to light and circadian cycles .

Note: Josh Ferber and Jack have discussed before how you can "short circuit" the system with exercise if signalling is not right. The March Q&A went a little bit into how TENS units can harm or hurt depending on context. This is why you need to be your own doctor, and also hopefully have a real doctor there to help along with a highly personal-context-specific treatment protocol.​

However, assuming healthy nervous system signalling to muscles, then I think the key is to fire those nervous pathways frequently and strongly. This means "heavy lifting" (measured by percent of 1RM, which is the best proxy for fiber recruitment), in all planes of the body, without metabolic fatigue. @[Danco3636](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1687/) is the best example on these forums of someone who is older, and who is doing well with exercise  (but he's a male, and the female context will differ ....)

\----

Thoughts manifest as nervous impulses. So yes, I do think your thoughts can affect the fate of your muscles

\----

As an end note, I have speculations on why would the body prefer Type 1 fibers over Type 2 as it ages, and how that ties into Ubiquination. I think I need more time to think

....

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-03-14T03:58:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158486>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=158485)
>
> Thoughts manifest as nervous impulses. So yes, I do think your thoughts can affect the fate of your muscles
> ....

[http://www.jonathanfields.com/brain-buff-research-thoughts-on-strength-fitness-weight-loss/](http://www.jonathanfields.com/brain-buff-research-thoughts-on-strength-fitness-weight-loss/)

"Building muscle, it turns out, is not nearly as mechanical as we thought. And, in fact, a recent study by Erin M. Shackell and Lionel G. Standing at Bishop’s University reveals you may be able to make nearly identical gains in strength and fitness without lifting a finger!

That study measured the strength gains in three different groups of people. The first group did nothing outside their usual routine. The second group was put through two weeks of highly focused strength training for one specific muscle, three times a week. The third group listened to audio CDs that guided them to imagine themselves going through the same workout as the exercising group, three times a week.

And, the results blew everyone away.

The control group, who didn’t do anything, saw no gains in strength. The exercise group, who trained three times a week, saw a 28% gain in strength. No big surprises there. But, **the group who did not exercise, but rather thought about exercising experienced nearly the same gains in strength as the exercise group (24%).** Yes, you read that right!"

I discussed this with Ray Peat before, and he stated that he's done this to a lesser extent and quantified it. Really interesting stuff. I have seen studies in the past indicating that simply viewing your muscles activate while doing an exercise causes increases in muscle activation and muscle protein synthesis, though the studies were too short in length to show any objective measurements on long term strength/muscle gains. Pairing this all with the images you shared above, it would make sense how nerve impulses directly impact muscles, and if one does this appropriately, they can potentially even aid in muscle strength and all that.

### sjoshua — 2015-03-16T23:41:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupling-light-uncouples-your-knowledge-ubiquitination-6-is-live.13196/post-158693>

> [CTforlife said:](/goto/post?id=158692)

>

So activating autophagy to change the size and shape of muscle tensgrity is hormetic, but proteins in anything but muscles changing shape and size isn't ? I really need you to clarify and stand by your statement. I really can't believe you posted that. Remember everyone needs questioning. And a size and shape change invokes mass equivilance and not a "hormetic" change. Barry posted a great post in his thread about 24/7 carb eaters out performing people like us. I think this bs needs to stop. If you call exercise hormetic when it changes size and shape I can't listen to you. You calling exercise hormetic means you are saying cold is hormetic. I mean cold just changes size and shape, so let's just be dumb and assume it's hormetic too.
>
> Cold nor exercise is hormetic. On this scale.

I think it is important to consider the meaning of 'hormetic' here:

> **hormesis,**
> _n_ the phenomenon in which low doses of toxins produce stimulating effects.
>
> [http://medical-dictionary.thefreedictionary.com/Hormetic](http://medical-dictionary.thefreedictionary.com/Hormetic)

I'm not meaning to step on toes, and do not intend to speak for @[Josh (Paleo Osteo)](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9815/) , but when you consider that cold creates a more efficient environment for cellular function it is hard to consider it a 'toxin'. The responses our bodies/fleshpods produce when confronted with cold are programmed and universal - this is why everyone, no matter their stage in pursuit of 'optimal' health, can benefit from exposure to increasing levels of cold. It creates a better environment for cellular function regardless of the other influences in the environment.

I would not say the same for lifting weights, though, as putting everyone under a squat bar (or even squat machine, or other controlled form of exercise that may be more 'safe') will not produce universally positive results. Even walking would be contraindicated to some conditions.

For those closer to optimal, such as yourself (in my view of seeing your posts around and interest in these topics), it may be experienced that lifting weights is beneficial to you every time you do it. But, what if we took this one step further - What if you were chronically training lifting heavy weights without allowing your fleshpod to recover? Or, what if you even pursued it with moderation in mind, and only lifted very light weights, but decided to train 24/7, only breaking to eat and sleep? I think eventually you'd experience the 'negative' or 'toxic' side of lifting which would produce unwanted results and create a condition where you were sliding away from 'optimal'.

Cold, on the other hand, while pursued intelligently (i.e. not going beyond the fleshpod's current threshold of ability) could be practiced 24/7 with only increasingly positive results. I cannot think of any form of muscular workout/weight lifting type exercise routine that could be designed in such a way that it would ONLY ever provide positive stimulus, in ALL who practiced it, especially if embraced without pause.

this is achieved through cortical awareness, and dynamic neuromuscular stability, not building muscle fibres. it is also our innate patterning through development, and doesnt require a hormetic stimulus, but rather an organisational patterning via gravity and sensory inputs.

The body then uses the influenza virus in an attempt to innovate on-the-fly to solve this immune system deficiency.

The only way to fix the problem then would be to heal the immune system components. Introduction of something like extra red light through lasers seems to "eliminate the virus", but under the model I just discussed, the red light simply restores immune system function, and thus the body doesn't need to use viruses to help it (and doesn't produce any of the "culprit" viruses of its own).

# Thread: ongoing sinus congestion and what to do about it

### takenoadvice — 2015-03-06T17:06:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ongoing-sinus-congestion-and-what-to-do-about-it.13202/post-157638>

Hi. Not my particular problem. A person I know who happens to mostly follow what turns out to be a paleo diet (with exceptions such as gluten-free bread every other day, sometimes fast food ), is plagued by ongoing sinus congestion and migraines. Changes in weather can trigger a migraine. Mid-40s female, has had hot flashes.

Chiropractor had provided direction on diet, I think most significant change has been cessation of dairy, now only goat cheese. Other grains still being consumed occasionally are rice, not sure about corn.

I had found a forum elsewhere in which many women described very similar pattern in symptoms, all around the same age, headache attacks coming in similar way. There were suggestions to investigate supplementing progesterone. Also a suggestion to drop bread altogether. I've also suggested to her to consider daily light patterns and drop late night TV.

Any advice or insight?

Combined with external stress factors that aren't solvable. Not overweight, figure excellent. Mainly periodic congestion and pain on 2-week cycle. Occasional huge drops in energy, regardless of diet or cycle.

From the several responses above, let me touch on several things.

grains: if dropping grains helps (I'm going to include rice, wheat, oats, corn in that) with sinus condition, exactly why could that be?
circadian rhythm: has office with exterior daylight, usually uses low light or candles in the evening, excepting TV. I have been suggesting to get more daylight during the day, but could natural sun lamp in the office help also, and if so, exactly what kind? I am wondering about that for myself, as well.
melasma: OK, I think I had found the melasma blog. [http://jackkruse.com/hashimotos-and-melasma/](http://jackkruse.com/hashimotos-and-melasma/) I would not have thought it relevant, but now I see how it could be related. There is a great deal to process there. At the moment I am just going to suggest more regular use of coconut oil, and keep studying. Chiro wants her to get a blood panel, I will ask exactly what they are measuring.
mold: I will think about this more, but I don't know of any obvious periodic source of mold in this case. I looked at surviving mold website. I will ask her to get a mold test kit. I see that some folks are more susceptible than others.

### Penny — 2015-04-07T00:30:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ongoing-sinus-congestion-and-what-to-do-about-it.13202/post-160454>

Full Article on sinusitus:
[http://www.lef.org/Protocols/Respiratory/Sinusitis/Page-01?p=1](http://www.lef.org/Protocols/Respiratory/Sinusitis/Page-01?p=1)

Fun little snipets:

Xylitol nasal irrigation – Xylitol is a sugar alcohol that appears to enhance the body’s natural defense against bacterial pathogens. An animal model showed that administering xylitol solution simultaneously with a bacterial pathogen reduced the amount of bacteria detectable upon a later examination of the sinuses (Brown 2004). In one trial, 20 subjects with chronic rhinosinusitis were randomized and instructed to rinse their sinuses daily with either a xylitol or saline solution. Fifteen subjects completed the study. Nasal irrigation with the xylitol solution was associated with a significant improvement on a standardized assessment of sinusitis symptoms (Weissman 2011). Another trial designed to assess the effects of xylitol nasal irrigation compared to placebo in the treatment of chronic sinusitis is recruiting participants as of November 2012 (ClinicalTrials.gov NCT00924404).

**Life Extension Suggestions**

-   [Lactoferrin](http://www.lef.org/search/products.aspx?k=dptlactoferrin) (providing 95% of apolactoferrin \[285 mg\]): 300 mg daily
-   [Vitamin C](http://www.lef.org/search/products.aspx?k=dptvitaminc): 1000 – 5000 mg or more daily
-   [Zinc](http://www.lef.org/search/products.aspx?k=dptzinc): 30 mg daily
-   [N-acetyl cysteine](http://www.lef.org/search/products.aspx?k=dptnacetyl) (NAC): 600 – 1800 mg daily
-   [Vitamin E](http://www.lef.org/search/products.aspx?k=dptvitaminE): 400 IU daily with at least 200 mg gamma tocopherol
-   [Rosmarinic acid](http://www.lef.org/search/products.aspx?k=dptrosmarinic): 100 – 200 mg daily
-   [Bromelain](http://www.lef.org/search/products.aspx?k=dptbromelain): 500 – 2000 mg daily
-   [Black cumin seed oil](http://www.lef.org/search/products.aspx?k=dptblackcuminseedoil): 1000 mg daily
-   [Herbal combination formula](http://www.lef.org/search/products.aspx?k=dptSinusHerbalFormula) (gentian root, primula flower, elder flower, sorrel herb, and verbena herb): Per label instructions
-   Nasal wash containing xylitol: Per label instructions

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-04-07T05:15:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ongoing-sinus-congestion-and-what-to-do-about-it.13202/post-160472>

I think xylitol is also an antifungal. I've heard that a lot of times when a sinus infection is constantly treated with antibiotics it leaves no competition for the fungus (or yeast). The fungus can now take over. A vicious cycle starts.

### Penny — 2015-03-09T03:26:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sense-of-impending-doom.13217/post-157940>

I tell ya Sean - for me - magnesium solves the weirdest stuff - hiccups, magnesium citrate in a cup - gone in 2 minutes - heart missing a few beats - gone soon after magnesium powder dissolved in a cup - it's dehydration Sean because all this stuff happens to me when there is too much sugar (my lovefest with honey comes to mind) - coffee - which dehydrates me - or going without water for long periods of time because I didn't take a water bottle with me... You are in London - the armpit of the world when it comes to EMF - England has 25% more autism than we even have in the states... 1 in 25 children...

So, I would be taking Ubiquinol, carnitine, magnesium in the form of epsom salts, citrate, malate and mag oil - and drinking tons of ice cold R/O water - for you, electrolytes might be a fabulous idea - it seems to me you have hypocalcemia from the calcium exiting your cell membranes - so k2 and d would be a great idea: I found this and it seemed to match you:

**Get Serious**

Get serious about this radiation pollution stuff and you will quickly see the difference, most likely first in your energy levels and ability to concentrate.

Some people who are sensitive to electromagnetic radiation (EMR) may have low-normal ionized calcium levels, says plant physiologist and biochemist Andrew Goldsworthy, PhD. Goldsworthy is a scientific advisor to the European Space Agency and to several European charities concerned with the environment and electromagnetic fields.

**It Messes With Your Calcium**

Weak electromagnetic fields cause calcium ions to detach from cell membranes, a well-documented effect that has significant consequences. That “highly conductive salty fluid” that we call blood makes the human body an efficient antenna for picking up electromagnetic radiation, says Goldsworthy. “If an alternating electrical field is applied across the membrane, the negatively charged membrane and the positively charged \[calcium and potassium\] ions move in opposite directions so that the ions tend to bounce on and off the membrane.”

Calcium ions with a double charge are more easily removed from the membrane than the single-charge potassium ions. Because potassium is more abundant in living cells than calcium, potassium ions are more likely to replace calcium, however, calcium ions give cell membranes a stability that potassium ions cannot. Weakened cell membranes tear more easily.

The body has mechanisms for repairing torn membranes, but, before healing occurs, weakened membranes allow cell contents to escape, causing inflammation and free calcium to enter the cell. Calcium triggers metabolic changes in cells, including enzyme activity and gene expression. Its effects depend upon the cells involved.

“Leakage in the hair cells of the cochlea give false sensations of sound (tinnitus),” explains Goldsworthy, “and those in the vestibular system (which sense rotation, linear acceleration and gravity) give false sensations of motions, which result in dizziness and symptoms of motion sickness, including nausea.” Too much calcium in brain neurons causes the cells to release neurotransmitters more easily.

Goldsworthy hypothesizes, “It is possible that at least some forms of electrosensitivity could be due to the victims having their natural blood calcium levels bordering on hypocalcaemia.”

Hypocalcaemia is a medical condition, usually caused by a hormone imbalance, in which the concentration of ionized calcium in the blood and in extracellular fluid is abnormally low. People with hypocalcaemia have fewer ions to complete with the potassium ions rushing to cell membranes disrupted by electromagnetic currents.

Hypocalcaemia and electrosensitvity share many of the same symptoms: skins disorders, paresthesias (pins and needles, numbness, sensations of burning, etc.), fatigue, muscle cramps, cardiac arrhythmia, and gastrointestinal problems. Goldsworthy says, “If this \[hypothesis\] is correct, conventional treatment for hypocalcaemia may relieve some if not all of these symptoms.”

Calcium supplementation is not such a hot idea because that's just more calcium being released all over the place - probably how one gets a calcified pineal gland... or calcified arteries or...

Too bad you can't just move... at some point where there will be nowhere to hide...

Caroline was on to a good subject... Here you have something that works with many others, perhaps you also.

\- Start to breath with your nose, not your mouth. Inhale with nose and feel a vibration in your larynx.

\- Breath should not be made by lungs but with your diaphrahm.

\- You can sit or lie down. Depends on your energy status.

\- Now you use a technique we call 1 - 2. You inhale for example one second and hold a short time and exhale for two seconds. You can use any time, just base in on that system, inhale for five and exhale for ten.

\- Close your eyes.

\- Level 2 is 1 - 4 - 2. Inhale for one, hold your breath for four and exhale for two.

\- Try to make a humming noise in your larynx.
