### shads — 2015-05-17T15:47:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hyperthryoidism-adrenal-fatigue-derealisation.13386/post-164518>

Hey guys, sorry for the lack of response, ive been overseas on holiday for the past month so haven't really been online much. Just landed back in Oz today - we had a week in San Fransisco, a week in New York and three weeks in the UK where im from. Was a great holiday, just what I needed as haven't had an extended period off work for some time now.

Not sure what happened with the old photo but ive put a new one up, hopefully this one doesn't disappear again!

> [billy said:](/goto/post?id=163456)
>
> Update on the new protocol? No pic bro? Dr K prefers a pic.
> I use Calm at night too. Is it helping?
> Are you now sleeping on the transformer for warmth?  I need to reduce my EMF at home more too...

I was definitely feeling improvements for the two weeks before going on holiday. My energy seemed a lot more stable, anxiety somewhat reduced and generally an improvment in overall mood. Whilst on holiday I decided to stop taking pretty much all the various supplements and potions, apart from vitamin c, mg and vitamin d. I guess I wanted to try to put it all to the back of my mind and focus on making the most of the holiday. Before we went I was a bit concerned with how I would cope with the jet lag, all the site seeing and walking around (the previous holiday we had at xmas I was typically limited to walking a few hundred meters before being tired), but I actually managed fine most days which was a pleasent surprise. In NY and San Fran we were out and about loads, with loads of walking around the various places, I only had a couple days where I had to get off my feet and take things easy. The jet lag hit pretty hard though for quite a few days after each flight.

Eating healthly was reasonably easy in SF. In NY, im glad to say it was very easy  - there's loads of paleo oriented spots to grab some decent food, loads of seafood and quick stuff like smoothies that actually have decent ingredients to choose from. The food choices went a bit downhill in UK, but it didn't seem to affect me too badly. I'll be back on epi-paleo from first thing tomorrow!

> [Penny said:](/goto/post?id=163457)
>
> My, what a high dose of methylfolate... I never go over 200mcg myself... When dealing with an ill person, I use Thorne b-complex #12 - it has \*all\* the b vitamins in it which work together - supporting the whole cycle is more helpful than one part of it - plus, since it is water soluble, more frequent low dosing seems to make more sense to me...
> If you get a headache from the methyl-guard plus, it means you are not using all the methyl groups and they have turned into glutamates, thus giving you a headache - magnesium will help the headache... you must at the very least add b12 to this regiment - I don't see it in your supplement list - take the b12 with or before the folate -

Yes, it was seemingly a way too high dose as I had quite a negative reaction to it. Only took one tablet (so thats 1mg of Methylcobalamin and 1mg of Methyltetrahydrofolate per day) for three days running, but it made me feel really spaced out and very off colour. I don't really remember having much of a headache, but something in it definitely didn't agree with me. I'll have a look in to the b-complex #12, thanks for the suggestion. Im seeing an MTHFR specialist in Sydney this week so it will be interesting to see what she says.

**Pseudohypoxia = low O2 around mitochondria to accept electrons** \= Low NAD+/NADH ratio = NAD+ drops in people with blue light exposure = elevated ubiquitin rates = low levels of electrons = electron density in tissues is a function of the DHA concentrations = low EZ size in cell water = dehydration = higher positive charges (protons) in proteins making them less hydrophilic = low intracellular pH = low redox potential = cell and mitochondrial swelling (cyto c release) = lowered magnetic and electric fields in mitochondria = low ATP levels = a lot of carbs and protein electrons on ECT = altered serotonin and dopamine levels in the frontal lobes = NT release tied to calcium efflux = calcium controls voltage gated channels, NMDA, and glutamate excitotoxicity= low DC electric current = **low tissue DHA = altered perceptions of reality and depression/anxiety.**

### billy — 2015-06-14T21:43:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hyperthryoidism-adrenal-fatigue-derealisation.13386/post-168131>

So Dr K crushed it. :head spin:

On the plane I now take off my socks and keep my feet in the metal frame of the seat in front of me. Lees jet lag, etc.
It's not a ground to Physical Earth, but I think of it as a transformer, or "separately derived system" in electrical speak.

A transformer's high voltage primary windings don't contact the low voltage secondary windings - hence separate power system.
The secondary side grounded conductor, or neutral, requires a new "grounding electrode conductor" for a ground reference/path. In a building, this can be to a copper water pipe in 10' of dirt contact, building steel, or a ground ring laid in the dirt. I figure it's the same on a plane - but in this case the separation from physical earth is much greater than fractions of millimeters. Not optimal but we still go to Thailand 1-2 times a year!

In Asia, we eat with feet in the sand on the beach and food is still not from big agra and on islands the fish is often caught by local fishers. Feels so good, as 311 might sing.

Check in again, it's been almost a month!

Currently taking transdermal b12, activated b complex, adrenal herbs, sunflower lecithin (for phospholipids), vit d, vit c and mg. About to try SAM-e this week to see if that helps (I have low homocysteine likely due to several MAT SNPs).

The last few weeks have been pretty good, I feel like ive made some decent progress - however I (stupidly) ended up having several beers last weekend (haven't really drank for the best part of a year now) which has set me back a long way. ### calishaye — 2015-07-04T01:19:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hyperthryoidism-adrenal-fatigue-derealisation.13386/post-170620>

hi Shads, I have most of the same symptoms, including the depersonalization and the ear/tinnitus thing and the hearing distortion. I only get it in one ear too, the right ear. It can get to the point where just the noise from the refrigerator motor is overwhelming. I can't go anywhere except outside when it gets that bad. You are the only other person I know of who experiences this. Have you figured out what brings it on? Mine comes and goes. I can last from a few hours to a few weeks. Sometimes it seems like dairy (even raw) will bring it on or lots of fat. I didn't start having it until I started eating lots of fat, raw dairy and living in this apt. Sometimes eating lots of greens seems to bring it on (high salicylates maybe??)

Hope things get better for you soon.

Jill

# Thread: increasing DHA in eggs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increasing-dha-in-eggs.13418/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 31
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### LisaLearning — 2015-03-22T13:16:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increasing-dha-in-eggs.13418/post-159168>

Is increasing the DHA content of eggs going to result in active DHA at the correct position on the glycerol?

I considered adding krill oil to my hens' food and wondered if this would increase the DHA content of their eggs, then I wondered if that processed DHA in the krill oil that was less bioavailable might just pass through that DHA making eggs with less bioavailable DHA. Or perhaps the hens' systems would somehow restructure the molecules and it would be more bioavailable?

Maybe instead feeding algae to my hens might increase the bioavailable DHA?

Jack, do you know anything about this, or have any thoughts on this matter? Or anyone else?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-22T17:12:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increasing-dha-in-eggs.13418/post-159186>

chickens can do what lambs and fish do but just not effectively........they do transmit iodine well however.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-03-22T17:34:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increasing-dha-in-eggs.13418/post-159191>

>
> I considered adding krill oil to my hens' food and wondered if this would increase the DHA content of their eggs, then I wondered if that processed DHA in the krill oil that was less bioavailable might just pass through that DHA making eggs with less bioavailable DHA. Or perhaps the hens' systems would somehow restructure the molecules and it would be more bioavailable?
>
> Maybe instead feeding algae to my hens might increase the bioavailable DHA?
>
> Jack, do you know anything about this, or have any thoughts on this matter? Or anyone else?

I remember in the book "Omega 3's - The Queen of Fats" by Susan Allport (an amazing book about DHA) she cites some studies where the compare a hen in Greece who scratches for snails, eats whatever is on the hillside with 'battery hens' and the DHA was 10 times as much in the egg of the Greek hen. That really brought home to me an egg is not just an egg. So I would think whatever their 'natural' diet is...............lucky you to have you own hens.

### LisaLearning — 2015-03-22T17:35:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increasing-dha-in-eggs.13418/post-159192>

So supplement their feed for the added iodine, but don't worry about DHA. Still with something like algae, then? I know people give flax to chickens hoping to increase omega 3, but I presume that isn't going to work for wanting to maximise DHA. What about mercury in those sea products - do chickens methylate mercury (a bizarre question - not expecting an answer, just asking in case you do know)?

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-03-22T23:00:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increasing-dha-in-eggs.13418/post-159227>

Here is some of what Susan Allport wrote in "Omega-3's the Queen of Fats" about the "Greek chicken"

"S. began by testing purslane, one of the most common wild green in Greece (and many other countries). She was amazed to find that this ordinary plant, a weed in most of the world's eyes has an alpha linolenic acid content 4 times that of cultivated spinach. Purslane is **not a cold adapted plant** (emphasis mine) like some other greens full of omega-3's such as rapeseed, spinach and aquatic plants. It is not for this reason it is full of Omega-3's. Rather botanists suspect it's high level of Omega-3's work to compensate for light damage since purslane grows in intense sunlight"

She (going by memory here I must read it again) has shown a connection between DHA and cold. How it helps the fish see at great depths, also went into how it helped fish withstand great pressure of water and also withstand great cold by keeping the cell membrane more pliable..............a lot of this is connecting up now with Dr K's work.............I really MUST read it again it is like 7 years since I read it. It's kind of amazing this book stood out for me like an island and now it is connecting up with all this other stuff............

### nonchalant — 2015-08-17T18:50:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increasing-dha-in-eggs.13418/post-174928>

@[LisaLearning](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9911/) I had some cheap fish (whiting) in the freezer that I originally bought for our cat. It is old and smelly, and we aren't going to eat it. So I've been thawing it out a few pieces at a time and feeding them to our guineas every once in a while. I have some large nails that I use to fix them to the ground so the birds can eat easier. They go bonkers for them.

I also separate raw milk and give them the skim milk. And yes, they get fish bones and such, and their own egg shells, sunned and crushed up. And oyster shell of course.

And you can water a spot in the yard and put down a piece of wood or metal on top. Leave for a few days, then uncover it when the chickens are nearby. They'll snatch the crickets and other bugs underneath with glee.

**THE BEST SUPPLEMENT:** Nuts are what I consider a supplement in any diet. Moreover, they are low carb supplement that also happen to fuel the human brain with ketones because many are pure ketogenic. Don't buy replace fake shit from your favorite supplement maker. Use nature's supplements in the form of brazils, macadamias, and pecans are ~90% fat by the way. Don't tell Uncle Dave this. He'll brand nuts next with flavored fat water. The NUTTY BONUS: they are loaded with magnesium, copper, selenium, many trace minerals and micronutrients. This is why they are one of the few supplements I always recommend.

# Thread: Questions on where to begin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-on-where-to-begin.13437/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 27
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Amber13 — 2015-03-23T19:10:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-on-where-to-begin.13437/post-159290>

I have been in pain since I was 17 for about 2-3 years now. At first I pushed through it ignored it and just took the pain pills my doctor prescribed. In college my pain became worse the more I tried to ignore it. First my stomach started hurting and I could no longer eat because of the pain I was down 30 pounds to around 90. Then I began suffering from incurable fatigue, finally my neck tensed and locked causing all my muscles to spasm. I could no longer get out of bed much less go back to college. Ever since then I have been searching for a diagnosis and cure. I started eating right it took a while but I can now eat with just mild pain, and I can get out of bed for a little after I fixed my sleep schedule. I've been told that I might have fibromyalgia. (In the process of getting that diagnosed by a rheumatologist.) But I know what the diagnoses of Fibro means for conventional medicine... no cure. I know I must find out myself what's wrong and treat in accordingly. I wanted to know what sorts of tests I should start with and maybe supplement suggestions. I really want to test if I have lyme but I've heard those are wildly inaccurate- are there any good ones? I've only had minor testing done so far stating my testosterone and progesterone, magnesium, and vit D, are all low and that I was homozygous for the MTHFR mutation.(I'm not sure about my other snaps as they only tested MTHFR.) but no treatment was administered for anything so far.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-on-where-to-begin.13437/post-159291>

@[Martin](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/950/) - can you let her know what type of testing you have done for fibro, mold, etc?

### Amber13 — 2015-03-23T19:50:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-on-where-to-begin.13437/post-159292>

### fitness@home — 2015-03-23T19:58:28+0200

I am heterozygous C677T

### cantweight — 2015-03-23T20:14:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-on-where-to-begin.13437/post-159298>

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=159295)
>
> @[cantweight](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1283/), you dealt with Lyme. What kind of testing/treatment did you do?

I used the Igenex Lyme and full co-infection Panel....this was back in 2009 I don't know if there are any better options now but this is what my doc called "gold standard" for Lyme testing.

Don't go down that crazy road with fibro meds....I feel like they caused me more trouble than they were worth....gabapentin, lyrica, cymbalta....then the rheumatologist added plaquenil and diclophenac.....what a god damned mess....avoid that shit at all costs!

Magnesium malate helped the fibro and so did getting my D level up. You have to get good sleep.....mind the hell out of your light cycles. My shit storm started around the same age as yours....I came through....Im sure you will too

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-23T21:27:47+0200

### Penny — 2015-03-23T22:25:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-on-where-to-begin.13437/post-159313>

And bone broth... and probiotics...

The best supplement for fibro is: Fibro Response by Source Naturals - there's an s-load of mag malate in it along with lots of wonderful other things to raise glutathione -

Magnesium malate by itself helps also - and someone I know got rid of 90% of her fibroid in 2 weeks by drinking one bottle (the big one) of Fiji water per day...

Epsom salt baths are helpful - anything to get your fascia hydrated - followed by morning sun - and eat lots of seafood at least once a day - seafood broths are very hydrating -

The best b vitamin for the 1298 IMHO is Thorne Research #12 - it seems to be the one people react to the least -

A great gut test for $99.00 is [www.americangut.org](http://www.americangut.org) - they will give you the DNA of your gut flora - in detail - a steal IMO - I would go for the low hanging fruit before blowing mega cash on Lyme tests...

No blue light at night - this is huge -

Start with

### Martin — 2015-03-24T14:49:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-on-where-to-begin.13437/post-159365>

Amber13, back when this started, how did the pain come on? A specific event or gradually? How are your cognitive abilities? I assume that the more you are active, the longer the recovery? Tell us more about your life leading up feeling crappy. Mom's health? Exposures to chemicals, drugs, any major stressors?

Light is a huge issue. Every day I read from Dr Kruse's work and various sources as to how it affects us. Strive to reduce your exposure to fake light of any kind, but especially blue light from electronics like cell phones and TV. Wear blue blockers.

I'd go after some more basic testing before leaping into specific mold or Lyme testing. Molds and bacteria are everywhere and it's our crappy ability to deal with them that's the real issue. DHA and light, baby.

Get DHEA, salivary cortisol and melatonin, HS-CRP, IGF-1, prolactin, BUN/Creatinine ratio, and perhaps alpha MSH. Unfortunately, there are no _specific_ tests that can point to fibro, but science is catching up... sorta. Light is one of the missing keys.

It's a difficult journey to be sure, but this is the place to find ANSWERS. That's the bright side.

Martin

### Amber13 — 2015-03-24T18:29:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-on-where-to-begin.13437/post-159368>

Thanks for you comments everyone Caroline I'll be sure to do that my sleeps not so good since I'm trying to get off the pain killers my doctor gave me. The withdrawals get bad at night even with tapering. My EMF situation was pretty bad up till now I've started limiting my room, but before even my bed gave off wifi! There's still not much I can do about the rest of the house because my family refuses to turn of the Internet router at all. I'm going to be trying to start college again soon but this time, since my physical limitations it'll have to be online and I know I'll be spending tons of time on the computer for school work and classes. Before I was eating a diet my functional medicine doctor told me to eat- similar to the Epi Paleo in nutrients but I was told no oysters or pigs, now I'm following the Epi Paleo diet I have some questions how many oysters should I aim for a day? And are pigs okay to eat?

Martin I always thought my stressed was the catalyst to my pain. My life was pretty stressed before I got sick- the most stressed I'd ever been at that point in my life. I don't remember if there were any chemical exposures, no drugs, but all the houses I have ever lived in has had mold. My moms health is not so great. She has high blood pressure and seizures she also had a stroke before at Age 30, shes on dialysis because of kidney failure. She doesn't handle stress well just like I don't. I was also diagnosed with pyroluria and yeast if that's any help. You say it's about how we deal with mold and bacteria as this is the issue but how do I know and improve how I react them? Through diet, dha, and light exposure or does methylation also have something to do with it? I also want to know if a heavy metal test or physical Therapy would be a worth it.

### Martin — 2015-03-25T16:29:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-on-where-to-begin.13437/post-159413>

I'm not 1298 or 677 so I don't know much to guide you. All the info above is steering you in the right direction. It's VERY hard to say what's best for you at this time as far as testing goes. We're all different because of our environment, so we can't rec something without knowing a lot of context regarding your life. Doing the basics is always the best place to start. Start face-dunking in cold water, CTing as much as possible, blue blockers, mindfulness, good water, all will establish natural baselines for your natural rhythms. All these things are inexpensive and easily done at home. If you have the money, go for more detailed testing and plug in what you've learned/heard/intuit.

Then, as you find out about specifics to your case, plug them in under the guidance of a doc who knows something about all this. Dr Tim Jackson has helped many people here. Read what you can, ask more questions, and keep moving.

### Amber13 — 2015-03-25T17:57:23+0200

Thanks martin, Besides the pain it's the fatigue that really gets to me- stopping me from doing anything. I think I'm going to start by fixing my hormones while of course following the protocols. I've also set up an appointment with Kendal Stewart I hope this helps also.

### Penny — 2015-03-26T00:49:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-on-where-to-begin.13437/post-159433>

and try "earthing" naturally with your feet on the ground, not plugged into anything - read the cold thermogenesis protocol - it is amazing - and pay attention to your circadean clock - food within a half hour of waking after your eyes have been exposed to morning sunlight - and blue blockers/flux (download it) at night - also try a red incandescent light to read by at night with some ice packs - amazing -

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-26T14:18:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-on-where-to-begin.13437/post-159465>

Where to begin? Evaluate your options................then determine your choices.

Do you embrace your options? Do you' feel' fenced in? We may be running out of options, but running out isn't an option for a winner. There are many ways to success, to help others, to improve ourselves. The key is not making things perfect, but to keep moving toward improvement incrementally using all your options available. Life is not a matter of chance, it is a matter of choice; it's a series of options we take to create the life we desire.

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-05-16T03:36:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-on-where-to-begin.13437/post-164399>

> [Amber13 said:](/goto/post?id=159296)
>
> Right thank you, I'm homozygous for A1298C

If a person are homozygous for MTHFR, they cannot convert synthetic 'folic acid' to natural 'folate' thus impairing methylation.
Folate comes from leafy greens. Folic acid is added to EVERYTHING and can cause problems when not metabolized. Since they cannot get folate from folic acid, they would need to eat leafy greens and avoid grains or anything that has folic acid in it since it hangs around unmetabolized. One can supplement with 5-methyltetrahydrafolate but detoxing is important before doing so because it could make one worse if there are SNPS in the transsulpuration pathway.
Dr Ben Lynch is a great resource for MTHFR but chances are MTHFR is a piece to the puzzle and won't fix everything.
[http://mthfr.net/](http://mthfr.net/)
I used to be really into the whole 'methylation' thing until I found this site and realized that this stuff has a bigger effect on our biology than any SNPS do. If you don't get Jack's stuff right you might as well be homozygous for all the SNPS!
Homozygous MTHFR is a pretty important SNP to take care of however

Do you think if someone is really doing what we are learning here we need to supplement for MTHFR? If we do the epi paleo RX, tons of sunlight, CT, cutting out blue light and EMF, optimizing sleep, is MTHFR an issue? Or have we bypassed it? I say this as I was all "f'd up" and am sure I may have had an MTHFR issue. Instead of getting tested for it, I was inspired by Jack's podcast with Tim Jackson. He talked about bypassing it. I think I have bypassed absolutely everything. My main focus is sun and DHA, tons of liquids and good sleep. I was a total wreck and seemed to have bypassed everything quickly. The reason I am asking these questions is now that I am really optimal, I have been thinking, "Do I need to go take the various folates" to optimize even more or do I just strengthen by current biohack by stacking in more--more DHA, more seaweed, more sun, more CT. Sorry, I am kind of thinking out loud here.

BTW, when I decided to bypass it, I used the Vikings as my guide. We know that the Vikings had no access to folate through veggies and were hypo methylaters. With the reflection of sun off the snow, cold, DHA, they became historically bad ass, and historically brilliant. By guess is they may have gone mad when they traveled and were outside of their environment. However, when they were in their environment, they bypassed their methylation defects and became genetically gifted.

# Thread: 500 error on home page
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/500-error-on-home-page.13438/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2015-03-23T20:38:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/500-error-on-home-page.13438/post-159299>

I have been unable to access [http://jackkruse.com](http://jackkruse.com) for a few weeks now from home. I can, however, access my dashboard and other pages behind the home page using other links. I've checked my security and privacy settings for Firefox, but I don't see a problem. I also tried IE, which is at the default install settings.

Does anyone have a suggestion for me to try? I have put in a ticket, but the problem seems to be on my end.

> **Internal Server Error**
> The server encountered an internal error or misconfiguration and was unable to complete your request.
>
> Please contact the server administrator, [webmaster@jackkruse.com](mailto:webmaster@jackkruse.com) and inform them of the time the error occurred, and anything you might have done that may have caused the error.
>
> More information about this error may be available in the server error log.
>
> Additionally, a 500 Internal Server Error error was encountered while trying to use an ErrorDocument to handle the request.

### Optimalbound — 2015-03-23T20:57:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/500-error-on-home-page.13438/post-159301>

It is possibly a cookie related issue.

[http://pcsupport.about.com/od/findbyerrormessage/a/500servererror.htm](http://pcsupport.about.com/od/findbyerrormessage/a/500servererror.htm)
[http://pcsupport.about.com/od/browsers/f/delete-cookies.htm](http://pcsupport.about.com/od/browsers/f/delete-cookies.htm)

### av8r — 2015-03-23T21:24:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/500-error-on-home-page.13438/post-159305>

Try deleting/clearing your browsers history.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-23T21:25:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/500-error-on-home-page.13438/post-159306>

its a cookies..........issue. New server is now in use

### JanSz — 2015-03-23T21:25:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/500-error-on-home-page.13438/post-159307>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=159299)
>
> I have been unable to access [http://jackkruse.com](http://jackkruse.com) for a few weeks now from home. I can, however, access my dashboard and other pages behind the home page using other links. I've checked my security and privacy settings for Firefox, but I don't see a problem. I also tried IE, which is at the default install settings.
>
> Does anyone have a suggestion for me to try? I have put in a ticket, but the problem seems to be on my end.

[http://pcsupport.about.com/od/findbyerrormessage/a/500servererror.htm](http://pcsupport.about.com/od/findbyerrormessage/a/500servererror.htm)
Since a 500 Internal Server Error is generated by the website you're visiting, you could see one in _any_ browser in _any_ [operating system](http://pcsupport.about.com/od/termshm/g/term_os.htm), even on your smartphone.

Most of the time, a 500 Internal Server Error displays inside the Internet browser window,

# Thread: YES abroad- Which countries to consider for optimal?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yes-abroad-which-countries-to-consider-for-optimal.13483/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 32
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Matty_M — 2015-03-26T03:12:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yes-abroad-which-countries-to-consider-for-optimal.13483/post-159444>

Hi everyone! I mentioned this in my journal (15 year old who could use some advice...)- but I applied for a scholarship with the State Department for the AFS Kennedy-Lugar YES abroad program. It was created to support Muslim relations after 9/11.

I was immediately interested, applied, and got to semi-finalist status (110 left from >1000). I just went down to DC for the weekend to the in-person selection event to be interviewed and meet everyone, and it was a great experience. I met so many teenagers who think more like myself.

Anyway, I think this could definitely be an opportunity to further my "healing" or at least get a phenomenal boost in my health. I think many places will be better than where I am.

These are all of the countries, listed in the order of my preference. I have considered the environment, the diet, as well as the culture and overall experience I might have.

1\. Turkey (Loaded seafood, slightly higher in EMF than I'd like but worth it for the culture)
2\. Indonesia (Loaded seafood, low-EMF, but near the donut hole...)
3\. Ghana (low-EMF, pretty good food)
4\. Malaysia (higher-emf, lots of seafood)
5\. Oman (actually very safe with the program, good stable king, referred to as Switzerland of Middle East by alumni who I talked to)
6\. Thailand (slightly political unstable, higher-emf)
7\. Phillipines (monsoons, etc.)
8\. Macedonia (more similar to home)
9\. Bosnia and Herzegovina (more similar to home)
10\. India (too many people, pollution, emf, etc.)

These are the things I _think_ I know. Please give any input you have and suggest and all changes. I really appreciate it!

Thanks,

\- Matt

### Lahelada — 2015-03-26T03:38:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yes-abroad-which-countries-to-consider-for-optimal.13483/post-159445>

Malaysia , educated polite population, relative peace between the major religions. If you can position yourself near the beach that should help balancing the EMF load.

\- Matt

### kovita — 2015-03-26T13:07:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yes-abroad-which-countries-to-consider-for-optimal.13483/post-159460>

I speak for myself now, everyone is different, so just my opinion. Avoid SE Asia. I lived there and I would not say it was good healthwise. lots of EMF (I assume you will be in the city), smart world with overuse of technology, people who seek materias more than anything else, seafood and fish not so good, clima not really good for someone of Nordic ancestry...
i loved Africa, however I have never been to Ghana, i lived in East Africa. that was the most powerfull experience of my life. Life is tough and unpredictable, lots of diseases, difficult to access high quality food....but the nature, the place...it really is our cradle and there is something about this place (magnetism?) that makes it healing irrespectively of anything else. Emf coud be very low of you go countryside, cities suck big time.
i know oman quite well since I lived in UAE. That could be a great choice. Not so much EMF, decent seafood, very laid back islamic politics, beautifull nature, nice people and I would guess there must be good magnetism. You can go and camp on the beach anywhere you wish, it is a great place and very safe place comparing to lets say Africa.

### Shijin13 — 2015-03-26T19:30:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yes-abroad-which-countries-to-consider-for-optimal.13483/post-159483>

I'd skip Indonesia, and Maylasia. they're great places to visit. but decent food/medical care is only found in the cities which are tech heavy. This includes Singapore, Hong Kong, most cities on the Thai coast.

I will say one place I'd consider in Indonesia is Ubud - however due to the large EXPAT population, there's bound to be lots of nnEMF in use. but I'd only consider this location if I didn't have medical issues.

As for the middle east - Ask @[TheKid](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/477/) about living there, and is it a good place to live. I've been to Qatar, and Bahraine. you're pretty much stuck in cities, there's not a lot of "country" that's remote that isn't desert. Water is a huge issue.

I would go back to guam in a heart beat. however, not sure what the nnEMF looks like now vs 1997. I'm betting it worse. but you're sitting on the deepest trench in the world - so it might just mitigate the nnEMF.

the chicxilub crater is ideal as Jack suggests. I'd also look at Panama... good medical care, access to good food and a nice balance of light/darkness and you can get out into the country.

### Erin Gaiser — 2018-07-17T20:42:00+0300

### Michael CULLEN — 2018-07-18T13:58:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yes-abroad-which-countries-to-consider-for-optimal.13483/post-246123>

I went to Turkey this May, some thoughts:

All the tourists areas in Dalaman are full of radiation. Every bar has wireless. Nearly every cubic CM of beach has high reading on Acoustimeter due to wireless antennas on trees opposite beach. Idiot tourists demand wifi, so every establishment has to have it.

If you go out towards mountains(just 5 minutes in car) and stay in villa YOU ARE GOOD to go. No detectable radiation levels in some places.

Every day is sunny. Every morning is warm and bright. The heat in the midday is ridiculous. It hit over 100 when I was there.

Dalaman is surround by mountains with pine trees.

Not too many cars on the road.

Seafood at every restaurant. Plenty of sea bass and prawns all over, restaurants on front have salmon and lobster too.

On the way back to airport there are plenty of green areas with few properties that have no wifi. As we got closer to the airport I noticed more and more masts, but still way less than UK Cities.

### Pogo — 2018-07-19T07:39:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yes-abroad-which-countries-to-consider-for-optimal.13483/post-246170>

> [Antonis said:](/goto/post?id=246052)
>
> Any recommendations for Indonesia would be appreciated. Am going next year in January. Obviously away feom tourists in a low nnEmf and population??

I was in Lombok back in May.

As long as you stay away from Mataram (the tri-city area), most of the island is never too far away from very poor to non-existent signal, and few people. It seems from mobile coverage maps that this is so for most of the islands in West and East Nusa Tenggara.

Obviously (most of) Bali et al now have ubiquitous 4G mobile and wireless access.

### Antonis — 2018-07-19T16:05:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yes-abroad-which-countries-to-consider-for-optimal.13483/post-246190>

> [Pogo said:](/goto/post?id=246170)

>

>
> Obviously (most of) Bali et al now have ubiquitous 4G mobile and wireless access.

So is Lombok a poor emf place to go?

### Pogo — 2018-07-20T08:20:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yes-abroad-which-countries-to-consider-for-optimal.13483/post-246253>

Depends on where in Lombok, Antonis.

As per previous, if you wish to avoid nnEMF, you should stay away from the more populous areas - Mataram and Senggigi, mostly. These are both in the East. Wireless infrastructure and population density is far sparser in the North, and more so again in the South and East.

Not sure how long this will be the case. In many areas, every second shop is a SIM dealer for one or two of the top telcos, so I am sure the infrastructure will modernise (and increase its reach) over time.

NB: This is all based on the quality of the signals that I observed while there; along with some validation from Telco coverage maps. I didn't carry an RF meter, so the reality may differ.

# Thread: Autoimmune issues and raw oysters
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autoimmune-issues-and-raw-oysters.13501/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-27T14:11:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autoimmune-issues-and-raw-oysters.13501/post-159527>

Right now my n=1 is such that I have to take things into consideration that hopefully a few months or a year from now I won't even have to think about.

For example - I have never eaten seafood in my life, except twice when I had allergic type reactions To scallops and clams. But I insisted my doctor test me for seafood/food allergies and the r esults shockingly came back that I am NOT allergic to shellfish after all. (I hope). So yesterday I bought a tub of shucked wild-caught oysters which are now sitting in my refrigerator.

At the store there was a sign that said to cook them to an internal temp of 145 degrees. But here you all say to eat them raw. I've been checking out threads here and all info seems to agree that it's perfectly fine to eat them raw \*IF\* your immune system is in good shape.

What if it is NOT in good shape?

I not only have Hashi's and have a family history of other autoimmune issues, but the past year my immune system has been functioning very poorly, sending me to an oncologist among other things due to how my body is reacting / acting lately. I didn't know it was unusual for people to be put on antibiotics 2-3 times a year every year rof their life, until very recently.. so maybe I've always had a bad immune system. After all I was born a month early via emergency c-section to a mother with pre-eclampsia (and diabetes and congestive heart failure and emphysema a few years later) who didn't breastfeed me.

So bottom line, do I try swallowing one of these things raw or do I cook them? If so, how? And while I'm in here, I also bought a big tub of coconut oil and some kimchi.. what does one do with these? Any suggestions are welcome because I'm completely clueless but eager to learn. Thanks!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-27T14:54:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autoimmune-issues-and-raw-oysters.13501/post-159531>

optimal e cook book has a lot of oysters in it........and any seafood cookbook is a good resource. Caitlin Weeks new cookbook is a good source too. Me........I like Julia Child.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-27T15:08:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autoimmune-issues-and-raw-oysters.13501/post-159534>

I have a huge cookbook collection here, mostly from the 50s - 70s. I'll take a look at see what I can come up with - thanks!

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-27T16:01:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autoimmune-issues-and-raw-oysters.13501/post-159554>

PS I assume that means I ought to cook 'em and not eat them raw. I'm afraid this means I will throw away the container unopened. I do not do fish smells. This is a hold the nose and swallow kind of proposition for me... hm.

### nonchalant — 2015-03-27T17:23:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autoimmune-issues-and-raw-oysters.13501/post-159559>

How about holding your nose and swallowing just one?

### Lahelada — 2015-03-27T17:45:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autoimmune-issues-and-raw-oysters.13501/post-159562>

Cold air alleviates smell. You could eat it outside. Btw my first oyster which I shucked myself had no smell just seawater. I would cook packaged ones. If your kitchen has a back door keep it open before you start cooking and after. Should be doable.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-27T18:28:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autoimmune-issues-and-raw-oysters.13501/post-159565>

Hm, ideas for sure. I have a window right near the stove so that could be helpful... and it will let in very cold air so that could also help... coconut oil smells wonderful, and onion/garlic would add to the aroma if I do cook them... dunno how I could utilize them though, this is all new to me!

### nicld — 2015-03-27T22:15:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autoimmune-issues-and-raw-oysters.13501/post-159571>

I heat my canned ones (from Aldi's) in bacon fat, or butter, or coconut oil and toss a little fresh asiago cheese on it. Not as good for you as raw but I cannot get fresh raw ones around here that often.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-27T22:57:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autoimmune-issues-and-raw-oysters.13501/post-159577>

I was wondering about looking for canned ones but I just wanted to buy what I saw as as much of an impulse buy as possible because I knew if I thought about it too much I'd talk myself out of it. I rarely go to the local Aldi's but I love Price Rite.. I wonder if they'd have some? But Aldi's is doable ... what size can do you get for what cost? Just curious, I paid ten bucks for 8 oz of these fresh chesapeake bay shucked ones at wegman's fish counter. they had fresh ones in the shell too but they were $1.50 each. Yeesh!

### nicld — 2015-03-27T23:02:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autoimmune-issues-and-raw-oysters.13501/post-159579>

I think a can is about $1.89 ish. Usually I send the hubby there to get them for me as I HATE shopping after work.

### JanSz — 2015-03-27T23:45:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autoimmune-issues-and-raw-oysters.13501/post-159580>

I my book Asian stores have a better record with pre-shucked oysters.
Specially high traffic superstores.
They sell them in **16oz tubes. $8-$9,** come from Washington State.
I usually buy few tubes, so the last one may be eaten up to 2 weeks after purchase.
I always eat them raw. My grandson loves them too, but not granddaughter.

Once I bought couple of those at Costco.
Luckily I have not shared them. Ate them within about 4 days.
Last day I got sick.
Getting sick was bad, but on the grand scheme that should not stop someone from eating them.
I just think that Asians are likely to eat them raw more often than others, so they are possibly more careful in caring for them.

I newer came across any smell with oyster.
If I did would throw it out.

///

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-28T00:04:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autoimmune-issues-and-raw-oysters.13501/post-159581>

Thank you for the input! I haven't opened these yet... maybe later this evening!

### Penny — 2015-03-28T00:43:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autoimmune-issues-and-raw-oysters.13501/post-159584>

Somewhere, Dr. Kruse has a video on how he prepares his Oyster Rockefellar... IMHO, this is the way to go for oysters and when I was a kid growing up on Cape Cod, this is the only way I would eat them... what a PITA I was...

# Thread: Infertile? Turn off the fake llght ladies and gentlemen?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-27T14:57:49+0200

1\. [http://www.medicaldaily.com/pregnan...in-plays-key-role-fertility-fetal-development](http://www.medicaldaily.com/pregnant-women-benefit-less-nighttime-light-melatonin-plays-key-role-fertility-fetal-development)
2\. [http://medicalresearch.com/sleep-disorders/electric-lights-disrupts-human-circadian-rhythm/12970/](http://medicalresearch.com/sleep-disorders/electric-lights-disrupts-human-circadian-rhythm/12970/)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-27T14:59:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infertile-turn-off-the-fake-llght-ladies-and-gentlemen.13503/post-159533>

[http://www.independent.co.uk/news/s...pics-ndash-and-what-does-it-mean-1658981.html](http://www.independent.co.uk/news/science/the-equatorial-enigma-why-are-more-girls-than-boys-born-in-the-tropics-ndash-and-what-does-it-mean-1658981.html)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-27T15:10:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infertile-turn-off-the-fake-llght-ladies-and-gentlemen.13503/post-159535>

The Earth's magnetic field varies considerably and in the last 1500 years it has weakened, while in the last 120 yrs the creation of non naive magnetic fields on top of the tectonic plates has soared. This is a double whammy for energy loss. Women are more sensitive to nnEMF naturally so when there is stress in the ecosystem for any reason more females are born to meet the stress for survival. This alteration of magnetic field directly is tied water chemistry because lowered magnetic field protection leads to more cloud formation and water for crops below to feed offspring. One needs few males and a lot of females to repopulate a stressed ecosystem. Charged particles, or cosmic rays, constantly bombard the Earth. We are shielded from these rays by the sun's and Earth's magnetic fields. The Earth's field is weaker than the sun's. When these fields are strong, fewer cosmic rays reach Earth. Conversely, when weaker more cosmic rays reach the atmosphere.

Clouds don't spring from thin air. Water vapor in the atmosphere forms droplets by condensing on small particles. There are many experiments suggesting electrically charged particles in the atmosphere, or ions, are an important part of this nucleation process. Cosmic rays, from the sun are the source of the charge. It gets more interesting: Many natural mechanisms have been proposed for climate change during the past millennia, however, none of these appears to have accounted for the change in global temperature seen over the second half of the last century. As such the rise in temperature has been attributed to man made mechanisms. Analysis of the movement of the Earth's magnetic poles over the last 105 years demonstrates strong correlations between the position of the north magnetic, and geomagnetic poles, and both northern hemisphere and global temperatures. Although these correlations are surprising, a statistical analysis shows there is a less than one percent chance they are random, but it is not clear how movements of the poles affect climate. Links between changes in the Earth's magnetic field and climate change, have been proposed previously although the exact mechanism is disputed. These include: The Earth's magnetic field affects the energy transfer rates from the solar wind to the Earth's atmosphere which in turn affects the North Atlantic Oscillation. Movement of the poles changes the geographic distribution of galactic and solar cosmic rays, moving them to particularly climate sensitive areas. Changes in distribution of ultraviolet rays (Vit D3 sulfation) resulting from the movement of the magnetic field, may result in increases in the death rates of carbon sinking oceanic plant life such as phytoplankton. (Lowers DHA) Here is the paper. Climate Change and the Earth's Magnetic Poles, A Possible Connection
Author: Kerton, Adrian K.
Source: Energy & Environment, Volume 20, Numbers 1-2, January 2009 , pp. 75-83(9)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-27T15:11:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infertile-turn-off-the-fake-llght-ladies-and-gentlemen.13503/post-159536>

equator has more protection from magnetosphere.....normally. This is is no longer true because of how man is altering the magnetosphere on the surface and to the ionosphere..........so it maybe the reason why girls are being selected for..........more. We should expect to see boys closer to the pole..........but we are seeing males inside the Arctic circle decrease because of many factors.........this uncoupling is worrisome if you follow Elizabeth Kolbert's book because it is a signal something unusual is afoot.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-27T15:11:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infertile-turn-off-the-fake-llght-ladies-and-gentlemen.13503/post-159537>

Sex in mammals is determined by the type of sperm that fertilises the egg. A sperm carrying the X chromosome of the man will become a female embryo whereas a sperm carrying the Y chromosome will produce a female embryo at conception. The higher a magnetic field strength the less superoxide is made. Free radicals are likely what determines sex in mammals. We also know that embryogenesis and morphogenesis are associated with electric and magnetic polarity.

-   Magnetism is one of the most amazing quantum biologic enigma's I am interested in solving. It is far more complex and ancient than we believe.

-   One of the major differences in sperm and eggs is the number of mitochondria. Sperm have few..........eggs have a ton.

    Sexing before conception requires separation of the X chromosome bearing sperm from the Y chromosome bearing sperm. One commonly used method to accomplish this is flow cytometry. With accuracy of 90%, approximately 10 million live sperm of each sex (X and Y) can be sorted per hour (Siedel, 2003). But it gets more interesting.............

    The sperm can then be dyed with something like Hoechst 33342 (bovine sexting dye) , which is a fluorescing DNA-binding dye (Garner, 2005). This dye binds to DNA molecules within the sperm, when the sperm are subsequently exposed to ultraviolet light the DNA containing the dye will fluoresce. X chromosome bearing sperm contain 3.5% more DNA than Y chromosome bearing sperm (Siedel, 2003), thus the X chromosome bearing sperm will absorb more of the DNA-binding dye. So women DNA is larger than males. Larger means they are less energy efficient from mass equivalence. This is one reason sperm have fewer mitochondria.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-30T09:59:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infertile-turn-off-the-fake-llght-ladies-and-gentlemen.13503/post-159823>

From '**The Body Electric**', by Robert O. Becker and Gary Selden. Chapter titled 'Breathing with the Earth', page 263 of the 1985 edition:

Francis Ivanhoe, a pharmacologist and anthropologist at two universities in San Francisco, suggests how important \[magnetism\] may have been to our own development.

Ivanhoe made a statistical survey of the braincase volume of all known Paleolithic human skulls, and correlated the increase with the magnetic field strength and major advances in human culture during the same period. Ivanhoe found bursts of brain-size evolution at about 380,000 to 340,000 years ago, and again at 55,000 to 30,000 years ago. Both periods corresponded to major ice ages, the Mindel and Wurm, respectively. and they were able eras when great cultural advances were made -- the widespread domestication of fire by Homo erectus in the early Mindel, and the appearances of Home sapiens sapiens and gradual decline of Neaderthals during the Wurm.

Two other other glaciations in the same time span -- the Ganz of about 1,200,000 to 1,050,000 years ago and the Riss of about 150,000 to 100,000 years ago -- didn't call forth such obvious advancements in human evolution. They also differed from the other two in that the average geomagnetic field intensity was much lower.

Ivanhoe has proposed a direct link from the magnetic field through the growth-hormone regulator pathways in the brain to account for the sharp evolutionary gains. He suggests that part of the hippocampus, a section of the brain's temporal lobe, acts as a transducer of electromagnetic energy. A part of the hippocampus called Ammon's horn, an arch with one-way nerve traffic directed by a strong current flow, may read out variations in the field strength, feeding them by a bundle of well-documented pathways called the fornix to the hypothalamus and thence to the anterior pituitary, where growth hormone is produced.

It's known that larger amounts of this hormone in pregnancy increase the size of the cerebral cortex and the number of its nerves cells in the offspring, as compared with other parts of the brain. Ivanhoe also notes that the hippocampus and its connections with the hypothalamus are among the parts of the brain that are much larger in humans than other primates.

The idea gains further support from the fact that neural activity in the hippocampus increases with electrical stimulation and reaches a maximum at 10 to 15 cycles per second, at or slightly above the dominant micropulsation frequency fo today's field. The most powerful shaper of our development may turn out to be the subtlest, a force that's completely invisible to us.
​

One study by Ivanhoe -- [http://myplace.frontier.com/~broosg/Anthro/CC2010.1.pdf](http://myplace.frontier.com/~broosg/Anthro/CC2010.1.pdf)

Cold + Increased magnetism as a stimulus for cortex size increase and cultural evolution.

A reversal of the Earth's magnetic field now, which will drastically diminish field strength, will be the trigger for an actual 6th extinction event (no exaggeration with this statement).

PSA test is actually a test for mold. But nobody in medical world explains that to the patient - docs need $ and by ripping your prostate out they make money.

Richard Ablin, MD, the doctor who developed the PSA test stated; "As I've been trying to make clear for years now, PSA testing can't detect prostate cancer.

PSA chemically is a 33-kDa serine protease which was found to be produced by molds Asperigilus flavus, A. fumigatus, A oryzae, ophiostoma piceae, and Scedosporium apoiospermun, all of which happen to be of the Ascomycete group of fungi.

### Huck — 2015-03-28T17:22:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/elevated-psa-prostate-biopsy-imminent-labs.13507/post-159640>

It is debatable as to how predictive an increasing PSA is for prostate cancer. I have an elevated PSA, but don't have cancer. I do have prostatitis and BPH.

Some things that may be helpful to look at are the trend and how fast PSA is increasing. To have a jump in PSA that big in just 3 months indicates something is going on, but not necessarily cancer. For a 1 time jump like this, doctors usually prescribe 14 days of a sulfa drug to rule out an infection. I just went through that and my PSA dropped over 2 points and is slightly lower than it was 2 years ago. Doctor may want to prescribe Cipro, but if you have to take an antibiotic, I believe a sulfa drug is a better choice. Make sure to rebuild with probiotics and fermented foods.

There is another test that goes with PSA that I think is helpful called Free PSA. It gives a percentage of likelihood that cancer is the cause of the increase in PSA. My doctor was sure I had cancer when my PSA went up, but I had a Free PSA done and it indicated I had a lower likelihood of cancer.

Other things can cause elevated PSA: sex, riding a bike, doctor doing digital rectal exam BEFORE taking blood for PSA, etc.

There are other tests that also indicate likelihood that elevated PSA is caused by cancer. I had a test called PCA3, which is normally used after a biopsy is negative. My thought was if it shows the likelihood of cancer after a negative biopsy, why not before. There are other tests available.

Jack's webinar goes through foods and supplements that are beneficial. I took most of them and still do. To a large degree, what you are trying to do is drive inflammation as low as you can. Jack also doesn't believe that PSA is a valid test for prostate cancer. The thing is, most doctors only know about PSA and that's what they will use.

The next step will probably be a referral to a urologist (surgeon). Probably they will do another PSA test and another DRE. If PSA is still high they will recommend a needle biopsy. The doctor will go in and take several samples (probably 12) from the areas of the prostate most likely to have cancer. The samples will be analyzed and if cancer is found they will be graded. Depending on the grade, a treatment plan will be developed.

This is where it is important to remember you are talking to a surgeon. And as Jack has pointed out, a surgeon who is affiliated with a hospital that has spent millions on a Da Vinci robotic surgery system. The likelihood is the proposed treatment will be robotic surgery to remove the prostate.

Let me suggest an alternative to the "blind" biopsy. I went to VCU in Richmond, VA and had an MRI instead of a "blind" needle biopsy. In my case, I was very fortunate and the MRI was negative for cancer. If cancer had been detected by the MRI, I would have had a guided needle biopsy. Using the MRI, the doctor would know exactly where the cancer was, and would only need to take samples from those areas. So instead of 12 samples taken at "random", only 2-4 samples would have been taken from areas that were suspicious.

The best place on the web I have found for information is [Healingwell](http://www.healingwell.com/community/default.aspx?f=35). This is a forum for men (and supporters) who are just starting out with an elevated PSA and don't know what to do to guys who have had treatment and cancer has returned. From getting simple questions answered to finding the best doctor for doing surgery to identifying and understanding treatment options, these guys are really helpful.

> Richard Ablin, MD, the doctor who developed the PSA test stated; "As I've been trying to make clear for years now, PSA testing can't detect prostate cancer.
>
>
> PSA chemically is a 33-kDa serine protease which was found to be produced by molds Asperigilus flavus, A. fumigatus, A oryzae, ophiostoma piceae, and Scedosporium apoiospermun, all of which happen to be of the Ascomycete group of fungi.

### Monte — 2015-03-28T18:37:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/elevated-psa-prostate-biopsy-imminent-labs.13507/post-159650>

> [Huck said:](/goto/post?id=159640)
>
> It is debatable as to how predictive an increasing PSA is for prostate cancer. I have an elevated PSA, but don't have cancer. I do have prostatitis and BPH.
>
> Some things that may be helpful to look at are the trend and how fast PSA is increasing. To have a jump in PSA that big in just 3 months indicates something is going on, but not necessarily cancer. For a 1 time jump like this, doctors usually prescribe 14 days of a sulfa drug to rule out an infection. I just went through that and my PSA dropped over 2 points and is slightly lower than it was 2 years ago. Doctor may want to prescribe Cipro, but if you have to take an antibiotic, I believe a sulfa drug is a better choice. Make sure to rebuild with probiotics and fermented foods.
>
> There is another test that goes with PSA that I think is helpful called Free PSA. It gives a percentage of likelihood that cancer is the cause of the increase in PSA. My doctor was sure I had cancer when my PSA went up, but I had a Free PSA done and it indicated I had a lower likelihood of cancer.
>
> Other things can cause elevated PSA: sex, riding a bike, doctor doing digital rectal exam BEFORE taking blood for PSA, etc.
>
> There are other tests that also indicate likelihood that elevated PSA is caused by cancer. I had a test called PCA3, which is normally used after a biopsy is negative. My thought was if it shows the likelihood of cancer after a negative biopsy, why not before. There are other tests available.
>
> Jack's webinar goes through foods and supplements that are beneficial. I took most of them and still do. To a large degree, what you are trying to do is drive inflammation as low as you can. Jack also doesn't believe that PSA is a valid test for prostate cancer. The thing is, most doctors only know about PSA and that's what they will use.
>
> The next step will probably be a referral to a urologist (surgeon). Probably they will do another PSA test and another DRE. If PSA is still high they will recommend a needle biopsy. The doctor will go in and take several samples (probably 12) from the areas of the prostate most likely to have cancer. The samples will be analyzed and if cancer is found they will be graded. Depending on the grade, a treatment plan will be developed.
>
> This is where it is important to remember you are talking to a surgeon. And as Jack has pointed out, a surgeon who is affiliated with a hospital that has spent millions on a Da Vinci robotic surgery system. The likelihood is the proposed treatment will be robotic surgery to remove the prostate.
>
> Let me suggest an alternative to the "blind" biopsy. I went to VCU in Richmond, VA and had an MRI instead of a "blind" needle biopsy. In my case, I was very fortunate and the MRI was negative for cancer. If cancer had been detected by the MRI, I would have had a guided needle biopsy. Using the MRI, the doctor would know exactly where the cancer was, and would only need to take samples from those areas. So instead of 12 samples taken at "random", only 2-4 samples would have been taken from areas that were suspicious.
>
> The best place on the web I have found for information is [Healingwell](http://www.healingwell.com/community/default.aspx?f=35). This is a forum for men (and supporters) who are just starting out with an elevated PSA and don't know what to do to guys who have had treatment and cancer has returned. From getting simple questions answered to finding the best doctor for doing surgery to identifying and understanding treatment options, these guys are really helpful.

Huge help! Thank you. I'm definitely going to register at Healingwell too. I'm reading your other forum posts on prostate as well. Lots to go on now which is better than the mechanized way doctors treat you or the shot-in-the-dark googleing.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-28T17:03:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stupid-questions-thread.13517/post-159636>

In your youth, your brain gets loaded with a paradigm’s policy of truth. As you age, experience and wisdom based upon failure build your inconvenient truth. You’ll see continue see your problems march by and multiply, if you continue to do as you were taught.

Things can change, if you change your ideas of the truth. Life can be different if you choose it
Most don't like to rock the boat........so they remain civilized with respect to the paradigm.
As time elapses and failures mount, you will always wonder what might have happened if you stopped lying to yourself.

This place exists and oasis to question your youthful thoughts and ideas. It's never too late to change events in the quantum world.
If you keep doing as you have always done, you'll create time and space of a reality where you will face the consequences of bad thinking
The delivering of this biologic proof will come directly from the ideas you allowed to infest your mind in youth.

When you get older........and failure mounts, never again to be fooled is what your brain and heart begin to resonate as your new paradigm or reality
Our species is capable of creating alternative realities with thoughts..........but few of us use this ability.

If you don't, you'll live a life where you get to 40-50 sick and tired of being sick and tired. You'll come and visit my profession with problems describing how you feel poorly almost as if you are tongue tied. The doctor will listen and do what they were taught in their youth. And most go with it.........because they have no better ideas of truth in biology.
With serial failures.........some will give up. Others will adapt better to learn the lesson well, that the paradigm of their youth was the real issue.
Some will begin to realize that the best plan of attack is to hide what you have to hide from those who you deal with daily.........some will be your own family and friends.
And you might get lucky and find an oasis of new ideas, where you finally feel comfortably to tell what you have to tell so that others might learn what took you so long to put into words. Some will leap without wings.......others will become voyeurs, and others will just run away..........
Those that run away will find that their problems continue to multiply as time elapses while their time shrinks...........with diseases.
None of this will change if you continually decide to faithfully pursue the policy of truth from your youth...........

> Those that run away will find that their problems continue to multiply as time elapses while their time shrinks...........with diseases.
> None of this will change if you continually decide to faithfully pursue the policy of truth from your youth...........

My approach has always been simple. If you reach a contradiction or dead end in your thinking, one of the assumptions you have made or accepted along the way has to be wrong.

At that point, you have a choice: (1) to revise your paradigm, and resolve the contradiction at all costs by any means available, or (2) to accept and integrate the contradiction into your thinking, and thereby limit the scope of your knowledge and voluntarily accept permanent mental detachment from some aspect of reality. (Which will eventually spread like a cancer to affect other aspects, BTW.)

99.9% of adults will mostly choose option #2. To choose option #1 requires epistemological courage. To maintain choice #1 requires methodological commitment. [Method is more important than content.](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/why-did-this-site-get-sticky.11310/page-4#post-135314)

### sjoshua — 2015-03-31T21:16:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stupid-questions-thread.13517/post-159982>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=159923)
>
> Hows this for a stupid question:
>
> Would it be cost effective for CT in summer to get a chest freezer, fill 3/4 it with water, set to 40F then CT in that?
>
> Would it collapse under the weight, leak or somehow electrocute me?

I have conceptualized this as well, and actually acquired some of the pieces but have yet to find a good freezer to execute. May I suggest a couple 'mods' that I pondered?

\- Assure chest freezer is large enough for you to fit in comfortably (prolly 7 Cu Ft minimum, 8-10 likely more ideal, craigslist is your friend!)
\- Line the inside with a pool liner, or other heavy-duty waterproof material ([www.amazon.com/gp/product/B0001DLLQS/](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B0001DLLQS/))
\- Freezers are pre-set to maintain a temperature in the range of 'freezing'  so, 'you' need a method to correct this by a small amount, since you cannot CT in a giant ice cube. I can think of two methods,
\*simple: get an outlet timer and determine how long to have it on to achieve the temps you want; I'd imagine a rotating on/off schedule every 4-6 hours might be effective to start, but your freezer may vary ([http://www.amazon.com/Hydrofarm-TM01715D-7-Day-Digital-Program/dp/B001BPOBTY/](http://www.amazon.com/Hydrofarm-TM01715D-7-Day-Digital-Program/dp/B001BPOBTY/))
\*precise: get an temperature controller box to plug the freezer into; set the temperature dongle in your freezer bath water, and tune it to your desired temp. This will power the freezer ONLY when it is below your threshold ([http://www.amazon.com/Lerway-All-Purpose-Temperature-Controller-Thermostat/dp/B008KVCPH2/](http://www.amazon.com/Lerway-All-Purpose-Temperature-Controller-Thermostat/dp/B008KVCPH2/))

other thoughts:
\*using H2O2 (hydrogen peroxide) in your water will help it stay 'fresh' longer; you may acquire 29-30% concentration (vs. the pharmacy ~3% concentration version) from gardening/farm supply stores, which will be more cost effective in the long run.
\*having it cold will also prolong the waters lifespan, as would trying to assure you are pre-cleaned before entering.
\*using Epsom Salts will likely help reduce the water's freeze temp while also providing your body with mineral soaking benefits
\*you will likely want to pick up a submersible pump and hose that fits it, for when you do have to swap the water (Home depot sells these, or [http://www.amazon.com/EcoPlus-185-Submersible-Pump-GPH/dp/B0018WVNXC/](http://www.amazon.com/EcoPlus-185-Submersible-Pump-GPH/dp/B0018WVNXC/))

### seanb4 — 2015-04-28T15:03:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stupid-questions-thread.13517/post-162649>

> [sjoshua said:](/goto/post?id=159982)
>
> I have conceptualized this as well, and actually acquired some of the pieces but have yet to find a good freezer to execute. May I suggest a couple 'mods' that I pondered?
>
> \- Assure chest freezer is large enough for you to fit in comfortably (prolly 7 Cu Ft minimum, 8-10 likely more ideal, craigslist is your friend!)
> \- Line the inside with a pool liner, or other heavy-duty waterproof material ([www.amazon.com/gp/product/B0001DLLQS/](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B0001DLLQS/))
> \- Freezers are pre-set to maintain a temperature in the range of 'freezing'  so, 'you' need a method to correct this by a small amount, since you cannot CT in a giant ice cube. I can think of two methods,
> \*simple: get an outlet timer and determine how long to have it on to achieve the temps you want; I'd imagine a rotating on/off schedule every 4-6 hours might be effective to start, but your freezer may vary ([http://www.amazon.com/Hydrofarm-TM01715D-7-Day-Digital-Program/dp/B001BPOBTY/](http://www.amazon.com/Hydrofarm-TM01715D-7-Day-Digital-Program/dp/B001BPOBTY/))
> \*precise: get an temperature controller box to plug the freezer into; set the temperature dongle in your freezer bath water, and tune it to your desired temp. This will power the freezer ONLY when it is below your threshold ([http://www.amazon.com/Lerway-All-Purpose-Temperature-Controller-Thermostat/dp/B008KVCPH2/](http://www.amazon.com/Lerway-All-Purpose-Temperature-Controller-Thermostat/dp/B008KVCPH2/))
>
> other thoughts:
> \*using H2O2 (hydrogen peroxide) in your water will help it stay 'fresh' longer; you may acquire 29-30% concentration (vs. the pharmacy ~3% concentration version) from gardening/farm supply stores, which will be more cost effective in the long run.
> \*having it cold will also prolong the waters lifespan, as would trying to assure you are pre-cleaned before entering.
> \*using Epsom Salts will likely help reduce the water's freeze temp while also providing your body with mineral soaking benefits
> \*you will likely want to pick up a submersible pump and hose that fits it, for when you do have to swap the water (Home depot sells these, or [http://www.amazon.com/EcoPlus-185-Submersible-Pump-GPH/dp/B0018WVNXC/](http://www.amazon.com/EcoPlus-185-Submersible-Pump-GPH/dp/B0018WVNXC/))

Great ideas, though I was most concerned with it holding the water without breaking... any ideas how to stop this?

### NeilBB — 2015-04-28T15:50:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stupid-questions-thread.13517/post-162656>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=162647)
>
> Why does the body ever lose it's brown fat / switch to a warm adapted state. Is it so the body can build fat in warm?
>
> What are the advantages to the body being warm adapted?

Because we don't live in an ice age (or the Arctic Circle). And we have a fundamental relationship with our star called Sol. Yin and Yang of life. You don't need all that brown fat all the time WHEN you have a proper relationship with the sun in a reasonable environment. Why would you? Cold is life preserving but light is what generates life. Life evolved with and takes advantage of the normal chronobiological cycles of our world. Sleep/wake. Light/dark. Cold/hot. Summer/winter. Wet/dry. Oxidation/reduction. Autophagy/apoptosis. Life/death. Pain/pleasure. Collagen zipped/unzipped. Melatonin-on/off.... Etc etc etc...

Life uses all these cycles to utilize energy and manage entropy. Life and time seem to actually emerge from these cycles. It's what it's all about. Chronobiology is the thermodynamic link between physics and biology. Life is not about Yin or about Yang. It's about the dynamic and cyclic relationship of Yinyang...

Our modern strategies may be to increase the brown-fat year round, but the reason for that is mitigation of and adaptation to a grossly abnormal environment, not the normal state of life for humans..

### NeilBB — 2015-04-28T16:23:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stupid-questions-thread.13517/post-162660>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=162647)
>
> Why does the body ever lose it's brown fat / switch to a warm adapted state. Is it so the body can build fat in warm?
>
> What are the advantages to the body being warm adapted?

Let me put it another way. Does your thermostat in your house turn the heater on when the temperature is 95 degrees outside? Does a business retain a division of employees that its forecast project won't be needed for months?

Life is fundamentally evolved to adapt and maximize its resource utilization in response to its perceived environment. It has millions of years of practice in funneling energy sources in response to the natural environment. Chronobiology is how life forecasts and prepares for "the future." But modern artifical life has disrupted life's ability to predict these cycles because we have obscured nature with random and chaotic expressions of light, temperature, and other forms of EM radiation.

A tree doesn't need brown fat and neither does a human being standing in the sun eating an apple. That human instead needs to direct resources to metabolizing said apple, transducing solar radiation, altering epigenetic expression and generating new mitochondria to manage those particular conditions of existence.

Why do humans usually not make any BAT today? Because the modern artifical world we have embraced "tells" our mitochondria that we are ALWAYS sitting in the equatorial sun eating an apple. Simple as that...

Biologically speaking, animals tend to store surplus energy as WAT in long-light cycles for later use. Life senses no need for thermogenic BAT then. Why would it? Mammals are uniquely seasonally sensitive and seem to be able to convert WAT to BAT when it becomes cold and dark. That's just how biology evolved...in order to manage the environmental inputs to correspond with perceived COE...

another stupid question. This is touching the last blog serie. The babies in the womb spent most of the time floating in the first two thirds of the fetal development (they are eseentially protein dense) and once they start to layer fat in the last part of the fetal development they slowly sink. My question points to the fact they tend to spend most of the time of the third pf the fetal development head down. How this happens depends upon the spectrum of light youre talking about.........for example the microwaving Kresser blog talks about the mechanism of action of microwaves.......they increase atomic vibrations while dehydrating.......where Xrays or cosmic rays destroy DNA, dehydrate quickly, and cause anemia, and lack of blood flow really quickly.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-11-13T03:29:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stupid-questions-thread.13517/post-181859>

> [Hansen Kenimer said:](/goto/post?id=181811)
>
> What would you say about someone who is very fair, very blonde, and blue-eyed, who doesn't really tan but never ever burns?

I'd say your proteins have a serious issue..........one that allows blue light to penetrate too deeply. That would put you at deep risk for things with high ubiquitin rates like cancer. Blue light toxicity is a big issue.

Light hits our surfaces differently. That light either pushes or pulls cell membranes that are loaded or deficient with DHA. If you read the beginning of my brain gut five blog you will see how DHA and D3 are linked by nature. Does you clinician make this connection? Now ask yourself this: Can light push things like cell membranes? Could this result in phonons and solitons that get transmuted by the tensegrity system in a cell? You bet your ass it does. Well taking a pill does none of this. Vitamin D3 carries no momentum or energy. The key issue is that animals, like fish, have the photonic power of the sun's rays in their tissues. Remember from Roeland van Wijk's and Fritz Popp's work we know that cells contain a lot of ELF-UV and we use it to signal. So their plasma Vitamin D3 levels match the light energy in the animals tissue when we eat it. In our bodies, we are taking the pill without any of the light energy being transferred to our tissues. Our vitamin D3 plasma are not coherent with the amount of light assimilated in our tissues; That D3 level is the quantum signal that the collision has already occurred. In us, when we swallow a pill, we get the signal but not the energy or momentum. It has long been known that light carries both energy and momentum, but no one seems to know how momentum and energy get turned into quantum information for the cell. Biology is all about preserving that information in cell form using chemicals like Vitamin D3. The momentum of light has not been so easy to describe for physics. New experimental results now suggest that the answer depends on whether or not the light can put fluid into motion: Since all cell membranes are fluid in humans this begins to make sense. If light can move a cell membrane, light slows and its momentum decreases and it exerts a pushing force on a CM; This is how D3 is made with UVB light. Clearly D3 needs a lot of momentum to be made. What happens if you have no UV light? Say you live indoors under because UVB light packs the biggest punch in our spectrum, but does an LED? Nope. Light frequencies that cannot move the cell membrane as well cannot slow the incident light at the surface and it penetrates like a missile. as the light travels the light's momentum actually increases more and speeds up into a tissue at deeper levels and it exerts a pulling force on the lattice around it. This is similar to what a bullet tract does when you get shot. This is how someone develops a disease like cancer, psoriasis and MS.
