I'm trying not to get bogged down in the minutia of the details, but instead looking to understand principles as you said. The principle I am struggling with here is how it is harmonious with our circadian biology for our skin receptors to be telling us it's cold in the spring/summer when our taste receptors, ingested electrons, and SCN are telling us its warm? Am I thinking in too black-and-white of terms here and/or am I missing the part where CT is basically good year around in our microwaved world because of its beneficial effects on our mitochondria (e.g. magnetism)?
>
> Jack--to go off your post. I'm assuming that one of the effects of living in our EMFd world is that since blue light is destroying DHA in the retina and thus our ability to yoke ourselves to the light cycles and adequately use the photoelectric effect that CT is helpful in pretty much all cases, even closer to the equator. To take this from the general to specific: you live in New Orleans and I will be moving to Phoenix. Both places have warmer temps/more sunlight most of the year. Yet, you do CT in the summer and eat vegetables? To offset the hazards of your day job like I will have to do being a student in school. And this is not a biological mismatch akin to doing VLC in a warm-adapted environment?

I can't speak for Jack, but can give you my quick take...

The presence or absence of light and magnetism are likely more dominant than temperature in circadian and seasonal signalling.

Jack loves to say that cold is "not hormetic." What does that mean? It means that cold is not some kind of stressor that requires a secondary adaptive response. It means it is beneficial fundamentally.

What is cold really? It isn't even a thing. It's just a shorthand way of referring to something that contains less heat. What is heat? It is motion of molecules or energy flow.

So when you put something cold next to something hot. What does it do. It increases energy flow from the warmer object. The cold object acts as a thermodynamic sink. Warm blooded mammals will dissipate heat in response. Regardless of season or time of day. So cold always enhances the flow of energy and tends to decrease local entropy in this way. Thermodynamic advantage. Big deal.

Cold isn't really a stressor at all. It merely withdraws heat from an adjacent system. It's only dangerous insofar as it withdraws heat from that system faster than it can compensate to maintain its function. "Cold adaptation" in mammals speeds compensatory heat production.

Warm blooded mammals will dissipate heat in response. Regardless of season or time of day. So cold always enhances the flow of energy and tends to decrease local entropy in this way. Thermodynamic advantage. Big deal.
>
> Cold isn't really a stressor at all. It merely withdraws heat from an adjacent system. It's only dangerous insofar as it withdraws heat from that system faster than it can compensate to maintain its function. "Cold adaptation" in mammals speeds compensatory heat production.

Let's explore deep cold a little more...If you make a mammal cold enough and manage to keep it alive, interesting things happen. The main threat to life in deep cold (cryogenics) seems to be not a direct thermal effect at all, but actually tissue damage related to water-ice expansion and crystallization. This is why cryogenics people are working on better forms of biological "antifreeze" to preserve organs and maybe preserve whole people, modeling what the Alaskan wood frog can do...

But the point is this. If tissue is not damaged, extreme cold at some point "overwhelms" mammalian thermogenesis and you enter a preserved state, aka cryogenesis...

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...about-infrared-and-ct.9675/page-9#post-147175](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/a-little-story-about-infrared-and-ct.9675/page-9#post-147175)

Time and entropy would seem to massively slow down or even stop when you get cold enough. Life pauses, and if not damaged can be "resumed" where it left off after being frozen in time. Jack has said that he thinks time and entropy are emergent properties of consciousness from a quantum origin. He also said that he used CT to "slow down time" in order to innovate other solutions to a bad environment...

As I mentioned in this previous post, from the NOVA cold documentary, hydrogen sulfide, extreme cold, and hypoxia together in the right circumstances seem to flip the "pause switch" to life, and _literally_ freeze time and entropy locally, as long as tissue is preserved.

On the macroscopic level, life is a thermodynamic process that seems to require the flow of time, and entropy.

Perhaps even routine cold thermogenesis by dissipating internal entropy can also dissipate internal time, relative to surroundings, even without full cryogenesis, if this all exists on a continuum. That certainly would not be "hormetic," LOL.

Thermodynamics is an older macroscopic science that does not take quantum mechanics into consideration. The quantum processes are a black box in the thermodynamic window. But all the properties of quantum signalling gases and internal magnetism (including O2 handling) would have to be in order for this to occur macroscopically.

Cold "puts the brakes on" thermal energy. Magnetism "puts the brakes on" electromagnetic energy. Cold operates on the macroscopic thermodynamic level. Magnetism operates subatomically, in quantum relativistic fashion. Oxygen, which converts deoxyhemoglobin to oxyhemoglobin, seems to be a magnetic intermediate for biology. Signalling gases like H2S likely use magnetism to adapt to different extreme environments...

Light in the form of photon can induce ionization of atoms to control and entangle them. One photon is capable of control large amounts of atoms to make them act as a quasi crystal or semiconductor. All semiconductors have to obey quantum solid state physics. This is how life jumps off the thermodynamic ledge into the quantum one.

Physicists tested this idea in the 1960's using the higher end photons in a laser. The photoelectric effect reasons that photons might ionize an atom only if they had an energy greater than a particular threshold energy corresponding to the ionization energy of the atom. In 1929, Maria Göppert-Mayer predicted theoretically that an atom might absorb two or more photons simultaneously, thus allowing an electron to transition to states unreachable by single photon absorption (Andrews 319). An atom absorbing multiple photons simultaneously might be ionized by photons with energies _less_ than the threshold energy of which Einstein spoke. Because any observable effect of this phenomenon could not be possible without a very intense beam of radiation, her prediction could not be investigated in detail until the construction of the first laser in 1960.
Ten years ago I hypothesized that the very something could be done at the extreme low end of photon power in the ultraviolet and blue range because of the higher energy these frequencies of light have. Blue light has some unique properties that allow it to tell time and to control atoms via ionization.

Physicists from MIT and the University of Belgrade recently have developed a new technique that can entangle 2,910 atoms using only a single photon — the largest number of particles that have ever been mutually entangled experimentally (previous record: 100) They have confirmed my theoretical beliefs built into my Leptin Rx.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-17T18:00:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mastering-the-basics-pathways-and-seasons.13531/post-161531>

NeilBB you were the one to bring up EPR a long time ago and I was hush hush but pretty stoked you were making connections way before most others. I was especially pleased because you were a MD in a specialty closely aligned with mine and you were becoming a starfish because, you were disenchanted with where our profession was headed as I was.

For the newbies, the [entanglement](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_entanglement) concept was first proposed in a 1935 paper by [Albert Einstein](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_Einstein), [Boris Podolsky](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boris_Podolsky) and [Nathan Rosen](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nathan_Rosen): two or more particles may be correlated in such a way that any change to one will simultaneously change the other, no matter how far apart they may be. For instance, if one atom in an entangled pair were somehow made to spin clockwise, the other atom would instantly be known to spin counterclockwise, even though the two may be physically separated by thousands of miles. What chemicals in us, specifically in mitochondria have these unique electron spin states? Free radicals called superoxide, H2O2, and the hydroxyl free radical. There are others, like H2S, NOS, CO and even CO2 to a degree.

Neil has always quietly wonder why I hold Einstein so dear when it was he who said entanglement was quackery. It was the basis of his 30 yr dispute with Neils Bohr over QED. In fact, Einstein dismissed the idea as “”spooky action at a distance"

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-17T18:55:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mastering-the-basics-pathways-and-seasons.13531/post-161544>

In the human brain multiple light photons of multiple frequencies coexist. What effect does this have on biologic atoms of nitrogen, carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen? Let us look at what physics has already proven in abiotic atoms.

When a single photon collides with a ground state cesium atom, the atom can absorb the photon via electronic transitions to energy levels higher than the ground state by d E=hb w . Now we need to exam some math. The mathematics of quantum mechanical wave functions tell us, selection rules govern (think frequency modulation) these transitions such that the change in angular momentum in single photon transitions is d l = +1, -1.
Remember photons are the FORCE carrier for light. This means even though they have no mass they due have a force associated with them. Force in electricity is measured by amperes, and in electron currents by volts. Think photoelectric effect. Light can go between all of these things by Einstein's photoelectric effect.

Thus, an electron in a ground state (s-state) may transition by one photon only to a higher excited state (p-state). When an atom absorbs _two_ photons simultaneously, as happens in tissues quite often, the electron will (must change by QED laws) change angular momentum by d l = +2, 0, or -2. This has massive implications for biology. Why? Since each photon has angular momentum of +1 or –1. This allows a ground state (s-state) electron to transition to another s-state or what we can call a transition state (d-state): These are states out-of-reach by single photon absorption only. This means all biologic processes are designed around transition states of metastability. It gets very interesting when you add more light frequencies. If a third photon interacts with the same atom's electrons soon after the two-photon process, the third photon maybe able to ionize the atom. A ground state (s-state) electron may not transition to a p-state by two photon absorption. Don't get lost yet...........

Your brain is built of atoms that are designed to be fully capable of absorbing massive amounts of frequencies all at once. That major chemical is DHA and all the proteins it allows to be present within your cell membranes. DHA specifically selects these optical protein switches based upon the frequencies they resonate at. So what happens in DHA? When absorbing multiple photons simultaneously, an atom will proceed to an intermediate state corresponding to something in physics called a characteristic "eigenstate". Eigenstate is a synonym for a "virtual state or an avatar if you like." These virtual states, however, are not exactly eigenstates; Instead, they correspond to no specific n or l state. Instead, they are merely superpositions of waves of the photon and electron. This is how memory and consciousness manifest. They are 100% quantum mechanical creations of light's interaction with DHA and the proteins it allows in cell membranes.

No population of electrons in atoms is capable of transitioning to the virtual state without specific frequencies of light present to allow that transition. Physicists do it with big atoms and powerful lasers.........your brain does it with extreme low powered UV photons and nitrogen. The lifetime of a virtual state is very short, relative to eigenstates.

In my view the closer the virtual state is to an actual eigenstate, the longer the lifetime of the virtual state persists and this is what humans call consciousness. DHA is the only lipid capable of bring the virtual and eigenstate close enough together in time to allow it to manifest. ### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-18T04:48:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mastering-the-basics-pathways-and-seasons.13531/post-161589>

So why is DHA the critical piece to all EUKARYOTES. The DHA double bonds consist of a sigma bond and a pi bond. By subtracting 80 kcal/mole (the sigma bond energy) from 145 kcal/mole (the C=C bond energy) we can calculate that the pi bond has a dissociation energy of about 65 kcal/mole. This is much less than the sigma bond energy, and therefore pi bonds are more reactive than sigma bonds. This means that, all things being equal, the **pi electron bonds of DHA are more likely to undergo _activation by light_.** Your brain is just like a star. It works optically and photonically. This is why optokinetics works to change neural circuits. The brain is all about light because 35-40% of all lipids in membranes in the brain are DHA. Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a mechanism describing energy transfer between two [chromophores](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chromophore). In the [conjugated](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conjugated_system) _chromophores_ like DHA, the [electrons jump between energy levels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Molecular_electron_transition) that are extended [pi orbitals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pi_bond), created by a series of alternating [single and double bonds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical_bond). A conjugated system is a system of connected [p-orbitals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/P-orbital) with [delocalized electrons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delocalized_electron) in compounds with alternating single and multiple [bonds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Covalent_bond), which in general may lower the overall energy of the molecule and increase stability. The largest artificial conjugated systems today are found in [graphene](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graphene), [graphite](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graphite), [conductive polymers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conductive_polymer), and [carbon nanotubes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_nanotube). The most important ones on this planet exist in your brain right now. It is time for you all to realize why I am pounding the table for a marine chain laden template in a microwave world. Most of these artificial chromophores are now being used in quantum computer design and in artificial intelligence with helium as their light emitting atom. Helium is now the one element all governments on Earth covet. Why? Its spectrum allows massive control by optical resonance.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-18T06:23:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mastering-the-basics-pathways-and-seasons.13531/post-161600>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=161595)
>
> [View attachment 1748](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/1748/)

When we get to biophotons........then the story gets deep.

**What are Spectral Lines and how do cells detect biophotons? What spectrum are they using?**

When a beam of white light passed through a narrow slit and fall on prism, the beam divides in many colored band or line. These color lines and bands are known as **spectral lines** and type of image of color is known as spectrum.

Spectrum can be three types.

1.  Emission spectrum
2.  Absorption spectrum
3.  Molecular spectrum

****Emission spectrum****
When an object gets excited in anything alive, it emits some radiation called mitogenic radiation or a bio-photon. These radiations can be divided in to spectral line, but to date we have no way to do this on living tissues. We can do it on star light. Star light can be divided into spectral lines after passing the light through the spectroscope. Such type of spectra is known as emission spectrum of the emitting substance. We can tell a lot about the atoms emitting the light from its emission spectrum. For example; when electricity passed through the glass tube filled with hydrogen gas at low pressure, gives off blue light. So what do you think the DC current in cells might do when H20 is charge separated during wakefulness? Remember H+ is loaded in cells and mitochondria. When this light is passed through a prism (Pink floyd), four narrow bands of emission spectra of are observed against a black background.

An object can be excited by

-   Heat object at high temperature. (leptin resistance)
-   with pressure, pass electricity in a discharge tube (Archimedes principle)
-   By electric discharge in a metallic filament. (DC current)
-   By other light photons (opticogenetics)

Was there any underlying reason for each element to generate a unique spectrum? Johannes Balmer studied the optical spectrum of hydrogen and found a pattern in the wavelengths of its emission lines.

Expressed in nanometers, the wavelengths of Hydrogen are

374.9 377.0 379.7 383.4 388.8 396.9 410.1 433.9 486.0 656.1
Balmer found that a simple formula reproduced these wavelengths:

n^2
lambda (n) = 364.5 nm \* ------------- , n = 3, 4, 5, ....
n^2 - 4

The result was curious as much as it was strange. As a reward for his work, this set of visible lines in the hydrogen spectrum are called the "Balmer series". This series confused many physicists in the early 20th century.
Paschen, in the infrared, measured lines with wavelengths

866.2 874.8 886.0 901.2 922.6 954.3 1004.6 1093.5 1281.4 1874.5
which matched the series
n^2
lambda (n) = 820.5 nm \* ------------- , n = 4, 5, 6, ....
n^2 - 9

and Lyman, in the ultraviolet, a series of lines with wavelengths
91.9 92.1 92.3 92.6 93.0 93.7 94.9 97.2 102.5 121.5
corresponding to the series
n^2
lambda (n) = 91.1 nm \* ------------- , n = 2, 3, 4, ....
n^2 - 1

In fact, scientists soon realized that hydrogen's spectrum could be broken down into a large number of series, each of which had wavelengths obeying a formula like:

n^2
lambda (n,k) = (limit) \* ------------- , n = k+1, k+2, k+3, ...
n^2 - k^2

The Lyman series corresponds to**k=1**with a limit of 91.1 nm, the Balmer series to**k=2**with a limit of 364.5 nm, and so forth.
But what did it all mean? Why were there regular patterns in these emission line wavelengths?

Within a short period of time, other scientists found similar patterns in other portions of the electromagnetic spectrum.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-18T06:23:58+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-18T06:24:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mastering-the-basics-pathways-and-seasons.13531/post-161602>

Another curious feature of the hydrogen spectrum appears if one writes down the **frequencies** of the emission lines, rather than the wavelengths. In the Lyman and Balmer series, we have (in units of 10^14 Hz)

Lyman lines: 31.899 31.498 30.760 29.165 24.609
Balmer lines: 7.533 7.292 6.891 6.152 4.557
Can you find pairs of frequencies which, when added together, yield another observed frequency?
This is called the **Ritz recombination principle**, after the scientist (Ritz) who first noticed it.

Again, why are there such simple patterns in the spectrum of hydrogen? What does it all mean?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-18T06:30:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mastering-the-basics-pathways-and-seasons.13531/post-161604>

The measurement and analysis of the visible hydrogen spectrum is a standard experiment performed by nearly every undergraduate physics major. Students measure the four visible lines of the Balmer series, and the analysis includes verifying Balmer’s formula and measuring the Rydberg constant. The hydrogen spectrum experiment is ideal because the spectral data were key ingredients in the **_development of quantum mechanics_** and can be understood using a simple relation.

The spectra of other atoms are _not as simple to analyze_. Adding just one more proton to the nucleus and one more orbiting electron to the atom for helium results in the com- plications of shielding and the effects of indistinguishable particles on the energy level structure. Helium is special because it is here where frequency become chaotic........and chaos is what life uses to live. When frequency become adynamic.......life goes extinct.

In fact, it is why all energy flows must be done via convection. The laws of physics require it. Quantum criticality is a new idea based upon old Russian ideas (Bose Einstein condensates too! BEC) that describes the actions and behavior of electrons in large molecules when they occupy the exotic state. Einstein kept the idea of BEC's but they could only be made by physicists in cold dry environments. I knew deep in my heart that a BEC had to be at the core of life in a warm wet environment. Low and behold along comes several harvard physicists in 2007 and Jim Al Khalili and some crazy ideas about leafs being quantum computers that control life with nitrogen. I stopped dead in my tracks. I knew I just found the holy grail. Light interacts with nitrogen to control atoms like carbon. This happens in our eyes, guts and mitochondria. I saw the blueprint. This happens in leafs and in mitochondria. This exotic warm wet state sits at the knife's edge between conduction and insulation. Read Khalili's book, "Life at the Edge" immediately. Its words and data proved my insights, yet again were spot on from ten years ago. I had an intuition and a conviction about disease reversal. I used Einstein's work to give me that intuition and I bio-hacked that idea fully expecting it to fail.........and it did not. Now I am writing about it because I know i am correct. I am out of the closet. When these organic molecules are conductors, some of their bound electrons are able to move freely under the influence of an electric field. By contrast, when these molecules are insulators, the electrons are not free to move. This allows light to alter ionization potentials of atoms so they can freely tunnel and entangle across cell membranes to carry out the job of living.

Nothing excites a compost pile like peeing on it. All that nitrogen wakes up the bacteria and the compost pile heats up nicely. The garbage quickly turns into a nice soil amendment.

### DavidL — 2015-04-19T21:12:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mastering-the-basics-pathways-and-seasons.13531/post-161732>

Other books on light:
True Chromotherapy (The first Professional Textbook Written Exclusively On Chromotherapy) by Ernest Stevens, Published in 1938 by The Rainbow Publishers. Much of this book is dedicated to diseases and their treatments. It also includes information on Chromatic Attraction, Infra-Red Rays, Chromo Stimulants, Chromo-Chemistry, and Sun and Color Charged Water.

Another Ernest Stevens book worth investigating is called Vibrations - Their Principles, published in 1920 by Stevens Light and Color Research Studios.

George Starr White, M.D. wrote an interesting book entitled, The Finer Forces of Nature in Diagnosis and Therapy, published in 1929. This book now is a re-issue from Sun Publishing Co. Albuquerque, N.M. Some topics covered in the book include: Moon Light – Influences All Life, Bio-Dynamo-Chromatic Therapy, Rithmo-Duo-Color Therapy, Color Indications in Health and Un-Health, and Magnetic Energy.

Light and Color, by W.J. Colyville was published in 1914 by Macoy Publishing and Masonic Supply Co. This little book was written by a friend of Dr. Edwin Babbitt and it summarizes much of Babbitt’s work.

A jewel of a book published in 1938 is Spectro-Biology by Maryla De. Chrapowicki. In the forward of the book she writes, that her first goal of the book is “to establish a definite proof that light is the only biological factor which controls every aspect of physical existence.”

He had me over to his house, where he plied me with a rad wine cellar to see what I KNEW, as I think it was a little unusual to find a med student such as myself to taken by those ideas. >
> I didn't edit any of this post due to time constraints. Don't reply with your intellegence, we NEED your awareness!

Thanks for sharing your thoughts and being candid. Now I will too. Honestly, I'm thinking no one is going to answer your post because they'll feel like their 'stepping in the dung' if they were to get involved and try to reply to your post, as most may simple come believe you've got yourself convinced and overfocused on Tesla. Listen, I'm a humble person. I don't let any sort of pride get in my way and I'm the first to admit I'm wrong about something. In a strange way, this is what drives me more and more to learn and learn why the Scientific Method is such an awesome tool. I'm the first to admit I was wrong when someone shows me the evidence. I'm never one to say 'I'm right' about all of the answers about life, the universe and everything. It's quite the opposite actually. Science is just a tool of observation as to what fits best for explanations, and I have an attitude of pulling back and putting together puzzle pieces to see how they fit when gifted with new knowledge as that set of knowledge gets introduced into the previous knowledge already understood. It's often hard for people to throw away notions in science for a new notion. Again, this is where evidence rules and the Scientific Method is your friend.

One can say they 'believe' that a hypothesis, this theory, that theory, etc. is true. Truth is core, is it not? No one wants egg on their face for being wrong after a lifetime of studying this or that person, looking for this or that datapoint, etc. It's also why I raise an eyebrow someone can't back up a claim with evidence, even if I want to 'knee-jerk dismiss'. Rather, when a scientist cannot backup claims, or cannot show the proper links through an idea set, but it's still is a solid explanation regardless, I just throw on my Sherlock Holmes lenses and look for the disconnects--usually it's MY OWN lack of knowledge that hasn't filled in one link to another. But I also have to be careful to not arrive at 'direct causation' once the links are established, since a link is usually not evidence anyway.

Often, I think we live in a time when a great number of smart people outright dismiss concepts, ideas or hypotheses because of have acquiring a huge knowledge set, a specialized knowledge set, or is simply thinking about how the entire construct works in one's head and how a new idea might push to hard against what's alread established. I view this as 'the pyramid' or having a solid basis of a knowledge set at the base and when you go higher up the pyramid, you get new hypotheses and potentials that are not as well-tested compared to the base-theories closer to the bottom of the pyramid that has more reliance due to more trials over time that fit the observations better.

This is in contrast to many scientists that speculate ideas that are not easily testable in-practice and here in reality to 'show the evidence', like Stephen Hawkings comes to mind. You can be a genius all day long and I appriciate looking into that genius, but the value of the genius isn't worth that much if it cannot be backed up with real-world evidence. So it can be very tempting to just outright dismiss others. This is why I must always go out of my way to never develop **an attitude of dismissal** because I always believe I could be lacking a knowledge set, ideas or not understanding the concepts behind the ideas well enough. Finding people that keep an open mind in 2015 is getting harder an harder to do, but also because the highly-intelligent among us are arriving at a different picture of the cosmos and the internal workings of life. That's fine and expected and desired. Eventually, we'll all be able to figure this all out.

Also, based on many well-intended and highly-intelligent scientists I have met along life's way can let their own pride get the best of them and get super-overfocused to the point of glorifying OCD. This sets up many amazing brains into an "on/off" mechanical state... over-compartmentalization of ideas shoving these ideas into one box and other ideas into other boxed, leading to dismissing ideas for the gigantic picture they've painted of the universe. Funny enough, humanity might also benefit from those brains if something can be seen from a whole, new POV (point of view). It's why creativity is king in my book no matter the source, even if the source is not friendly or doesn't appear to 'fit' into my knowledge set at a given moment.

Color-coding...yeah in an indirect sense. The specific light wavelength is what the plant desires for optimal use. If you look at photosynthesis, plants back-reflect more green than any other color. They don't wanna 'eat' green as to why we're all seeing it. Now bounce down the spectrum a few colors from green and you get yellow, orange, and then red. Plants love redlight. This makes sense because the wavelength is slower with red. But if you start at purple or even higher into the UV, you're storing more power because the wavelength is getting more compressed. Plus, I felt AMAZING and thought (subconsciously) it was all about food. Wow- just saying that shows me how much I've learned and grown in 6-7 months. But I digress...Here is my question: Am I paying the price for my summer ketosis? Have I inadvertently made my mitochondria, which have suffered so very much by my mismatched existence, worse without realizing it?

I'm going out on a limb and guessing that some people on this forum have experienced what I'm currently going through. If so, I hope you will chime in and provide some much needed guidance. I need a path back to crabs, not carbs!

### Psyche — 2016-01-04T16:52:20+0200

@[lilreddgirl](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/10648/) Thanks for your response. My nnEMF exposure has not increased that I know of, but is always a source of concern and first place to look. Over the weekend, I went on a hike and it was COLD. The tips of my right index finger and thumb went white and would not get any blood flow. I was a bit concerned about it. I scoured the forum, and found this very thing was talked about in a Reynoud's thread ([https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/raynaud-thread.4235/page-2](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/raynaud-thread.4235/page-2)).

I am most likely magnesium deficinet, and the irony is that I don't supplement MG because it's hydrophilic and I am probably not hydrated enough for supplementation to be doing any good. However, I will start using MG oil, and supplements while focusing on hydration. It will be interesting to see if it helps.

The short light cycle is definitely a big player overall. I remember reading somewhere that Jack says you either embrace the cold or chase the sun in winter. I'm learning I have problems if I do neither

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-05T15:28:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mastering-the-basics-pathways-and-seasons.13531/post-185470>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=179995)
>
> You can bio hack light easy if youre smart.........few are.

Cold increases dopamine............using purple light. All hormones correlate to dopamine and UV light assimilation. January webinar 2016 shows those links in detail.

We don't simply create probabilities, nature does with her dealing of light frequencies to our surfaces. What she deals is rarely constant on a daily or seasonal basis. We are designed to spot her trends to guide us in each cell. When powerful trends are found we stop living based upon probabilities and we begin living life with certainties. Nature favors the dynamics of correlated novelties. In this way, relationships of things to others things in nature becomes more important than how each were are fundamentally created.
Not everybody can assimilate light in the optimal fashion. We have various surfaces that react to a combination of light frequencies. Depending on the light spectrum light that penetrates into our surfaces between 0.1 mm to a great depth and the magic of light happens in the top layers. If your top layers cannot convert this energy well due to a e.g. badly composed cell membranes, lack of melanin, or faulty tunneling in your respiratory chain, bad photochemical reactions happen fast because of how the photoelectric works fundamentally. Most people forget that the gut is highly sensitive to light information because of how the microbiome releases light. Bacteria release 5000 times more light than our cells do. On a relative basis are gut sees massive amounts of light frequencies as soon as we eat something. In this way, with any problem that develops in the gut, your reaction to light will be different. There could be tons of reasons why alternative reactions to light occurs in this fashion. Light always plays a role and how light works and when is not well understood. When frequencies are altered for any reason, disruptions of clock genes can happen in no time by tiny things most people are not aware of. When bad reactions occur while living an outdoor life in perfect light, you know something is wrong in your environment for your particular cellular costume. That is your cells talking to you! The key is, do you pay attention to these trends properly? If you miss it, it is likely because your dopamine levels in your eye and brain is suboptimal for some reason and you need to be aware of it.

### John Schumacher — 2023-01-28T20:37:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mastering-the-basics-pathways-and-seasons.13531/post-319259>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=161557)
>
> Hint Hint
>
> Yes blue light is everyone's big issue. Why? NADH is a fluorophore in cytochrome one. What is its detector? FADH2. In cytochrome two. What are all flavins responsive to in mammals? blue light frequencies. Riboflavin is the base of all flavins. Guess what it is really bad news? All cryptochromes that control circadian biology are run by flavin proteins in them. So this is how blue light ruins your cytochromes. Blue light also releases cytochrome C in position 3 to cause mitochondrial swelling. It also destroys DHA levels in cell membranes lowering your ability to load cholesterol with electrons. The redox potential is the measure of “the current of your body’s electric charge”, coming from the battery of life, which is water, which is contained within the body’s semiconductors within cells and tissues loaded with collagen and cholesterol. Water is not only life’s battery, but it also acts as liquid crystalline semiconductor in the quantum cell. It is recharged by sunlight. The redox potential is a measure of the electrical potential across your cell membranes. DHA is the key to maintaining that charge. The bigger the gradient is, or becomes, the more alive and well you remain, while the smaller the gradient becomes, the more likely low energy and disease will result. Blue light lowers DHA in CM and in your brain. When DHA is gone what else is gone? It also LOWERS you melatonin levels in your brain. This allows you to collect more positive charged protons in your brain further discharging your mammalian battery. The less DHA it has, the lower ability you get to capture electrons from outside or inside your body. It also makes your gut micriobiome more simplified because it destroy the anoxic environment (nitrogen link) it needs to communicate with cytochrome one (nitrogen link). Remember your mitochondria used to be a bacteria and it is desigend to communicate with cytochrome 1 via the NADH and NAD+ levels. So when cytochrome 1 is broken by blue light/nnEMF, and it is in most blue light toxic people (insert Mr. Moore) you get increased blue light toxicity and all its collateral damage known as neolithic disease. This changes the spectrum emitted from NADH. It means the detector in FMN or FADH2 cant see the correct light frequency any more. Signaling and ECT is changed forevery and induces more collateral damage in mtDNA that codes for cytochrome proteins. You suddenly believe you need fats 100% of the time. Sound like anyone you know? Nothing matters more than fixing your local environment when you inhabit a planet engrossed in a 24/7 microwaved blue lit world. Todays' Earth is a alien as Mars is to us today. This is why Mr. Moore and many others using ketosis are failing...........and you need to understand why he is. I am just the dude with the flashlight showing you why they might be dead wrong.

Preaching with a choir - _now_

Mechanisms of blue light-induced eye hazard and protective measures: a review
[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332220307708](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332220307708)

-   The retina and [ocular surface](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/ocular-surface) are vulnerable to blue light hazard.
-   The main mechanisms of hazard are [oxidative stress](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/oxidative-stress) and mitochondrial [apoptosis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/programmed-cell-death)

Blue light [phototherapy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/phototherapy) is an effective method to lower serum [bilirubin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/bilirubin) level of [neonatal jaundice](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/neonatal-jaundice) patients in clinical practice. However, the potential hazard of short-wave light to eye has aroused much concern. Some studies have indicated that high-energy blue light can penetrate the cornea and lens and reach the retina directly, causing photochemical damage to the retina; this is called blue light hazard. Photochemical damage to the rod cells via blue light hazard has long been recognized in 1966. Since then, numerous experiments have confirmed blue light hazard and found that the degree of damage is related to various factors such as the intensity of blue light received by the eye, distance of illumination, direction of the line of sight, and spectrum of the light source. Javier Vicente-Tejedor et al found that removing the blue component could protect the function and morphology of the retina significantly using blue-blocking filters.

Blue light (441 nm)-induced retinal lesions resulting from photochemical damage and not from thermal injury were first reported in 1978. Mounting evidence suggests that overexposure to blue light induces a signiﬁcant increase in [ROS](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/reactive-oxygen-species) production, which contributes to the loss of [photoreceptors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/photoreceptor), [lipid peroxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/lipid-peroxidation) and cell [apoptosis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/programmed-cell-death).

_The synergistic effects of blue light and N-retinylidene-N-retinylethanolamine (A2E) and photoreversal of bleaching will further aggravate photochemical damage and cause the activation of inflammatory reactions, DNA damage and inhibition of mitochondria and [lysosome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/lysosome) function._

Emerging evidence indicates that blue light phototoxicity is not only limited to the retina; it also damages the [ocular surface](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/ocular-surface) by [oxidative stress](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/oxidative-stress) and the inflammatory response. Furthermore, some scientific data suggest the transparency of crystalline lens becomes reduced with age, causing a progressive increase in absorbance within the blue light spectrum. Previous epidemiological studies have indicated that exposure to blue light may be associated with the development of ARM. However, a meta-analysis in 2018 showed no association between sunlight exposure and the risk of AMD, a finding that is consistent with the position statement of CIE.

[Docosahexaenoic acid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/docosahexaenoic-acid) (DHA) is an [unsaturated fatty acid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/unsaturated-fatty-acid) in RPE cells. Singlet oxygen and hydrogen peroxide could mediate the [lipid peroxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/lipid-peroxidation) of DHA and produce HOHA. HOHA generally causes cell apoptosis by destroying mitochondria and lysosomes. Additionally, HOHA is a precursor to CEP. The CEP immune response leads to the production of interferon-γ (IFN-γ) and interleukin-17 (Il-17) by CEP-specific T cells, causing cellular inflammation \[[59](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332220307708#bib0295)\].

RPE cell tight junctions form a blood-retinal barrier between the choroid and retinal cells. The blood-retinal barrier plays a key role in the transport of nutrients, water, and electrolytes. Blue light decreases the barrier function by downregulating the [scaffold protein](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/scaffold-protein) Zonulae occludentes-1 (zo-1) expression through the PKC-ζ pathway. Furthermore, oxidative stress, produced by LED exposure, upregulated the unfolded protein response genes. It decreases trigeminal neural [cell viability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cell-viability), impacts the primary culture of neural cells, and increases cell death.

It is widely recognized that blue light is the most harmful to the retina and ocular surface. The mechanisms involved in blue light injury mainly include the oxidative stress response, mitochondrial apoptosis, [inflammatory apoptosis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/pyroptosis), mitochondrial apoptosis, and DNA damage. Additionally, blue light affects optical [nerve conduction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/nerve-conduction) by inhibiting mitochondrial activity.

[Lipofuscin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/lipofuscin) and [rhodopsin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/rhodopsin) are important media in RPE and photoreceptor cell light damage. Blue light upregulates ROS production, causes mitochondrial and lysosomal dysfunction and disrupts the epithelial barrier, resulting in apoptosis.

Blue light Damage:
The activation of the mitochondria-dependent apoptosis pathway, caspase-dependent apoptotic pathway, p53 pathway, and the [ER stress](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/endoplasmic-reticulum-stress) (ERS) and [unfolded protein response](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/unfolded-protein-response) (UPR) signaling pathways play a major role in blue light damage. These pathological changes may result in various eye diseases, such as diabetic retinopathy, glaucoma, and dry diseases.​

# Thread: life as a daisy journal (kimeka)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 24
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kimeka — 2015-03-30T00:56:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-159794>

A little of me. I've been depressed for a long long time. Since a few days before my 8th birthday my grandmother died. Before that, I remember being happy. After that day, I feel my life was never seemed to be as happy. I always felt like I was not wanted and just an annoyance.
Around 10 years ago, it was suggested I had PCOS (polycystic ovarian syndrome.) But was never told anything more about it at that time. In 2007, we decided to try to have a baby. I tried acupuncture and saw a fertility specialist in 2008. Missed timing, I got pregnant on my own. Two weeks later, I miscarried. A few months later, I became pregnant again and switched doctors. The new one gave me progesterone shots for 8 weeks. The whole pregnancy I was under a lot of stress due to work. I was a little overweight and had high blood pressure. In the last two months, I had pre-eclampsia and was retaining water. In February 2010, I had a c-section. My daughter is healthy and now five years old.
I lost the 30 lbs I gained during pregnancy. However my sister in law always suspected I had a thyroid problem. When I would bring this up with any doctor I saw, I would get only tsh tested and be told it was normal. Nothing more would be done.
In 2012, I slowly was gaining weight. I tried limiting what I ate and tried to exercise but I was always exhausted. I would literally fall asleep if I sat still too long. I was even falling asleep at work. Throughout the year I went from 175 to 235 lbs. I was ended up wearing my maternity pants because I couldn't even fit in my pants. As December of that year came by I was having a hard time walking a few feet without having to stop and rest for a few minutes. I was thinking I had developed a respiratory infection. After almost two weeks, I went to the doctor. She sent me to the ER. I thought it was going to be a quick trip and back home with a script. I was wrong. I was to stay a week. They ran several tests and got my blood pressure under control. I was told my heart was pumping at 21%. They even checked my arteries and found no blockages. I was also put on water pills and lost 15 lbs of water weight during that week. Due to my husband's pressuring they gave me a recommendation to see an endo to check for thyroid problems.
After getting out, I was given a defibrillator vest to wear for 3 months. During those three months, I saw the endo and a sleep dr. I was diagnosed with sleep apnea. After a sleep test, I remember being fully awake and alert for the first time in who knows how long. I eventually got a a cpap machine and have used it daily. I'm uncomfortable not using it. When I saw the endo, I was told I have hashimoto's. She never went into and blew off any suggestions about possible adrenal fatigue. She also made me see a nutritionist who pushed grains. We stopped seeing them. She prescribed me synthroid which I felt did nothing for me. When I asked if it was possible it had an allergen in it (wheat/corn/soy) she blew it off as if I was all in my head.
By August of 2013, I had a visit with the endo and I was experiencing heartburn, headache and anxiety attacks. This sent me to the ER only to be sent home with a pain pill and told to take an antacid.
It would come and go. I would sometimes go a week or two feeling fine and thinking maybe the er doc was right and it was just a bug that went through my system but it would come back again for a couple days. I remember losing 20 lbs during those months. It was becoming depressing. In November 2013, my sister in law recommended her doctor. I saw her and she tested my thyroid. Asked me tons of questions. She also tested me for allergies and sensitivities. She suspected low stomach acid. She told me to take L-glutamine to help heal my stomach and do the specific carbohydrate diet for a month. I was feeling better within 48 hours. I couldn't believe it. She changed the synthroid to tirosint and added t3 since my t4 wasn't converting to t3. However when she did this, my anxiety ramped up. The heartburn and anxiety seemed to correspond with full moons. Now it seems to correspond with right after I had my period. My general dr thought it was a gallbladder problem and was thinking of removing it. The new dr told me to avoid the foods the allergy test ranked high. This helped a lot.
I aksed to try NDT and she prescribed westhroid. The first day I felt great after that I got chest pains and anxiety. I stopped. We agreed that I had to get the adrenals on track first and raise my iron levels. I tried again last month and I was doing ok for almost three weeks but severe heartburn came back. I'm considering a new dr as I feel this one isn't one the same track with me at this moment plus I need one with more flexible hours.
Currently I am not on any thyroid medicine. I think when I get a new dr. I'll start from scratch with this one.
I'm also been trying to find a new job as the one I have has a hostile environment. I have a boss that encourages negative behaviour and punishes good. When I was pregnant, he gave my job away but due to 90% of the employs there that complained and not leave him alone. He was pushed into hiring me back. Ever since I was in the hospital, he has been nice to me since but has not changed the tension and mood of the place. There are days that I want to just walk out if it wasn't the fear of not being able to pay my bills let alone for food. We're trying to so hard and want to get out of the basement of my parents' house. It's so damn depressing that I wish there was somewhere I could go and just scream and throw things. I'm so frustrated. I know when I was in the hospital I was at the bottom and I could only go up from there but clearly I'm feeling like when I try to climb up I'm climbing a smooth slick wall. I'm tired of living in a basement with no natural light. I'm tired of living in debt. I'm tired of my horrible job. I'm also tired of being sick. My only bit of happiness is my husband and daughter. I want us out of here. I want to not having to dread going to work everyday. I want to wake up with a smile and not be because its a day I have off. Am I crazy to think this?

### kimeka — 2015-03-30T02:06:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-159799>

I also forgot to mention that after seeing the nutritionist because of blood tests said I was pre-diabetic I decided to go paleo. I lost 40 lbs on it until the end of August 2013 when I was having the food sensitivities, heartburn and anxiety that I lost an additional 20 lbs.
I stopped taking T3 in July and since then I went from 150 back to 170-175. Very much to my disappointment.
When it comes to food I feel like every time I turn around I have to stop eating something. I'm a finnicky eater and already a limited on things I like to eat but it's like every time I turn around I have to stop eating something. My food list gets smaller and smaller. My husband says I should eat more fats but I'm scared to eat more fats due to pain below the ribcage. Which makes me think of the dr telling me about my gallbladder and its possible removal due to gall stones. I also ran my 23andme results through nutrahacker for a free sample and it mentioned I would benefit from a low fat diet. I have no clue what to do with that. I hate being hungry during meals and hate feeling that I can't eat anything. I also feel that my eating disorder will rear its ugly head. When I was 17, I weighed 95 lbs for a 5'2" girl. I barely ate and if I did I binged. No throwing it up and I rarely ate good food. Mostly junk. My dad would only cook homemade pizza or tacos. My mom cooked a few things as she hated to cook and still does to this day. Granted nowadays she cooks prepackages meals. I was left to find my own meals which was hard when I was in school. After my grandmother died, I had no breakfast and no lunch and if I didn't like what my mom was cooking, I was left to fend for myself. I survived on junk food. I remember my sophmore year of high school I transferred schools because my parents wanted to keep my brother in private school and I was sent to public school. I met a girl there who became a good friend and shared half her lunch with me so I wouldn't be hungry. When I found her on facebook, I sent her a message saying thank you for being my friend, helping me to not feel so alone in a new school and having the kindness to share a lunch with someone who didn't have lunch for herself let alone any money to purchase anything for lunch.

### Lahelada — 2015-03-30T02:16:33+0300

Hi Kimeka,
Like the name. Welcome and no,you are not crazy thinking this.

As far as the baaement flat is concerned some here have elected to sleep in theirs to sleep better. So it is not all bad. Important though that during daytime you get out in the sun as much as you can.

### kimeka — 2015-03-30T03:08:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-159802>

Every time I apply and don't get a call back or I go on an interview and don't even hear back from them, I try to tell myself that this might not be the right time. Maybe the next time will be the right time. My husband suggests I start my own business but I tell him that I want it to be something I could be passionate about but I have no clue what that could be. My job has sucked creativity out of me and any passion I had about anything I liked to do. I get an idea but it doesn't spark anything in me to make me do it. Let alone I have no clue to make it different from the million of other people who are doing the same thing.

Lahelada, a hug right back to you from way up here.

### caroline — 2015-03-30T05:58:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-159813>

Thankyou Kimeka for all you have shared. As Lah has said - being in a basement isn't such a bad thing ...just get outside as much as possible. A lot of us wish we had a basement to sleep in!

What do you work at? Your DH suggests you start your own business ...what does he have in mind? what does he see you doing?

Your daughter and husband are everything .... you can and will move forward. It may be one baby step at a time - but you have taken the biggest step ....you have found jackkruse.com!

Dr. K. always tells us ..... we all have to help ourself - no one can do it for us. It only happens when we are sick and tired of being sick and tired.

Please keep reading and asking questions ...this place is your life line now.

### Inger — 2015-03-30T10:14:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-159830>

OMG... Kimeka that reads like so much struggle.......
Is your husbands not working, so you maybe could work less and stay home more?
You need to be outside anytime you are able to...... I think you have a garden? That is already very good news  you can earth and tan
but working full time and being a mom and wife is IMHO pretty much a disaster for health (for most of us women these days)
If you cant work less.... it still will help a lot to do anything you can - it will always help.
Step by step. Use candles instead of fake light. Go outside. Tan nude on the weekends!
Even if everything seems dark, if we start to do the baby step into the light everything will get better.... slowly
You are so happy to have your daughter and husband... skin contact is so healing... you can get as much of that as you want

### nicld — 2015-03-30T19:55:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-159855>

What are you and your husbands professions? Maybe time to take a drastic step and find someplace completely different if you can (not everyone can).

### kimeka — 2015-03-30T20:41:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-159859>

Can't really make a drastic change. I, myself, have barely any money. Today, I'm having a hard day. Barely slept due to heartburn during the night. Only to have my mother yelling and screaming at me just a little while ago. I'm so tired of feeling this sad and helpless.

### nicld — 2015-03-30T21:55:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-159862>

Sometimes in the darkest days you have to make the hardest decisions to save yourself.

My husband, dd and I rented a place with my mother and little sister along time ago. NEVER AGAIN!!!! It was hell and my mother and I did not talk to each other for almost 9 months (some of it while living together) so I feel for you.

There are jobs up by me.

### caroline — 2015-03-31T02:17:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-159877>

thinking of you Kimeka and sending you a big hug .... xo

### Inger — 2015-03-31T12:05:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-159914>

Kimeka, do not despair. There is always a way! You just need to see it. You will, if you start looking........ I know this for sure!
I have thought I am helpless too.... but then I started to look... and now I am so glad I did  My issue also was money...
Just never give up! Focus on finding solutions... eat loads of seafood and run away from blue light/technology so your brain works better and you will see more
throw out the TV... ditch all social media etc etc... use the little time you have wisely... it will go upwards
(((hugs)))

### Inger — 2015-03-31T12:06:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-159915>

Do everything you CAN do.. it is all that matters. Watch your life change...

from Jacks facebook site...;

> Are we willing to release all that we are, for all that we could be? It is hard to see what you don't know is even there. The eye cannot see what the mind does not know. What's interesting is that our connectivity to the virtual world now outweighs the connection with the natural world. That connection is driving us to an inconvenient truth. We are losing huge energy flows to brains and immune systems as we embrace modern life, and its going to force into a no win situation if we don't reconnect with the natural world soon.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-31T15:43:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-159932>

I understand how you feel. You're not alone. I'm glad you've found us. That was so thoughtful to message the girl who used to share her lunch with you, obviously you are a kind and loving person. It's good to have you here. \*hugs\*

### kimeka — 2015-04-13T03:52:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-161044>

It's been a while since I last wrote. I'm sitting in front of my laptop wearing some ice packs as I type this. Since I last wrote, I had an interview which in turn got me a trial day. Have not heard back on whether they are hiring me or not. The down side is it's a dollar less than what I make now but if I get it, I see it as an opportunity to get out of where I am at now. I worked seven hours that day and I felt like I was there for less than four. Plus, It gave me more time to spend with my daughter than I normally would which would be two hours if she didn't get up early before I go to work.
As for my depression, as the day seems to slowly get longer and a bit warmer I am finding myself becoming not so doom and gloom every day. Now if I could just kick my creativity into gear it would be great.

### caroline — 2015-04-13T04:02:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-161046>

Are you getting outside more and taking advantage of mother nature? watch children playing? kick a football around? go for a long walk in the woods? dance like no one is watching? sing at the top of your lungs?

Please try and focus on and enjoy simple pleasures ....focus on others......really listen to what someone else has to say - you will reap huge benefits.

Good luck with the new job!

### kimeka — 2015-04-15T12:13:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-161260>

I feel lied to. Job was a bust. Their craigslist ad said full time @ $12/hr. I applied for it back in February. Took over a month to hear from them for an interview. This was kind of a red flag for me but I thought it wouldn't hurt to try so I set up the interview and went. I did a trial day last week. It took several days to hear back and their response was that it wasn't a full time position. It would be just 2 days a week @ $11/hr. I barely get by at $13. There is no way I can at $11. I was so hoping this was going to work out. It was close to home. It gave me more time with my daughter. It had less stress than the place I am currently at and definitely didn't have the negative atmosphere of my current job. I'm so frustrated.

### nicld — 2015-04-15T15:58:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-161282>

That's too bad. Hang in there, something will come along, just keep digging.

### fitness@home — 2015-04-15T16:20:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-161285>

Is there an agency you can sign up with in your town (or surrounding town)? Sometimes they have the inside track to some better jobs.

I've done temp to hire in my past and it ended up being a very good stable job. Stayed with that company tenyears before moving to Texas. Then another agency contacted me about the job I have now. The recruiter was persistent because I turned her down three times about doing an interview. She kept calling! Finally I capitulated and the rest is history.

Don't give up!

### Lahelada — 2015-04-15T16:53:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-161290>

Look at it positively. You had an interview. That means you had practice in projecting your value to someone. When I was a manager xi used to send my people to inerviews when they expressed interest in advancing saying from the outset that it takes 3 interviews to get your pitch right. So next time aim just a bit higher than you are comfortable with. If you do not get it then you are just not ready at THIS time. Try again and soon.

### kimeka — 2015-04-30T14:34:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-162861>

For the past couple days, I've been feeling like crap. I did something in my hand the other day that felt like something snapped like a guitar string on the back of my hand and it hurt to move it sometimes. The next day it was radiating up my hand. I was telling my mom about it and she says it sounds like carpal tunnel like she has. Its because I do repetitive movements all day and moved differently for something else and my body didn't like it. It's feeling better today but every now and then it lets me know it's still there. I'm also dealing with some mild heartburn that I am getting rather tired of having monthly. It's always after I ovulate. Before it was right my period but has moved to right after ovulation. Sometimes it starts around 4:30 pm till sometime during my sleep. I've tried baking soda, acv and both seem to make me throw up. I take charcoal and that helps me a little to at least get me to fall asleep. I would just like to be able to get through a month without having any heartburn at all. I also take magnesium citrate daily if that's any help.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-30T14:53:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-162862>

that GERD sounds like a bad environment issue for sure.

### kimeka — 2015-06-10T04:55:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-167324>

For a week and a half, I've been dealing with chest pain that also can make my shoulder blades hurt. The past few days, I am almost fine during the day but 4:30 pm hits and it starts to come back. When I would get like this before it would happen either right after I had my period but the past few months it was right after ovulation. This time it was right before, during and after my period. Sometimes it can keep me up until I fall asleep from exhaustion. I literally don't want to take HCl capsules with my meal and I feel like the l-glutamine (2-3 750mg capsules) isn't helping either. For the past week I have been drinking just broth by Friday I was able to eat some rice crackers. Yesterday, I had a salad which consisted of romaine, spinach, cucumber, hard boiled eggs, roasted chicken, and roasted garlic. I was alright until I bed time and I remember waking up an hour after falling asleep and feeling like crap.
I saw my cardiologist yesterday and he gave my heart a listen and said he didn't hear my murmur anymore.

C-reactive protein is 1.7 last year it was 0.5
cholesterol 253
triglyceride 68
HDL 41
CHOL/HDL 6.2
Non-HDL 212
LDL 198

glucose 76
sodium 138
potassium 3.9
chloride 102
CO2 26
bun 8
creatine 0.8
total protein 7.1
albumin 4.2
calcium 9.3
bilirubin 0.9
alk phosphate 56
SGOT 19
SGPT 18
WBC 4.9
RBC 4.54
Hemoglobin 13.8
HCT 40.7
MCV 89.6
MCH 30.4
mchc 33.9
rdw 12.2
platelet count 265
absolute nucl rc 0.0
nucleated rbc% 0.0
lymph% 26.3
mono% 9.0
neut% 63.5
eosin% 0.4
baso% 0.6

There are a few more listed for the cbc-w differential but I'm tired and I hurt at the moment. I need to find a new primary care dr that knows and understands paleo without giving me an attitude or disapproving manner. I also need one that is a pediatrician too. I feel like I'm going backwards and want to move forward and not be in pain.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-06-10T05:08:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-167325>

I think your gut issue is tied to your environment and not to anything else.

### kimeka — 2015-07-09T16:29:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-as-a-daisy-journal-kimeka.13532/post-171476>

The pain subsided for a while. But as I wait for my period to arrive, it's creeping back at night. The pain is where my underwire sits. During the day, I can almost ignore it but at night, it almost makes it difficult to sleep. It literally heightens my anxiety. I'm beginning to feel like all I can have is chicken broth again. I was like that for two whole weeks. I don't want to go through that again.
Still looking for another job. My hubby sent me a video for guided mediation to make me laugh during my breaks at work.

I can't help but laugh when I listen to it.

# Thread: Calcium Channel Blockers useful for flying?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calcium-channel-blockers-useful-for-flying.13552/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lookingforanswers — 2015-03-30T15:27:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calcium-channel-blockers-useful-for-flying.13552/post-159848>

Hi All,

I wasn't sure where to post this. I have extreme EMF sensitivity and am taking a trip to the south next week to spend 2 weeks to see if I can find some improvements in my health. I found a great place right on the beach and I plan to be in the ocean every single day!

I am very nervous about flying because any time I'm around a lot of EMF I have issues where my face and ears burn up and my body temperature gets extremely hot. I also end up with stomach pain right below my ribs and I get major anxiety. I'm hoping the plane won't be too terrible since there at least won't be wifi - that and excessive cell phone use are my main triggers.

Any input on using a calcium channel blocker on the flight? Do you think this would help? My doctor is willing to prescribe it for me.

I did read Dr. Kruse's jet lag prescription and will be doing everything else he suggests even though this is only a 2.5 hour flight and in the same time zone.

Thanks for any suggestions!!

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-03-30T21:54:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calcium-channel-blockers-useful-for-flying.13552/post-159861>

i think it would be helpful in that context alongside some good mitochondrial support such as coQ10, PQQ, niacin and magnesium threonate.
lots of sun before flight to maximise vitamin D production, electron dense meal before flight and lots of water.

### lookingforanswers — 2015-04-01T16:16:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calcium-channel-blockers-useful-for-flying.13552/post-160055>

Thanks Josh! I will give all of this a try . . . fingers crossed I have no issues!!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-01T17:38:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calcium-channel-blockers-useful-for-flying.13552/post-160061>

Best calcium blocker pre flying is sunlight before the flight to preload and precondition the system. This is what cardiac surgeons do now to minimize the risk of reperfusion injury

### lookingforanswers — 2015-04-01T17:43:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calcium-channel-blockers-useful-for-flying.13552/post-160065>

Thanks Dr. Kruse. Unfortunately I'm not getting a lot of sun in the northeast right now . . . hence why I'm heading south!!

I have a meditation program on my cell phone (I only use it in airplane mode) and I'm t hinking listening to that would really help with my anxiety, but I wasn't sure about using headphones. ### Penny — 2015-04-01T17:34:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-any-headphones-ok.13553/post-160058>

I've had great luck using GABA Calm by Source Naturals for anxiety... in fact, I have yet to find one person who I've recommended this to that hasn't found a state of "zen" with it - and these have been some really stressed out people... in fact, I even tested it out in a neurofeedback session - calmed the brain waves right down...

[http://www.amazon.com/Source-Naturals-GABA-Orange-Tablets/dp/B000GFSV5A/ref=sr\_1\_1?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1427898879&sr=1-1&keywords=gaba calm](http://www.amazon.com/Source-Naturals-GABA-Orange-Tablets/dp/B000GFSV5A/ref=sr_1_1?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1427898879&sr=1-1&keywords=gaba+calm)

Thanks so much @[Penny](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/446/) ! I forgot all about my GABA. I actually have a product called Kavinace that is wonderful for calming anxiety. I take a PM version to help me sleep as well. I'll have to add that to my packing list!

### Penny — 2015-04-01T17:50:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-any-headphones-ok.13553/post-160066>

Maybe take some ice packs? Truly awful environment..

Deep "belly breathing" breaths are also a direct hit to the Vagus nerve:
[http://www.newsmax.com/Health/Headl...e-stimulation-brainstem/2014/09/15/id/594746/](http://www.newsmax.com/Health/Headline/anxiety-vegus-nerve-stimulation-brainstem/2014/09/15/id/594746/)

# Thread: Urgent...epi paleo for 12 y.o. Border Collie w/anal tumor?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urgent-epi-paleo-for-12-y-o-border-collie-w-anal-tumor.13556/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2015-03-31T05:22:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urgent-epi-paleo-for-12-y-o-border-collie-w-anal-tumor.13556/post-159888>

Anyone here try the epi paleo diet for a dog that has had cancer?

My sweet friend, a Border Collie girl, was found to have an anal tumor. Her mom will do X-rays tomorrow to find out how bad this is....

The good/bad is, she lives in the mountains, away from EMF.....no cell signal.

During the day unless it's freezing or pouring rain she is always outside. Running less in recent years....but still a lot, and really fast - LOVES being outside, and when she's not running or chasing something, she's lying on the ground, sometimes the grass, sometimes the mulch, sometimes small-stoned driveway, preferably in the sunshine.

I told her mom to give her sardines (she loves them!) and salmon...as little regular dog food as possible, which she doesn't like much anyway.....but I wondered if anyone here has any experience with cancer in a dog.

Trouble is too - bc she is outside, and has LOTS of free space/woods/hills/ponds she runs around in, we don't know how or if she'd even tolerate a collar, if they do any surgery. She has a great life, really, for a dog, now (not in her youth, but last 5-6 years or so).

Just breaks my heart bc she's incredibly sweet.

Mom is open to anything.....

Anyone have ideas?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-31T05:51:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urgent-epi-paleo-for-12-y-o-border-collie-w-anal-tumor.13556/post-159889>

### Hope — 2015-03-31T05:52:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urgent-epi-paleo-for-12-y-o-border-collie-w-anal-tumor.13556/post-159890>

She has had a good environment all her life actually...

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-31T05:57:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urgent-epi-paleo-for-12-y-o-border-collie-w-anal-tumor.13556/post-159892>

can't with that story and that is the truth.........

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-03-31T09:03:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urgent-epi-paleo-for-12-y-o-border-collie-w-anal-tumor.13556/post-159900>

dogs are also obligate carnivores and are often fed all sorts of shit in kibble etc that is incompatible with their gut for long periods of their life. that too is a mismatch.

### Inger — 2015-03-31T10:33:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urgent-epi-paleo-for-12-y-o-border-collie-w-anal-tumor.13556/post-159906>

This ^^
I feed my cat carnivore and lots of seafood and she is 16 yo and doing so well
The vets I work for... their both cats died of diabetes pretty young.. and they feed the regular cat food
also their new cats have an insatiable appetite like crazy.,. they have to hide all foods or the cats will eat them... like.. bread... apples... anything... they never get satiated.
to me a pretty bad sign

How do she feed her dog?

The EMF can be bad even if the outside space is great.. maybe in the house there is high magnetic fields, microwave oven... other electronics... wifi.. fake light... etc....?
I would test their sleeping space inside and the house....

### Hope — 2015-03-31T17:32:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urgent-epi-paleo-for-12-y-o-border-collie-w-anal-tumor.13556/post-159952>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=159906)

> to me a pretty bad sign

>
> The EMF can be bad even if the outside space is great.. maybe in the house there is high magnetic fields, microwave oven... other electronics... wifi.. fake light... etc....?
> I would test their sleeping space inside and the house....

Thank you guys. I am concerned about the food, yes - too much processed food though I always give her fish, some meat etc. and am working on my friend with it.

She has TV in her house, microwave and there is an outdoor light which shines into some rooms. Thankfully she's getting rid of it. And the doggie does go back into the back rooms where it is darker. I will test the house soon, for sure, when I go back......though I only have a body voltage meter.

Thanks again for the support. Hopefully she will listen. She will do anything for a doggie.......so hopefully this is something she's open to.

The little girl is going in right now for testing. I hope she is OK because she is NOT using to riding in a car, going to the vet, much less testing.

Her life is running around, taking creek and pond baths and being outside.

### Hope — 2015-03-31T17:33:14+0300

### Penny — 2015-03-31T18:02:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urgent-epi-paleo-for-12-y-o-border-collie-w-anal-tumor.13556/post-159957>

Got ya covered - you need to get a vet to get this vet's chinese herbs - actually, I have my bunny on them - my vet went to his acupuncture school and said she's seen this stuff cure everything, including cancer -

[https://www.tcvmherbal.com/](https://www.tcvmherbal.com/)

It can only be obtained by a vet - however... you can read his book on chinese herbs for vets on google books and see what is in the stuff... astragulus is a huge player - I would also be giving him turmeric and Japanese Knotweed:

[http://www.natural-dog-health-remedies.com/resveratrol-benefits.html](http://www.natural-dog-health-remedies.com/resveratrol-benefits.html)
Ginger makes resveretrol more bioactive as does black pepper but that would be a non-starter here...

Here's the link to the book -
[http://books.google.com/books?id=2u...ce=gbs\_ge\_summary\_r&cad=0#v=onepage&q&f=false](http://books.google.com/books?id=2ut1Cvgj9pYC&printsec=frontcover&source=gbs_ge_summary_r&cad=0#v=onepage&q&f=false)

There is also a vet - who actually treats epileptic people by changing their diet - called [www.dogtorj.com](http://www.dogtorj.com) - he found he could cure dogs of many ills just by removing the crap from pet food - he has some recommendations on his site -

Maybe you could get him to get the herbs...
