# Thread: Calling moms w/babies later in life - getting out of testing??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 43
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2015-03-31T19:13:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-159970>

Anyone out there in JK land have had a baby when they were over 35?

I am searching for a midwife practice or any doctor who won't force me into testing I do not want. I just got off the phone with the only low-tech practice in my area and they would ONLY take me if I follow their "Standard Protocols" of testing.

Yuck! It's like I am forced to do stuff I don't want. And this is MY body.

Examples are....the DIABETES test - where you gulp down 75g of CORN syrup (can you tell I wanna scream!). I do all I can to avoid that crap and then subject my baby to it?

AND because of my age they'd do a doppler ultrasound in weeks 36 and up. EVERY single week!

I am seriously considering going to Germany and not coming back to have my baby there. Though maybe they'd force me there too, I don't know.

So anyway - does anyone have ANY thoughts on how to get out of testing such as this stuff? I want to try to find a place, even if I have to go out of state. I don't care. My body is my body.

I do have a friend who did NO testing, no ultrasound except for one early one...but she was 29. Not AMA.

Thanks so much in advance...for any stories/suggestions!!!

### Marie — 2015-03-31T21:22:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-159984>

I'd find a Hypnobirthing practitioner in your area and ask them for a recommendation. They should know local docs who are more natural birthing oriented and willing to sidestep a lot of the BS. (Hypnobirthing is more about avoiding pain cycles and intervention, less about hypnosis.) If that doesn't work look for a TCM practitioner who focuses on fertility and ask them.

### cantweight — 2015-03-31T23:31:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-159987>

What about a home birth midwife? Might be the only option to avoid all the modern crap in the states.

### fitness@home — 2015-04-01T00:24:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-159992>

Hope, I was wondering if you were expecting. Congratulations !

Follow your gut and do what is right for YOU!!

### Hope — 2015-04-01T01:35:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-159998>

>
> I'd find a Hypnobirthing practitioner in your area and ask them for a recommendation. They should know local docs who are more natural birthing oriented and willing to sidestep a lot of the BS. (Hypnobirthing is more about avoiding pain cycles and intervention, less about hypnosis.) If that doesn't work look for a TCM practitioner who focuses on fertility and ask them.

Thanks Marie! I have done. Sadly they sent me to the place with this. Will try to talk to them later this week.

> [cantweight said:](/goto/post?id=159987)
>
> What about a home birth midwife? Might be the only option to avoid all the modern crap in the states.

Yes, Christi - I did find someone out of network who comes HIGHLY recommended by my prenatal yoga teacher - who happens to be from my home town and went to my high school! Whew. She loves her. Have to convince DH with it though - financially and the home thing. We have an appt next week where he should be there too. She sounds wonderful.....she said I wouldn't have to do the diabetes test, nor the stress test. She said she doesn't consider people high risk just bc of age. so that was great. And we shall see.

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=159992)
>
> Hope, I was wondering if you were expecting. Congratulations !
>
> Follow your gut and do what is right for YOU!!

Thanks Dawn. I still can't believe it. Of course I have no idea how things are. Just that I am growing and blood work is OK and the OB said I can do my trip to Germany too. Only had one early US and no genetic testing......I am trusting my body and what happens, happens. The less testing and stuff, the better for me.

### Hope — 2015-04-01T14:47:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160049>

### Hope — 2015-04-01T17:38:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160060>

you mean you WANTED it?? I am shocked!

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=160059)
>
> I mandated it...........

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-01T17:39:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160062>

Yep.........Older eggs lead to a lot more problems and the baby can be very altered.

### Penny — 2015-04-01T18:02:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160068>

Yeah - I had my first kid at 38 and my 11 lb baby boy at 40... truly would have been enlightening to know I had blood sugar issues - or at least leptin issues - the only tests they did was the ultrasounds (not 3d) because twins ran in my family

I also got a CVS (Chorionic villus sampling ) at 10 weeks from a doctor who did one every 5 minutes to test for any genetic anomalies - at 43, you have a 1 in 6 chances of a Down's kid...
[http://www.mayoclinic.org/tests-procedures/chorionic-villus-sampling/basics/definition/prc-20013566](http://www.mayoclinic.org/tests-procedures/chorionic-villus-sampling/basics/definition/prc-20013566)

Seems to me they could test HBA1C and blood glucose instead of the syrup thing - I would never do the syrup thing even if I wasn't pregnant...

Maybe be in the system up until 36 weeks...

Congrats BTW

### nonchalant — 2015-04-01T18:09:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160070>

Oh, Hope **congratulations!!** I was also wondering about it. You have been making a lot of pregnancy comments/questions lately.  So happy for you!

There are alternatives to the syrup test, as Penny mentioned.
I had a doctor that was pretty conservative, no ultrasound, etc. But yeah, I was in my 20's.

### Shijin13 — 2015-04-01T19:06:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160077>

Congrats Hope on the pregnancy.

I used Birthcare in Alexandria

I waived all genetic testing. I did do the 20wk ultrasound, and the heart beat doppler weekly post 30 weeks. I was having pre-term labor with Havoc due to the stress of my job. Initally my MW put me on 1/2 time work, limiting my time at work, but it didn't stop the pre-term labor with working 1/2 time. The pre-term labor stopped, when I stopped working at 34 wks when I was placed on bed rest.

I went into spontaneous labor at 38 & 1/2 weeks.

### Lahelada — 2015-04-02T01:59:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160090>

Congrats Hope, wishing you all the best.

### cinnamon — 2015-04-02T02:26:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160094>

Oh my! I'm thinking back to the many times I read how much you longed to have a child of your own. Congratulations to you Hope!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-02T04:03:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160099>

### Hope — 2015-04-02T21:32:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160158>

> [Penny said:](/goto/post?id=160068)
>
> Yeah - I had my first kid at 38 and my 11 lb baby boy at 40... truly would have been enlightening to know I had blood sugar issues - or at least leptin issues - the only tests they did was the ultrasounds (not 3d) because twins ran in my family
>
> I also got a CVS (Chorionic villus sampling ) at 10 weeks from a doctor who did one every 5 minutes to test for any genetic anomalies - at 43, you have a 1 in 6 chances of a Down's kid...
> [http://www.mayoclinic.org/tests-procedures/chorionic-villus-sampling/basics/definition/prc-20013566](http://www.mayoclinic.org/tests-procedures/chorionic-villus-sampling/basics/definition/prc-20013566)
>
> Seems to me they could test HBA1C and blood glucose instead of the syrup thing - I would never do the syrup thing even if I wasn't pregnant...
>
> Maybe be in the system up until 36 weeks...
>
> Congrats BTW

Thanks so much, Penny - I am not at all concerned about diabetes, at least not yet. I eat so carefully. Though I have started more carbs, at the recommendation of Lana Asprey, Dave's wife. Had a great consult with her.

The corn syrup is really awful.......I can't see myself doing it at all.

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=160070)
>
> Oh, Hope **congratulations!!** I was also wondering about it. You have been making a lot of pregnancy comments/questions lately.  So happy for you!
>
> There are alternatives to the syrup test, as Penny mentioned.
> I had a doctor that was pretty conservative, no ultrasound, etc. But yeah, I was in my 20's.

Sally that is so great!! How do I find a doc like that??? Actually there is a Catholic practice I went to 3 years ago...bc they got it about thyroid I thought. The one who said I should give up trying to have a baby bc it wouldn't happen. Three years ago.

I hope I can do a sugar alternative. The thing is, my current OB has been OK with my not doing THAT test because she knows my history, eating healthy/no sugar (though I do eat some carbs now), low blood pressure - at least so far - today it was 94/54 for my H2O2 IV!!!!!!

Trouble is that OB doesn't deliver at a birthing center, which would be my preference over a hospital, in every way so I will have to switch if I want to do that. The one place I called there called me "demanding" because I asked for alternatives and they told me I wouldn't be a good fit for their practice. Could be the universe and I now found a different natural birth center too. We shall see.

I haven't shared the news so directly because I really wanted to wait.......but I got so upset about this particular thing I had to ask about this on here. I DO NOT like all the fear-mongering, comparing MY N=1 body to statistics. Thank goodness I am going to Europe very soon.....I cannot wait (that'll be enough radiation ha, though I will do much grounding and relaxing while there). There is so much to think about, WHEN MY BODY says....relax, trust me, have fun, enjoy the process. I even don't feel like studying much, though I read a little about what happens each week which is neat. But I feel my body asking me to connect with IT, be OUTSIDE......and OUT of my head. I was there all my life!

### Hope — 2015-04-02T21:35:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160159>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=160077)
>
> Congrats Hope on the pregnancy.
>
> I used Birthcare in Alexandria
>
> I waived all genetic testing. I did do the 20wk ultrasound, and the heart beat doppler weekly post 30 weeks. I was having pre-term labor with Havoc due to the stress of my job. Initally my MW put me on 1/2 time work, limiting my time at work, but it didn't stop the pre-term labor with working 1/2 time. The pre-term labor stopped, when I stopped working at 34 wks when I was placed on bed rest.
>
> I went into spontaneous labor at 38 & 1/2 weeks.

Gretchen, that is great about the testing you got to waive. Were you above 35? Was the U/S showing stuff and did they tell you you had to see a specialist etc??

In my one first time mom class, met a girl, **_25 years old_**, went through 6 weeks of lots of testing, being told her baby had Down’s, maybe, kidney and brain issues. Finally after a special US and 4/5 hours at Children’s hospital, everything is now actually OK. The regular U/S wasn’t clear enough perhaps. She said it’s been horrible and she hasn’t even been able to be excited to be pregnant (she is 30 weeks now). And she is 25. She agreed, they are LOOKING for bad things….even if they can’t do much. Ugh. I really don’t want all this.

I really am sick of all the fear mongering. I can understand if something goes wrong etc...but 'just' bc of my age.....

For me so far the most difficult thing? Dealing with these doctors!!!!! So stressful for me. I'd rather keep doing my acupuncture....

### Hope — 2015-04-02T21:36:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160160>

Heh, I got that doctor on accident.
I chose him from the phone book, based on his first name. I was looking for a female OB, and chose one with the first name of Mahendra. LOL!
But I liked him, once I got over that disappointment.  It was a smallish city, and there weren't too many to choose from. No female OBs.
Everyone asked me whether I was having a boy or a girl, and I had no clue until the blessed event. People probably thought I was odd. Still do, come to think of it.

### Shijin13 — 2015-04-03T01:08:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160167>

@[Hope](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5412/) I just told my MW running tests, weren't going to change the outcome of the pregnancy. we told them during the 1st pregnancy (Chaos) - which I was 34. Same thing with Havoc and I was 36.

I have a cousin who's a genetic counselor and she told me, If having the tests and the results would change your decision to have the baby, you need to be sure that your ok with not having the baby, or knowing your carrying a baby that will die in utero . If the test results wouldn't influence your decision to have the baby, don't get them.

My children were gifts, miracles really as I shouldn't have been able to get pregnant with my PCOS. When I found out I was pregnant I knew I would love them no matter what. There were no other options for me.

As you know, I'm a bit of a PITA as a patient. fortunately my MW were ok with that. I'm still friends with the MW who stayed with me after my transfer to the hospital, when Chaos was born.

### Jude — 2015-04-05T10:27:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160333>

Wonderful news Hope, sincere congratulations.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-04-05T14:54:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160341>

Congratulations.

My mother had me at age 38 back in 1973 - I was her sixth child. She told me that she was strongly advised... urged, even.. to have the "brand new" test (back then), an amniocentesis. She asked if they found something 'wrong', could they then fix it by finding it earlier, or otherwise change it? They said no. She asked, why would I need to know now instead of in several months? They replied, in case you would want to terminate. She said "I do not consent to testing then" and just didnt' sign the forms.

I don't know if that can still be done as simple as that, but I would think so. (I saw that you stated that you were having issues with places regarding their "standard protocols" of testing, and really hope you ca find a place that will work with you.) In the end I think doctors just don't want to get sued. One time I forgot to get my shot of depo provera until a month late and didn't want pregnancy testing before getting my shot so my doctor had me just write down that I understand I could be pregnant and want the shot anyway so he could put it into his records in order to mitigate any blame that might come out of it. Then he didn't care either way anymore. Maybe just offer to give them a statement in writing that says you understand the risks and refuse the testing? I know it sounds terribly simplistic and maybe it is. I do live in a tiny town so I'm sure things are different here.

Good luck no matter what!

(edited to add - it's strange, my final pregnancy was with a high-risk doctor since I was in a car accident when I was 13 weeks along and ultrasound in the ER showed a lot of blood between the uterus and placenta... and I had to have labor induced 3 weeks early due to pre-eclampsia... but still I don't recall ever having any tests done besides the normal ultrasounds. I think I had 2. They definitely didn't do the glucose testing for either of my second or third pregnancies at all, I know that. But my pregnancies were in 93, 95, and 03 so perhaps things have changed in the past dozen years and they're more insistent about testing now. I'm so pleased for you and inspired by your determination to have this child your way. Sending good vibes your way for a lovely pregnancy.)

### Hope2Learn — 2015-04-05T14:57:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160342>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=160062)

I ask because other articles cite more of a 1:30 chance and it seems to be one of those statistics that gets thrown around often but rarely consistently.

### rlee314 — 2015-04-05T20:12:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160352>

huge congratulations, hope!!

i think it was you who had the most lovely words to say to kovita when she shared of her pregnancy several months ago. your words really stuck w/me.

i'm 36-conceived at 35.
no testing. no US.
homebirth.
so far i'm having an excellent pregnancy.
i had an amazing pregnancy w/my first, who turned 4 in december, also a homebirth, and breastfed for 3.5 years.

trust your mama intuition.

homebirth is expensive but SO much less invasive than the alternative.
you can buy a glucometer and do your own post prandial tests and definitely do a fasting hba1c--know that a keto diet can alter hba1c as i think has been discussed in threads here before.
get LOTS of sun!! stuff yourself w/DHA....

so happy for you!!

### Hope — 2015-04-07T18:52:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160555>

Aww thanks all you guys are SO sweet! I have had no time to be on here and am running off now - I am going to Germany next week...and SO Much to do. I have a wedding to attend and cannot find a flattering dress my mother is OK with. I will likely have to let it go....LOL and do something comfortable but this belly.....is growing. LOL.

I can't wait to read everyone's input.

Tomorrow I have the 20 week U/S. Decided to do it bc I found a MW who I love and if all is well I would switch to her care - she is so calming and nurturing, just what I need for this process. And she knows about Christiane Northrup, my hero

Oh - I did get a meter. FBG was 87, PP was 105. MW said it's good, but I haven't had time to research. Just wanted to see. Bc I do eat carbs now, suddenly. Feels SO weird LOL

Rlee - that is awesome!  and thanks for the kind words. Yes I remember kovita well and the words that came to my heart when I read her words in September. She is SUCH an inspiration, as many people here  Did your MW not say it would be good to know if you have placenta pre via? This MW said if I do, it would require C-section as the baby can't come out naturally then. And yes, home birth is my dream and desire.......

### kovita — 2015-04-07T20:48:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160568>

Hope, I cannot offer any help for USA, just experience here in Austria, Geman system might be very similar it often actually is. You have to fill all required medical checks as per the local regulation in case you will apply for maternity leave and support money, without full "mother and baby book" they will not pay any benefit. There is oral glucose tolerance test, but I skipped it easily on request. There is no mandatory doppler either. I have my next check at 38 weeks getting just normal quick measurement ultrasound and check on the umbilical veins and flow in cerebral arteries. There is no other check before birth happens, unless you demand separately. I suggest some regular checks are desirable providing you do not spend too much time just observing the fetus and it makes sense to repeat toxoplasma and hepatitis tests again late in the pregnancy.

Giving birth in normal hotely alike room in position I choose, no medical intervention, no fetal heartbeat monitor, have not seem a doctor at all. I know as well how medical are births for example in Asia (that is why gave birth in Europe despite of living in Dingapore some years back). i hope your pregnancy goes on well, I am 5 weeks away now and my belly growths like crazy. I gave birth twice almost at 42 weeks, not sure why this time I feel like it may come sooner ;-)

### rlee314 — 2015-04-10T20:13:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-160847>

i don't feel like birth-in a healthy pregnancy should be feared or medicalized. so, i choose to homebirth. and meditate and visulaize every day. my midwives and i are watching out for placenta previa symptoms. but, again, i don't entertain those thoughts or possibilities.

best wishes.

### Greenisland — 2015-05-06T13:11:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-163440>

Midwife here.... When working with a practice that demands the test (or is required by their regulations or back up docs), another option is to ask for an equivalent glucose load in the form of carefully measured apple juice, jelly beans made from real sugar, white bread with jam, etc. A HBA1C plus a week of logging your blood sugar will give a provider better information as to what your body is handling real food on a day to day basis. If you fail the 1 hour challenge, the OB will call you back in for a 3 hour challenge, and if you fail or are borderline then what do they do? They ask you to log your food and blood sugar levels for a week and see what is really going on!!! Logging really is the gold standard. But mainstream OB care assumes that the patient is neither motivated enough, nor trustworthy enough to log accurately. Just today I was talking to a doc who about a GD client of mine that is risking out of my care for a number of reasons and he didn't think she was capable of monitoring herself nor of making any changes other than taking meds. "Well you know, someone with her mental capabilities...." The woman is as smart as a whip but is low income and has a speech impediment. It made my blood boil!!!

I think therein lie the main difference between OB and midwifery care: The midwife expects her clients to be motivated and a decision maker about her own care. She also isn't paternalistic enough to think that a woman isn't capable of taking part in monitoring her own body. A midwife is geared to working with a healthy self motivated client. U.S. OBs have to work with the crack mamas, McDonalds addicts, sendentary GMO feeders all day long. The epi-paleo/biohacking/optimal living woman is quite frankly not who their were trained to work with...vibrantly healthy women are out of their scope of care. Ideally these surgeons should just be caring for that percentage that are really sick and need the tests, drugs and surgery (30%?) and we would have an army of midwives like in the northern european countries. By the way.....Happy International Day of the Midwife!!!

### Greenisland — 2015-05-06T13:14:06+0300

### nicld — 2015-05-06T22:28:19+0300

### notsoperfick — 2015-05-13T18:22:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-moms-w-babies-later-in-life-getting-out-of-testing.13577/post-164136>

Hope that's excellent news  I think Shiijin's advice is the most relevant, you don't need any testing beyond what you might like to have. Even ultrasound shouldn't be too frequent, we all managed fine when there wasn't any! What will be will be. The biggest influence on having a Down's is an older father, it has more to do with them than it does whether the mother is old. Here's an article confirming that and you can of course google the Professor's name to view his research outcomes: [http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-102697/Downs-risk-children-older-men.html](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-102697/Downs-risk-children-older-men.html)

It is your body, your baby, your choices. If you keep yourself informed then you can choose your options. Midwives are probably more clued up than doctors because they oversee mothers and deliver the babies, that said there are probably some very oldfashioned mws out there so try to pick wisely and on recommendation.

I wish you lots of joy and few worries

I did tell her about my home testing....and that I'm a healthy eating fanatic LOL. So that was good.

### Eddie Garza — 2015-03-31T23:36:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/want-optimal-health.13579/post-159988>

Hi folks,

I just joined the forum and I am looking to turning my health around. I have numerous issues, some of which are low adrenal function (daytime saliva cortisol are extremely low, nighttime levels are high), low testosterone, leaky gut, heavy metal toxicity, candida overgrowth, sleep apnea, always tired, body aches, inflammation, frequent headaches, etc. I would like to get in contract with a doctor that can help me. If I could, I would like to see Dr. Kruse, but that probably isn't possible. If there is a doctor who is part of his network that someone can recommend, that would be fantastic. I live in Texas so the closer the better.
Health and Happiness to all.
Eddie

Dawn (aka fitness@home)

### Eddie Garza — 2015-04-01T05:41:30+0300

### caroline — 2015-04-01T10:07:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/want-optimal-health.13579/post-160026>

Hi Eddie.......caroline is my middle name and what my DH always called me - I love it too!

Let's start with your sleep ....most important

Get all electronics out of the bedroom ...no ipad, TV, iphone, alarm clock etc. etc.
Can you turn off the power to your bedroom?
Does your house have a smart meter?

do you have blue blocker glasses? this is vital for when on your computer etc.

no fake lite after sunset ...... you need to reset the Rolex in your brain!

Please read the leptin reset - really important...

rise and go outside and look at the sun - barefoot and naked LOL
have a BAB within 30 minutes of rising
at least 4 hours between meals and before bed
drink R/O or spring water
get outside as much as possible and hug a tree or two
do you live near an ocean? lake? river? stream? puddle?
C/T is a game changer

What are you eating?
seafood, seafood, seafood

### Eddie Garza — 2015-04-03T05:59:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/want-optimal-health.13579/post-160179>

Hi Caroline,

My home does not have a smart meter, but I will look into turning off the circuit breakers.

My home alarm control panel is about 6 feet away from my bed. Should I have that removed? Also the main box to the alarm is in my bedroom closet about 12 feet away from the head of my bed. Should that be relocated?

I currently do not have blue blocker glasses, but I will buy some tomorrow. Is there a specific brand you recommend? Should I wear them at work while and at home? What about when I watch TV that is over 12 feet away from the sofa?

You said no fake light after sunset. What kind of lighting should I use to see? Full spectrum lighting?

I will go outside to get that early sun. What does BAB stand for? Build a Bear? LOL, probably Big A@# Breakfast, right?

I will hug a tree, but probably in my back yard. Don't want the neighbors to think I've lost it.

### Inger — 2015-04-03T11:25:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/want-optimal-health.13579/post-160203>

Welcome Eddie!

Do not worry about mercury in seafood. Just avoid big predator fishes like tuna and swordfish. And of course try to get seafood from cleanest waters. But even if a little contaminated you still need the seafood desperately. The positive effects will way overweight the negatives  You need to get as much seafood in as you can. I personally eat seafood 2-3 times / day mostly, and I do so well! I never felt better in my life, seriously    I eat it mostly raw, raw seafood is amazingly healing stuff

CT = cold thermogenesis = cold water therapy.. or cold air too but water is way more effective. Read the CT blog series of Jack
in practice it can look like this (what I do); Cold water face dunks in the morning, then a hot shower followed up with a dip in my rain barrel on the terrace, water temp maybe 3 degree C
then window always wide open in the night in my bedroom, keep it as cool as you comfortably can in your home, dress less, not more  Go outside for walks/spend tie in the nature
and take it slow, increase your cold exposure slowly, your body will get used to it and you will start to crave it . It is super energizing!

You should have at least 4 hours fasted before bedtime. I like to eat my last meal no later than 4-5 PM, so I usually have about 5 hours fasted before bedtime. It is important to have a empty belly when you go to sleep, you will sleep way better and your body can finally heal in the night

Yeah, BAB is big ass breakfast

For lighting use candles or red lamps, like salt lamps, red bulbs.. I use a infrared light in my bathroom for light in the evening when I shower.. it is nice  otherwise I use just a few salt lamps (reddish, 15 watt) and some times candles. I love the darkness. You need f.lux on your screen, on the darkest setting. and blue blockers........ you can get cheap ones on amazon, they work too. I have the blueblockers brand, and then some cheap orange ones too for 6 € that work well too. Ideal would be to spend the evening in total darkness for sure! We do the best we can  If you stay in complete darkness you sure will heal faster

Ditch the TV... it only destroys your health
Instead use your time to read Jacks blogs.... spend quality time with your partner... massages... sex.... you get it  That will be a way wiser way to use your time
We need to use our time wisely............

### yewwei.tan — 2015-04-03T07:29:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquitination-8-is-live.13615/post-160184>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=160134)
>
> Read Life at the Edge as soon as it comes out in the USA in June.
>
> I would suggest you buying it from the UK................It is that important to understand light's interaction on nitrogen.

Just finished 'Life on the Edge' (kindle edition from Amazon's Australian Store)

It is good **observational overview** of Quantum Biology. That is, it tells you in broad detail the empirical facts about the experimental evidence that we have on Quantum Biology. In that sense, it tells the reader factually what we currently know.

However, I do not trust this book in elucidating further mechanism on how living systems organise. The authors have beliefs that prevent good discussion of particular mechanisms.

In the introduction, the Big Bang, as well as the Sun as a nuclear reactor are mentioned. Both theories are false , and clearly the authors subscribe to conventional models, but those particular mis-steps are forgivable.

Sidenote: 30 problems with the Big Bang  -- [http://www.metaresearch.org/cosmology/BB-top-30.asp](http://www.metaresearch.org/cosmology/BB-top-30.asp)
Then there's Eric Lerner's [http://bigbangneverhappened.org/](http://bigbangneverhappened.org/)
Halton Arp's models of intrinsic Red Shift are more likely to be true than an expanding Universe --

Some errors that are not forgivable are a complete dismissal by the authors of Roger Penrose's ideas of microtubules as quantum qubits. Throughout the book, there is no treatment of water and it's role in the small spaces that surround cells, neither is there a discussion about the electrical nature of gravity. Microtubules filled with superconducting Casimir-pressured water will behave very much like a qubit, and while we can't say that it is the basis of a quantum computing brain, dismissal of this idea means that the authors of 'Life of the Edge' are not thinking in a way that allows them to elucidate further mechanics.

But again, the stuff that they get right based on observational evidence is treated pretty well. I like the chapter on how life creates "order from chaos" using noise. There is a good emphasis on why you need some noise:

\- Too much noise, and particles can never become quantum coherent and never perform quantum mechanical actions (like proton tunnelling)
\- But too little noise, and things become "too coherent", in the sense that all superpositions of a particle are equally possible, and no single choice can be made. The definition of living is in being able to make a choice (observation), and collapsing the quantum wave function to advance the organism to a new state (which contains more information).

Sidenote: the discussion on noise reminds me of this 1994 paper by Van Jacobson about how to reduce TCP message synchronization using added randomness -- [http://ee.lbl.gov/papers/sync\_94.pdf](http://ee.lbl.gov/papers/sync_94.pdf) . It seems that even in a complex system like the Internet, shared protocols lead to emergent synchronization of message delivere. Too much synchronization of Internet data packets leads to huge variances in network load, which is generally something we try to reduce.​

\----

The book also contains little too much analogy for me, and to me, oversteps the bounds of useful analogy (see the Richard Feynman video below). The only analogies that were useful to me were the music ones, mainly because they closely describe the actual wave phenomena that underlie quantum mechanics.

It is a personal preference of mine to speak using the exact terms of the topic at hand. Those terms are there because they describe concepts precisely and accurately, whereby one piece of terminology means one thing exactly.

I do not like trying to make things easy by describing using familiar concepts, which can have subjective interpretations. This is even more problematic when it comes to quantum mechanics, because concepts like "spin" will be totally muddied by analogy.​

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-03T19:12:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquitination-8-is-live.13615/post-160233>

this came from FB IPMG group discussion I was tagged on for a nurse with pseudotumor yesterday:

Pseudotumor usually affects the vision and neuropthamologists usually have to do optic nerve fenestrations. If the headaches are bad and she has low D3 levels her environment is toxic. If she is a nurse it is likely a huge factor especially if she is a night time shift worker. She needs to avoid this ASAP. Is the dermoid related, yes. Why? Complicated but here it goes.........Dermoid cyst of the ovary are cystic teratoma's. A teratoma is a benign tumor that is associated with altered cAMP signaling. cAMP is the second messenger used in hormone signaling but it is also actively involved in ultravioltet light signaling in the skin, bone, brain, and in nucleic acids. If you read my quantum Vitamin D3 blog, you would have seen how the dermoid and brain are connected are via light. The skin and brain both come from the same material in an embryo. This tissue is called neuroectoderm. At one time in all life the skin and brain were connected. They remain connected by subatomically by free radical chemistry of entangled electrons. In the adult these tissues are connected as they once were. But the altered light environment can stimulate both previously connected tissues to demonstrate altered growth if the altered light stimulus is present in one organ. In your sister it appear it is her brain via her retina. This happens via melanopsin receptors. Here is another twist most do not know. In the embryo, neuroectoderm receives a signal from bone morphogenetic protein. This is an inhibiting signals from a protein called noggin. Noggin leads to the development of the nervous system from this tissue. Here we see bone tissue can directs brain formation. This protein also affects growth of things from skin remnants. A dermoid is a skin remnant. This also means your sister likely has some degree of osteopenia. You can check that with a tibial compression test using a butter knife. I talk about this in my current blog, ubiquitination 8. Everything is connected in a quantum ecosystem. This is the key principle behind coherence in the living matrix of all biology is tied to the free radical chemistry they both share. Are their more connections you should be aware of with the skin? Yes, this is big for your sister......... The integumentary system covers the surface of the embryo and its specialized skin structures including hair, nails, sweat glands, mammary glands and teeth. As a system it has contributions from all embryonic layers. These are the things a dermoid teratoma is made up from. Most neurosurgeons forget these details. This means that every system at some level is tied to specific light frequencies that sulfate Vitamin D3 in the skin. Your sister's lifestyle and location on the planet do not allow her to do so. This is what is driving the dermoid growth and her brain expansion, because both tissues are losing energy to their environment and this causes their growth. Moreover, it is why the sulfation of Vitamin D3 by the sun is important to understand.I covered that in Tensegrity 7 blog post. She likely has a very altered D3 and vitamin A cycle because of a chronic light frequency mismatch in both tissue linked to her neuroectoderm. This drives altered cAMP to cause both to lose the energy. What helps both of these conditions? DHA and cold thermogenesis? Why? The DHA reason is in the current blog.......but the CT reason is due to AMPK pathways signaling. It controls mTOR and IGF1 and cold is the only direct way of stopping the altered cAMP signaling when you have a light mismatch. I covered that late in the ubiquination 7 blog. Dermoids contains an array of developmentally mature, solid tissues like skin, with hair follicles, and sweat glands, clumps of long hair, pockets of sebum, blood, fat, bone, nails, teeth, eyes, cartilage, and thyroid tissue. You need to get your sister tested for osteopenia too.[http://www.jackkruse.com/quantum-biology-7-vitamin-d/](http://www.jackkruse.com/quantum-biology-7-vitamin-d/)

Chronic cold temperature changes water optical abilities, because it affects water density. Water density change directly affects CSF dynamics. Your sister has pseudotumor which is a DISEASE OF ALTERED CSF DYNAMICS because of density shifts. When you CSF is less dense you brain swells. This links density to buoyancy. Buoyancy is linked to Archimedes principle. Remember your sister's brain floats in a sea of CSF, just as a boat floats in water. Her sea in her cranial is got a huge density issue. Cold exposure directly changes the density in water. Cold water has more electrons in it and those electrons can enter her brain to shrink it thermodynamically. This is why NS use cold in brain trauma. Cold action is enhanced if the brain is loaded with DHA. Your sisters brain is not. Fake light destroys DHA. This is why Archimedes principle is critical to understand regarding the sea within your cells. Water density changes facilitate AMPK signaling, which then inactivate IGF 1 and mTOR pathways in one fell swoop to limit her dermoid growth and shrink her brain. Cold exposure extends longevity because surface cold exposure is able, by itself, to increase AMPK and blood glucose to try to slow clock genes down. Clock genes are what control tumor growth of the dermoid. Complex stuff........for sure but all explainable and understandable when you know a bit of physics. This is why the mammalian dive reflex is based upon surface temperature changes to affect water density to our face alone. It is also why it forms the basis of my CT protocol.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-04-04T02:22:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquitination-8-is-live.13615/post-160260>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=160185)
>
> Time to dive down the rabbit hole again
>
> Researching nitrogen will inevitably involve the study of Nitric Oxide (NO) as well, which is one of the 3 quantum gases mentioned in Tensegrity #12 -- [http://www.jackkruse.com/tensegrity-12-quantum-cell-membrane/](http://www.jackkruse.com/tensegrity-12-quantum-cell-membrane/)
>
> Quick reminder for anyone embarking on research that the effects of Nitric Oxide and S-Nitrosocysteine are different, though many research papers treat them as the same -- [http://qhwiki.com/study:s-nitrocysteine-as-no-surrogate](http://qhwiki.com/study:s-nitrocysteine-as-no-surrogate)
>
> ....

Did 2 study excerpts:

\- '**Structural profiling of endogenous S-nitrosocysteine residues reveals unique features that accommodate diverse mechanisms for protein S-nitrosylation**' -- [http://qhwiki.com/study:s-nitrocysteine-protein-s-nitrosylation](http://qhwiki.com/study:s-nitrocysteine-protein-s-nitrosylation)
\- '**Carbon and nitrogen metabolism regulated by the ubiquitin-proteasome system**' -- [http://qhwiki.com/study:carbon-nitrogen-ubiquitin](http://qhwiki.com/study:carbon-nitrogen-ubiquitin) (linked from the Ubi8 blog)

Cysteine as mentioned in EE12 -- [http://www.jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-12-battery-charged/](http://www.jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-12-battery-charged/)

That first paper talks about how cysteine acts as both an oxidative redox controller, but also as a mediator of protein synthesis by providing protein scaffolds (see the 'Linear Motifs' section of the paper). Cysteine is also basically found everywhere, as the paper demonstrates.

There are 3 more papers talking directly about S-nitrosylation, NO, and ubiquitin ligase activity, that I want to get to (but haven't):

\- [http://www.pnas.org/content/101/29/10810.full](http://www.pnas.org/content/101/29/10810.full)
\- [http://www.jbc.org/content/281/45/34124.full](http://www.jbc.org/content/281/45/34124.full)
\- [http://www.jbc.org/content/287/7/4411.full](http://www.jbc.org/content/287/7/4411.full)

### Jill1 — 2015-04-04T05:37:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquitination-8-is-live.13615/post-160273>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=160185)
>
> Time to dive down the rabbit hole again
> Researching nitrogen will inevitably involve the study of Nitric Oxide (NO) as well, which is one of the 3 quantum gases mentioned in Tensegrity #12 --
> ....

Nitrogen mentioned some time ago in a posting by Jack - not sure it helps or ?? I dug deep to find this one.

L-arginine is the major source of nitric oxide (NO). We need NO for glucose metabolism when sulfated cholesterol is MIA. Normally we use eNOS in summer to make sulfate, as I laid out in Tensegrity 7. eNOS switches its chemical function in cold weather seasons and uses nitrates to transport O2 and glucose in winter. Transport of glucose and O2 is dangerous in blood plasma.......so we have to change carriers as season change and eNOS is thermoplastic. I gave you that 3 yrs ago in CT-4 blog post. NO is a poor substitute for SO4 −2 in summer..........but it works like a charm when its cold, your ketotic and hydrated. It becomes less dangrous to allow ROS and RNS in your plasma..........as it does in summer time but it can pinch hit for a short time if your redox is not trashed. Moreover, it can function in some of the missing roles. Sulfated cholesterol decreases in cell membranes when ECT slows, or we lose our electric charges, or we are hypoxic. Because sulfate delivers oxygen to myoglobin, it supplies cholesterol to the cell membrane under the quantum direction of DHA (Bill Lagakos alert), Sulfated cholesterol breaks down glucose specifically to protect the cell’s proteins from glycation and peroxidation damage. This protection is mandatory in a highly oxidative season like summer, because it is energy costly because it invokes ubiquination pathways and protein replacement. It costs the cell TONS of energy thermodynamically. NO helps reduce glycation, which helps DHA and O2 tissue delivery immensely. But here is the quantum key to understanding oxidaive damage: nitrogen can only be reduced from +2 to 0 whereas sulfur reduces from +6 to -2. That is a lot more electron protection. This is a way higher therapeutic index. This is why we use sulfur during summer and not nitrogen to transport O2 and glucose. Nitrogen is only safe when we are ketotic and inflammation is low and the zeta potential of the plasma is high.
.

This is a decent amount of information, so I'll let it sink it before commenting further. Yup, that's a good description of Nitric Oxide

\---

Of course, "Nitrogen" means more than Nitric Oxide. Right now, I'm looking back at Nicotinamide ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicotinamide](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicotinamide)) and NAD+ ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicotinamide\_adenine\_dinucleotide](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide))

Right now the biologically relevant Nitrogen-containing configurations I'm looking at are:

**(1)** Nitric Oxide free radical (including all intermediates and derived compounds)

**(2)** Nitrogen in hexagonal rings (as seen in NAD+), which I think introduces the propensity for proton-coupled electron tunnelling -- [http://qhwiki.com/study:proton-coupled-electron-tunneling](http://qhwiki.com/study:proton-coupled-electron-tunneling)

**(3)** Amines ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amine](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amine)), because of the lone pair, making any such molecule polar, and exposing. Amine-containing molecules usually are chiral as well, which makes for functional differentiation using light-mediated mechanisms.

Example: L-cysteine to D-cysteine conversion via UV irradiation.
Hunch: D-cysteine back to L-cysteine would involve fluorescence of UV light, which must be stimulated by a vibrational force -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Franck–Condon\_principle](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Franck%E2%80%93Condon_principle) (see the symmetry between absorption and fluorescence spectra)

In my mind right now, Amides seem to be just structural support components that don't go through too much signalling. I'll hold this view for now, and hope to be proved wrong

Also, back to nitrogen fixation, and the Carbon/Nitrogen ratio (C/N ratio) in plants as described in the paper '**Carbon and nitrogen metabolism regulated by the ubiquitin-proteasome system**' -- [http://qhwiki.com/study:carbon-nitrogen-ubiquitin](http://qhwiki.com/study:carbon-nitrogen-ubiquitin)

From an energetic substrate point of view, "Carbon" is energy (carbs and fats), while "Nitrogen" is signalling and transform (proteins). There should be parallels of a plant's mechanism of controlling C/N ratio, and that found in humans. The Cysteine papers I posted above basically links that balance to protein turnover rates (ubiquination), but I'm not making the connections yet

....

### prAna303 — 2015-04-04T19:46:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquitination-8-is-live.13615/post-160313>

This blog kind of nails it, the feeling i have been having about a modern society today.

My country is very open to help refugees, people in deep need. Today i see a growing issue between us who has been here for many generations and the so called new swedes. I have meet a couple of them in many kind of ways, friends, people in need of help, list goes on. What always felt wrong is this...

Almost everyone is very fat, very thin but always kind of presenting a deep sadness, i always felt that light is their issue.

When they move here, from countries with tons of sun and dark and cold nights they never get tought to change their unit to match a world where summer is endless and winter is close to death. They take their customs with themselves, their religion, simply all things that should work back home and try to make it work here... Mismatch from hell.

This is why thinking and many times religion can do damage almost beyond repair. To have laws that is not based upon the very place and situation, always changing makes you take the wrong path. When the mismatch goes haywire, you get extreme ways of thinking and acting, energy down the drain makes you a perfect slave to words.

I always felt that we save many from wars but gives them hell beacuse we do not allow us to act in harmony with the nature you have. Coming from a country with fruits and greens to Sweden means you must look around and act upon that place, not your thoughts and ideas. With my friends they have taken foods and ideas with themselves to share with us who live here, we get global, we say... We can buy everything from the middle east here today, 24/7, 365 and all day and night.

Many times i said we must learn from nature and accept that we are different, not just because customs and ideas, we are of different kinds, sure we look pretty much the same but that does not mean we performe the same. It does not matter, my best friend and i must always look into my own reality, change when we feel off.

To be off, we should not by law become on.

Ever have a laptop with a worn-out battery? What happens? It works, but you need to keep it plugged in all the time, right? To overcome the shitty battery that can't hold much of a charge...

If you want to carry that laptop away from electricity (think sunlight) for longer periods, that battery needs to store a lot more charge to be functional, doesn't it?

So living away from the equator means a less steady power source and a greater need for a stronger battery (DHA) to compensate.

> Connects all back to my post earlier on... If you think you can use the same solution all over the world you are going to wars with only one winner, not i will it be.

Yep. And that's why equatorial life does relatively better on land than in the oceans, isn't it? Water blocks light. The local solar advantage doesn't really apply underwater, and the water is not cold or oxygenated enough to support a great battery...

And as you move from the equator, life tends to adapt from direct solar-driven to battery-driven. Which means it needs more water, DHA, and cold. So aquatic life begins to trump land-based until you reach the poles, where it is virtually all aquatic...

Over evolutionary time, the epigenetic variability to better adapt to such extremes was further selected for in life that was able to thrive in variable conditions. (Factor X) Mammals, especially, seem to have acquired the unique machinery to straddle the boundaries between living like a warm equatorial tree and a cold polar fish at different times. They needed to so that they could survive the seasonal variations which tend to recapitulate the geographic and historical ones. And humans, of course, have the most epigenetic adaptability of all.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-04-08T05:07:05+0300

Yew if you stay in AUS closer to the equator is a smart move. They key is water and DHA and IR and UV from sun........you can re create CT anywhere.​

I posted this in the Quantum Health Facebook Group ([https://www.facebook.com/groups/872...d=977551078924453&offset=0&total\_comments=100](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/976963748983186/?comment_id=977551078924453&offset=0&total_comments=100))

Restoring good mitochondrial health is based on a lot more than dietary templates. Circadian biology becomes much more important.

OPINION: What is the best way for a person to recover good mitochondrial health?

My answer is to make it as easy as possible to adhere to circadian rhythms, while doing as many measures as possible to improve signalling.

I am biased to believe that making a move to a location which has as little variation in seasonal light, magnetic, and water cycles is the ideal for recovery and maintenance of good health

(I am biased because I moved from 37deg south to 16deg south , and now enjoy 11hr days during "winter", and 13hr days during summer, with year-long 220+hrs of bright sunshine a month)

My reasoning was that reducing this variance in environmental-imposed demands for different signalling mechanisms gives one more margin for error when it comes to your own optimal health strategies.

At my current location, I can go keto and exercise more, or eat more carbs, or caloric restrict, while doing CT in a cold tub, and still fall within the range of circadian appropriate constraints.​

That said, I'd probably want to experiment with a Northern Hemisphere (>50deg north) winter one of these days

### yewwei.tan — 2015-04-09T12:58:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquitination-8-is-live.13615/post-160723>

Quick summary of quite the epic paper that @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) shared somewhere (I think it was on Facebook ).

'**Role of E2-Ub-conjugating enzymes during skeletal muscle atrophy**' by Cecile Polge, Didier Attaix and Daniel Taillandier -- [http://qhwiki.com/study:e2-ub-conjugating-enzymes-skeletal-muscle-atrophy](http://qhwiki.com/study:e2-ub-conjugating-enzymes-skeletal-muscle-atrophy)

The importance of protein shape is stressed very strongly in this paper (discussed in OSF3 to OSF5). Missing amino acids of changes in shape of the backside of E2s don't allow for ubiquination to take place (potential for decreased clean-up activity).

I still suspect that Nitrogen is relevant in so far that all nitrogen functional groups on amino acdis are polar as hell, and can be manipulated electromagnetically.

The whole E2 and E3 coupling also just looks to my as a double-confirm feedback mechanism, as if to ask "Are you sure?" before breaking down a protein. This coupling again introduces multiple dependencies for the protein turnover process to occur.

As the papre points out, almoast all studies today are in-vitro assays that do not even look at the E2-E3 interaction. Forget about any condiseration to protein folding and electromagnetic modification of protein structure, or electric resonance between enzyme active site and substrate.

It would be great if we could get time-axis analysis of E2-E3 activity and subsequent protein breakdown rates. Then we could expose tissue to nnEMF and have an actual quantitative measure of systemic ubiquination rates and the direct cost to the body. But too bad, we're not there yet , and likely won't be there for awhile. For now, we are stuck making logical speculations like what the authors of the paper do.

.....

### SCRN2007 — 2015-04-15T06:34:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquitination-8-is-live.13615/post-161235>

I am not the person who posted the above on Facebook, but I am very glad to find this information regarding PTC/IH. I am also glad that I refused a V-P shunt for my IH. I was called "a fearful, alarmist pediatric ICU nurse" for refusing, but consideration of the failure and revision rates (in adults) and visiting various Facebook support groups in which shunted adults were still needing Diamox, and still experiencing the same symptoms for which it was suggested I be shunted, is what made up my mind.

I recently found a post you wrote in the IPMG group where you told a mom of a girl with PTC to read Pollack's new book and stated, "it is all based upon the charge separation of water adjacent to collagen in the subarachnoid space." Interestingly, my subarachnoid space looks abnormal on MRI, with possible evidence of a past SA cyst. I was also diagnosed with Ehlers Danlos Type 3 (i.e., the only Ehlers Danlos type for which a specific collagen gene mutation has not been found, and for which even my conventional geneticist suspects there is an epigenetic etiology). I leak from the nose and ears when my ICP is high, which my geneticist said is the case with a few of her EDS patients who have IH . . . and I found a couple of articles published regarding collagen defects and CSF leaks, which suggest that I am not the only one who might have torn myself a pressure relief valve or two.

The incidence of IH in EDS is currently being blamed on cranio-cervical instability and faulty drainage by the only NS in the US who gives presentations on EDS; however, is it possible that faulty charge separation of water throughout the body and in the brain is undermining the integrity of collagen somehow, or that faulty collagen is preventing proper charge separation of water throughout the body and in the brain?

In addition to icing the carotids, is PTC a condition that could be helped by drinking cold RO water instead of cold spring water? I plan to read Pollack's new book, but will probably comprehend it a bit better if I can make some headway (pun intended) with this condition. Right now my cognition is at the mercy of the barometric pressure's stability--no fun.

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=160233)
>
> this came from FB IPMG group discussion I was tagged on for a nurse with pseudotumor yesterday:
>
> Pseudotumor usually affects the vision and neuropthamologists usually have to do optic nerve fenestrations. If the headaches are bad and she has low D3 levels her environment is toxic. If she is a nurse it is likely a huge factor especially if she is a night time shift worker. She needs to avoid this ASAP. Is the dermoid related, yes. Why? Complicated but here it goes.........Dermoid cyst of the ovary are cystic teratoma's. A teratoma is a benign tumor that is associated with altered cAMP signaling. cAMP is the second messenger used in hormone signaling but it is also actively involved in ultravioltet light signaling in the skin, bone, brain, and in nucleic acids. If you read my quantum Vitamin D3 blog, you would have seen how the dermoid and brain are connected are via light. The skin and brain both come from the same material in an embryo. This tissue is called neuroectoderm. At one time in all life the skin and brain were connected. They remain connected by subatomically by free radical chemistry of entangled electrons. In the adult these tissues are connected as they once were. But the altered light environment can stimulate both previously connected tissues to demonstrate altered growth if the altered light stimulus is present in one organ. In your sister it appear it is her brain via her retina. This happens via melanopsin receptors. Here is another twist most do not know. In the embryo, neuroectoderm receives a signal from bone morphogenetic protein. This is an inhibiting signals from a protein called noggin. Noggin leads to the development of the nervous system from this tissue. Here we see bone tissue can directs brain formation. This protein also affects growth of things from skin remnants. A dermoid is a skin remnant. This also means your sister likely has some degree of osteopenia. You can check that with a tibial compression test using a butter knife. I talk about this in my current blog, ubiquitination 8. Everything is connected in a quantum ecosystem. This is the key principle behind coherence in the living matrix of all biology is tied to the free radical chemistry they both share. Are their more connections you should be aware of with the skin? Yes, this is big for your sister......... The integumentary system covers the surface of the embryo and its specialized skin structures including hair, nails, sweat glands, mammary glands and teeth. As a system it has contributions from all embryonic layers. These are the things a dermoid teratoma is made up from. Most neurosurgeons forget these details. This means that every system at some level is tied to specific light frequencies that sulfate Vitamin D3 in the skin. Your sister's lifestyle and location on the planet do not allow her to do so. This is what is driving the dermoid growth and her brain expansion, because both tissues are losing energy to their environment and this causes their growth. Moreover, it is why the sulfation of Vitamin D3 by the sun is important to understand.I covered that in Tensegrity 7 blog post. She likely has a very altered D3 and vitamin A cycle because of a chronic light frequency mismatch in both tissue linked to her neuroectoderm. This drives altered cAMP to cause both to lose the energy. What helps both of these conditions? DHA and cold thermogenesis? Why? The DHA reason is in the current blog.......but the CT reason is due to AMPK pathways signaling. It controls mTOR and IGF1 and cold is the only direct way of stopping the altered cAMP signaling when you have a light mismatch. I covered that late in the ubiquination 7 blog. Dermoids contains an array of developmentally mature, solid tissues like skin, with hair follicles, and sweat glands, clumps of long hair, pockets of sebum, blood, fat, bone, nails, teeth, eyes, cartilage, and thyroid tissue. You need to get your sister tested for osteopenia too.[http://www.jackkruse.com/quantum-biology-7-vitamin-d/](http://www.jackkruse.com/quantum-biology-7-vitamin-d/)

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-04-06T21:13:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-and-grounding-good-insurance-or-grey-area.13671/post-160439>

My wife has M.S. and she swears that grounding helps minimize her symptoms, especially the 'burning' in her hands, but she also thinks that it completely shields her from EMF. I'm not so sure about that.

I know it goes a long way to help minimize the effects of EMF but I don't think it would completely eliminate it. This is just my intuition based on my electrical education.

There is a big difference between being grounded with a grounding pad and being grounded in the Pacific Ocean!

Anybody have any thoughts on this subject? I'm sure Jack has written about it somewhere but asking is much faster. (lol!)

# Thread: strong iodine solution 12.64% topical for Hashi's
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strong-iodine-solution-12-64-topical-for-hashis.13697/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope2Learn — 2015-04-08T00:19:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strong-iodine-solution-12-64-topical-for-hashis.13697/post-160588>

I picked this up today as recommended and prescribed by my bhrt doc and my compounding pharmacist. I'm supposed to apply one drop daily to my thyroid area for ten days, then switch to other sites thereafter once daily.

I opened it up, took the dropper bottle out of the larger, standard plastic childproof prescription bottle, and went to carry it into the bathroom to look in the mirror and apply a drop to my neck, when I smelled something. Looked down at my hand and the stuff was all over my hand and the label even though I didn't open the dropper bottle itself.

I washed it off with soap and water quickly but am not going to put a drop on my neck today now because of this.

My questions are - where on my neck to I apply the one drop daily, exactly? Does it matter?

Why do I switch to other areas after ten days, anyone know?

But most importantly - how big of a deal is it that i'm supposed to apply one drop and one drop ONLY to my skin each day and I just somehow ended up with many, many drops on me?

(bonus question - I use dorzolamide \[carbonic anhydrase inhibitor\] for glaucoma - this won't have any interactions, will it? The biggest potential side effect of the dorzolamide is kidney stones. There is also a caution about taking it with any other drug that might increase the potassium levels in your blood for any reason... would iodine do that? If so I mustn't use it - but obviously have already just had a large exposure \*eek\* - thanks!)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-08T01:42:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strong-iodine-solution-12-64-topical-for-hashis.13697/post-160590>

[http://www.jackkruse.com/hashimotos-and-melasma/](http://www.jackkruse.com/hashimotos-and-melasma/)

**Think this:** It’s not always about trying to fix something that is broken…..sometimes, it is about starting over and creating something better. That is why failure is so good for those who are ill. Few of them realize the lessons it is bringing to them because their brain is not working well……..failure moves off your current beliefs to see a new path you might have missed. This is where the road to healing lies, in most people’s blind spot.

**Just because you understand the picture I am painting, does not mean you will gain a reversal either. Why? Even if you’re on the right road to health with new wisdom, you’ll get run over if you just sit there. You must realize these gateway diseases are tied to what “you allow” to occur in your environment.**

[Iodine](http://www.ameerrosic.com/why-i-gained-30-pounds-in-90-days-thyroid-problems-in-women/) is critical for oxidation protection of [PUFA](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PUFA)’s utilized by the [brain and thyroid](http://www.ameerrosic.com/why-i-gained-30-pounds-in-90-days-thyroid-problems-in-women/), such as [DHA](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Docosahexaenoic_acid). It also appears that iodine is a lot better protector of all [lipids](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lipids) in all cells and not just the brain. With [iodine deficiency,](http://www.ameerrosic.com/why-i-gained-30-pounds-in-90-days-thyroid-problems-in-women/) the body loses the ability to handle [ROS](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reactive_oxygen_species) optimally, and other organ systems have to offset those losses to protect the cell from more oxidation. Supplementation is not the answer.

# Thread: Over did Lugols Iodine

### Danny — 2015-04-08T01:45:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/over-did-lugols-iodine.13698/post-160592>

I was taking 3 to 5 drops of lugol's 2 % for about 1.5 months. Then I sloppily increased it to about 12 to 15 drops a day for about 2 weeks. I started getting symptoms like low blood pressure, a little acne on my face, slight frontal headaches, anxiety, poor sleep. I stopped taking it 5 weeks ago. My doctor told me it will just have to come out of my system. The symptoms do seem to be getting better, but it seems to be dragging on. Does anyone know of anyways to hasten this? I've just read about salt flushing or loading and am experimenting with this. It seems to help. By the way, before this I was doing really well following Kruse's Epi-paleo and leptin Rx. I've got an appointment w/ another doctor in 2 days. Thanks!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-08T01:50:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/over-did-lugols-iodine.13698/post-160593>

### Arborescence — 2015-04-12T15:09:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/over-did-lugols-iodine.13698/post-160966>

That is quite interesting as I have started Lugol's a few days ago. Where you taking the co-supplements?

On the CureZone forum, they are quite a lot into iodine (and iodide) supplementation and here is what they say about natural sources: [http://www.curezone.org/forums/am.asp?i=1803456](http://www.curezone.org/forums/am.asp?i=1803456)
[https://docs.google.com/document/d/16Dpy1\_mGUy3dOxSpr7QY4oeiDVvS0jH9bkeQK\_KzvG4/edit#](https://docs.google.com/document/d/16Dpy1_mGUy3dOxSpr7QY4oeiDVvS0jH9bkeQK_KzvG4/edit#)
[https://docs.google.com/document/d/139Xa8u8kgNVT-K17TxYvsM7qsSfusxyvloIXWbU92rg/edit#](https://docs.google.com/document/d/139Xa8u8kgNVT-K17TxYvsM7qsSfusxyvloIXWbU92rg/edit#)
[https://docs.google.com/document/d/1t\_6oprpjmDCMqwCXP\_6BMf0Mbr9DivmOSALeJTjReuY/edit#](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1t_6oprpjmDCMqwCXP_6BMf0Mbr9DivmOSALeJTjReuY/edit#)

**Seafoods are not recommended**
Reasons why Seafood is not recommended

\- - - Seafood contaminated with radiation Pacific Ocean from Japan

[http://www.greenpeace.org/internati...esearch-reveals-serious-marine-contamination/](http://www.greenpeace.org/international/en/press/releases/Greenpeace-Fukushima-radiation-research-reveals-serious-marine-contamination/)

\- - - Seafish contaminated with Mercury

[http://www.fda.gov/newsevents/newsroom/pressannouncements/2006/ucm108664.htm](http://www.fda.gov/newsevents/newsroom/pressannouncements/2006/ucm108664.htm)

\- - - Seafish contaminated with Arsenic

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/828352](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/828352)

\- - - Kelp contaminated with Arsenic & Mercury. Contamination with heavy metals can be a serious problem with sea vegetation. Recently, 8 of 9 herbal kelp supplements from a health food store were found to have excessive levels of arsenic in them [(74)](http://www.hacres.com/pdf/documents/research-Advances_in_Iodine_Nutrition.pdf) . For this reason, and because only iodide is present, this is not a preferred source of this iodine.

[http://www.livestrong.com/article/414053-what-side-effects-does-ingesting-too-much-kelp-produce/](http://www.livestrong.com/article/414053-what-side-effects-does-ingesting-too-much-kelp-produce/)

\- - - Seaweed is no longer a healthy food

[http://breastcancerchoices.org/seaweed](http://breastcancerchoices.org/seaweed)

-   Lugols & Iodoral Iodine is pure and has the correct ratio of iodine & iodide

Another good article that might make us reconsider eating food from the sea: [http://www.tuberose.com/Japan'sFukushimaCatastrophe.html](http://www.tuberose.com/Japan%27sFukushimaCatastrophe.html)

I would be really interested to know what Dr. Kruse thinks about all this?

No coincidence that AIDS became an "epidemic" at the dawn of the digital/nnEMF age, this seems to be the next evolution. ### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-11T01:10:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/members-april-2015-webinar-drops-today.13745/post-160865>

**CAN MUSIC HEAL US?**
That being said, can sound frequencies also affect us biologically? They sure can. The mixing of frequencies can help or hurt our cells depending upon the environment that they are forced to exist in. What determines that fate, is the atomic make up of cells, the electrodynamic field the cell senses, and the orientation and geometry of things suspended and connected to the cytoskeleton of the cell. Sound energy can recharge our batteries’ the DC electric charge in us. We can see this current in an ECG, EEG, MEG data from our brain. Ultrasound was found in Dr. Gerald Pollack’s work to expanded the exclusion zone in water by its presence or absence. This is why music can help or hurt us. The frequency of music plays a role in the ability to regenerate by altering the EZ size and eventually affecting the DC current strength of the mammalian battery. Anything that increases the EZ in water has the ability to increase the DC current in cells. Why do I bring up this linkage of sound frequency to the DC current? Our brain is designed to sense all types of wave forms from our environment. Our brain summates, subtracts, and reconstructs the three dimensional waveforms and their frequencies to simulate the geometry of life. The geometry of life is captured in the [3 dimension fractals found within a cell.](http://motherboard.vice.com/read/what-fractals-can-teach-us-about-cancer) _Geometry links thinks to quantum mechanics._
_
[http://www.jackkruse.com/ubiquitination-9-frequency-awakening/](http://www.jackkruse.com/ubiquitination-9-frequency-awakening/)

Ubi 9 is now live.........enjoy your weekend........cause I will! French Quarter fest!!!_

Sidenote: I love the titles of these old texts . 傷寒雜病論 (Shānghán zábìng lùn) literally meaning "Hurt (傷) Cold (寒) complex/miscellaneous (雜) illness (病) discussion (論)". So "A Discussion on Complex Diseases caused by Cold Damage"

### Josh — 2015-04-11T01:45:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/members-april-2015-webinar-drops-today.13745/post-160869>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=160867)
>
> Hmmm, worth picking these up?
>
> Sidenote: I love the titles of these old texts . 傷寒雜病論 (Shānghán zábìng lùn) literally meaning "Hurt (傷) Cold (寒) complex/miscellaneous (雜) illness (病) discussion (論)". So "A Discussion on Complex Diseases caused by Cold Damage"
>
> ....

See what is available on line. The classics are treasures. Just the "The Difficult Classic" gave birth to 30+ schools when it came to Japan in 1611 or so. These systems are examples of practitioners who thought of stages/levels of disease as they progress. They are more holistic than modern allopathic models. They are much more logical approaches to COE's. My point was that in these models one sees evidence of the progressive loss of redox/qi>blood>body fluids>jing as the body becomes increasingly challenged. A portion of the symptomology may be due to the viral shedding and such....I have looked for explanations for these things since I was first exposed to these models and others 20 years ago. There are many discussions on the challenges of treating disease that affects more than one level at a time which is what happens when the environment and the redox change quickly for the worse=now....As I recall these models and others like them often appeared at times when intelligent practitioners recognized an acceleration of change and sought an explanation and a new model to work with that addressed it more effectively. There are many treasures in teh history of traditional medical systems. Getting good translations or any translations at all is another matter. Consider that most were written in Classical Chinese which is not even a spoken language. There are few translators with good Classical Chinese and multi-system medical understanding coupled with good English to do justice to the texts that may have survived. One scholar I talked with 10-15 years ago estimated that at that time less than 2% of the Oriental medical literature was translated. It is interesting to study for a different way of thinking about things more rooted in the actual environment that the body faces than the current molecular models. The quantum bridges it all, but it is just emerging here. Perhaps we shall see that the Russians have gone much further. They literally sit astride both worlds and have been traversing them for centuries. In a traditional medical family in China, the child begins training more or less when they can stand and completion is somewhere toward mid-life. I do not know how many family systems have survived. Some of my teachers had been trained this way. I referred to Yi Tian-Ni somewhere who was 48th generation in her family. She was a great translator and had trained in allopathy as well. Unfortunately, she died in a car crash.

Ubiquitination #9 excerpts and notes -- [http://qhwiki.com/excerpts:jk-ubiquitination-9](http://qhwiki.com/excerpts:jk-ubiquitination-9)

Also added this part about Cyclotron resonance frequency. Credit to 'Cross Currents' by Robert Becker for explaining this is good detail:

The cyclotron resonance of Ca2+ is particularly important. By this phenomena, external electromagnetic fields of Ca2+'s cyclotron resonance frequency, in the context of the Earth's static magnetic field, can **concentrate energy of the applied field** upon Ca2+ ions, and excite these ions.

Excitation can cause Ca2+ ions to move out of a cell (efflux). This was covered in Energy and Epigenetics #3 ([http://www.jackkruse.com/energy-and-epigenetics-3-autoimmunity-cancer-autism/](http://www.jackkruse.com/energy-and-epigenetics-3-autoimmunity-cancer-autism/))

The cyclotron resonance frequency of Ca2+ depends on the magnetic field strength of the Earth, but generally falls within the 20-50Hz band. This is exactly the frequency emitted by many electronics, and high-voltage power lines.​

### PaulG — 2015-04-11T05:48:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/members-april-2015-webinar-drops-today.13745/post-160880>

Talking of senses I just found this [list of modalities](http://www.biosensor-physik.de/biosensor/sensor.htm), Any ones we should be learning about??

> "It has long been obvious that sensory modalities go well beyond the classic five human senses of hearing, sight, taste, smell, and touch. Living things not only sense sound, light, chemicals, and pressure, but also position, heat, gravity, acceleration, electrical and magnetic fields, and even the passage of time. A glance at a list of some of the better-studied of these sensory systems (Table 1) may lead to the impression that life has evolved to monitor just about everything. Can there be any unifying themes, any biophysical principles?
>
> A physicist looking over Table I might point out that living things sense manifestations of just two of the four fundamental forces: the electromagnetic force and the gravitational force. The electromagnetic force has infinite range, and it dominates on the length scale at which life exists: from nanometers up to tens of meters. It holds molecules together, and is the basis for light, heat, sound, and all of chemistry. The gravitational force also has infinite range, and, although its effects are far weaker, it, too, affects living forms. But the remaining two forces, the strong force (or nuclear force) and the weak force (responsible, for example, for ß-decay), appear to pass undetected through the biosphere. It is likely that, because the range of both the weak and strong forces is finite and short (small, even on the scale of an atom), large-scale consequences are wholly insignificant. Perhaps so. But who knows? Someone may yet find an example of a creature that detects nuclear processes or violates parity conservation."

vertebrate rod and cone vision
vertebrate taste transduction
chemotaxis in bacteria

echolocation in bats, birds
